mern.title: Web Fundamentals
mern.description: The foundation of everything web. Learn how browsers render pages, how to structure content with HTML, style it with CSS, and add interactivity with JavaScript.
mern.challenge: Build a fully responsive personal portfolio page using only HTML & CSS.

sideLeft.title: How browsers parse and render HTML/CSS

children.title: The critical rendering path and render-blocking resources
children.description: Browsers parse HTML into a DOM, CSS into a CSSOM, then combine them into a render tree before painting. Understanding this tells you exactly why CSS in <head> and JS at end of body matters.

children.title: Semantic HTML and the accessibility tree
children.description: Semantic elements like <nav>, <main>, and <article> create an accessibility tree that screen readers traverse. Choosing the right element is never just aesthetic — it determines keyboard navigability and ARIA roles.

sideLeft.title: CSS layout models: flow, flexbox, and grid

children.title: The box model: content, padding, border, and margin
children.description: Every element is a rectangular box. Knowing how box-sizing: border-box changes width calculations prevents the most common CSS layout bugs.

children.title: When to use flexbox versus CSS grid
children.description: Flexbox is one-dimensional (a row or column); grid is two-dimensional (rows and columns simultaneously). Knowing which to reach for prevents fighting the layout algorithm.

sideRight.title: Responsive design with media queries and units

children.title: Mobile-first breakpoints with min-width media queries
children.description: Write base styles for small screens, then add breakpoints upward. This produces leaner CSS than desktop-first overrides.

children.title: Browser DevTools: inspecting layout, styles, and the network tab
children.description: DevTools lets you live-edit CSS, inspect box models, and see every network request. It's the fastest feedback loop you have while building.

children.title: Selecting elements and modifying the DOM at runtime
children.description: querySelector, textContent, classList, and setAttribute let you read and rewrite the page from JavaScript. These are the primitives React abstracts over.

children.title: Event bubbling, delegation, and removeEventListener
children.description: Events bubble up the DOM tree. Delegating a single listener on a parent instead of many on children is more performant and avoids memory leaks.

mern.title: JavaScript Deep Dive
mern.description: Go beyond the basics. Master modern JavaScript features, asynchronous patterns, and how to communicate with external APIs.
topics: ES6+ — destructuring, spread, arrow functions, modules
mern.challenge: Build a weather app that fetches live data from a public API and handles loading/error states.

sideLeft.title: The JavaScript event loop and async execution model

children.title: Call stack, task queue, and microtask queue ordering
children.description: The event loop processes the call stack first, then microtasks (Promise callbacks), then the macrotask queue (setTimeout). Knowing this order explains why Promise.then always runs before setTimeout(fn, 0).

children.title: How closures capture free variables in the scope chain
children.description: A closure is a function that retains references to its outer lexical scope even after that scope has returned. They enable module patterns, memoization, and React hooks' dependency semantics.

children.title: Promise chaining versus async/await for sequential calls
children.description: async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises; each await suspends the function and resumes it in a microtask. Understanding this prevents bugs when mixing callbacks and Promises.

children.title: Promise.all, Promise.allSettled, and race conditions
children.description: Promise.all rejects immediately if any promise rejects; Promise.allSettled waits for all. Choosing the wrong combinator is a common source of dropped errors in parallel API calls.

sideRight.title: Modern ES6+ syntax patterns used in MERN codebases

children.title: Destructuring, spread, and rest in function signatures
children.description: Destructuring unpacks arrays/objects inline; spread copies or merges them. Both appear constantly in React props, Redux reducers, and API response handling.

children.title: Array methods: map, filter, reduce, and their composition
children.description: These three methods replace most imperative loops. Composing them — filter then map — is how you transform API response arrays into what the UI needs.

sideRight.title: Fetch API and error handling patterns

children.title: fetch, Response.ok, and typed error handling
children.description: fetch only rejects on network failure, not on 4xx/5xx responses. You must check response.ok and throw manually — a mistake that causes silent failures.

children.title: ES modules: import/export and the difference from CommonJS
children.description: ESM is static (analyzed at parse time); CommonJS is dynamic (require runs at runtime). MERN projects mix both, and understanding the boundary prevents bundler errors.

mern.title: React Fundamentals
mern.description: Learn how to think in components. Build dynamic UIs with React, understand the component lifecycle, and manage local state.
mern.challenge: Build a task manager app with add, delete, and filter functionality — no backend yet.

sideLeft.title: React's component model and virtual DOM reconciliation

children.title: How React diffs the virtual DOM and batches DOM updates
children.description: React creates a virtual DOM tree and compares it to the previous one on each render, applying only the minimal real DOM changes. This diffing algorithm (reconciliation) is why React is fast with many small updates.

children.title: Unidirectional data flow: props down, events up
children.description: In React, data flows from parent to child via props, and children communicate back via callback props. Violating this pattern leads to the tangled state that Context and state managers solve.

children.title: Why state updates are asynchronous and batched
children.description: Calling setState doesn't immediately mutate state; React queues the update and re-renders. Relying on stale state in the same handler is the #1 React beginner bug.

children.title: useEffect dependency array and cleanup functions
children.description: The dependency array controls when the effect re-runs; an empty array means run once on mount. Omitting the cleanup return causes memory leaks from subscriptions and timers.

sideRight.title: JSX syntax and component composition patterns

children.title: JSX compiles to React.createElement calls
children.description: JSX is syntax sugar; <Button color='blue'>Click</Button> becomes React.createElement(Button, {color:'blue'}, 'Click'). Understanding this demystifies JSX restrictions like single root elements.

resources.label: React Docs — Writing Markup with JSX

children.title: Rendering lists with key props and conditional rendering
children.description: Keys help React identify which list items changed, added, or removed. Using array index as key breaks reconciliation when items reorder — use stable unique IDs instead.

sideRight.title: Controlled forms and event handling in React

children.title: Controlled inputs: value prop + onChange handler
children.description: A controlled input's value is driven by React state, not the DOM. This makes validation and programmatic clearing trivial but requires a handler for every keystroke.

resources.label: React Docs — Reacting to Input with State

children.title: Synthetic events and event pooling in React
children.description: React wraps native events in SyntheticEvent for cross-browser compatibility. Accessing event properties asynchronously requires event.persist() or extracting values before the async call.

mern.title: React Advanced
mern.description: Level up your React skills with global state management, client-side routing, and reusable hook patterns.
topics: React Router v6 — nested routes, params
mern.challenge: Extend your task manager with multi-page routing, a global theme toggle, and persisted state.

sideLeft.title: Context API internals and when it causes re-renders

children.title: How Context propagates values and triggers consumer re-renders
children.description: Every component that calls useContext re-renders when the context value changes — even if it only uses part of the value. Splitting contexts by update frequency is how you avoid this.

children.title: useReducer for state with multiple related sub-values
children.description: useReducer is preferable to useState when next state depends on complex logic across many fields. It also makes state transitions testable in pure functions.

children.title: Nested routes and the Outlet component for shared layouts
children.description: In RRv6, nested route definitions render into the parent's <Outlet>. This replaces the old switch-based approach and makes layout composition declarative.

children.title: useMemo and useCallback: when memoization helps and when it hurts
children.description: Memoization prevents unnecessary recalculations and child re-renders but adds overhead from the comparison itself. Only apply it when a profiler shows a real performance problem.

sideRight.title: Custom hooks for reusable stateful logic

children.title: Extracting stateful logic into custom hooks
children.description: A custom hook is just a function whose name starts with 'use' and that calls other hooks. It lets you share logic like useFetch or useForm across components without prop drilling.

children.title: Code splitting with React.lazy and Suspense
children.description: React.lazy dynamically imports a component, and Suspense shows a fallback while it loads. This keeps the initial bundle small by deferring routes or heavy components.

children.title: useNavigate, useParams, and useSearchParams
children.description: useNavigate replaces the history object for programmatic navigation. useParams reads dynamic segments and useSearchParams reads query strings — both typed with TypeScript generics.

children.title: Protected routes using wrapper components and redirect
children.description: Wrapping routes in an auth-checking component that redirects unauthenticated users is the standard pattern. Combine with useContext auth state for a clean implementation.

mern.title: Node.js & Express
mern.description: Move to the server. Build REST APIs with Node.js and Express, understand middleware, and handle routing and error management.
mern.challenge: Build a REST API for a blog — CRUD endpoints for posts, with input validation and error handling.

sideLeft.title: The Node.js runtime: event loop and the module system

children.title: How Node.js handles I/O without blocking using libuv
children.description: Node delegates file I/O and network calls to libuv's thread pool; the event loop picks up callbacks when they complete. This is why a single-threaded Node server can handle thousands of concurrent connections.

children.title: CommonJS require versus ES module import in Node
children.description: CommonJS loads synchronously; ESM is asynchronous and statically analyzable. Node supports both, but mixing them requires understanding .mjs/.cjs extensions and the 'type' package.json field.

children.title: How Express routes and middleware compose with next()
children.description: Every Express handler receives (req, res, next). Calling next() passes control to the next middleware; calling next(err) skips to error middleware. The order of app.use() calls is the execution order.

children.title: RESTful API design: resources, verbs, and status codes
children.description: REST maps HTTP verbs to CRUD: GET/read, POST/create, PUT-PATCH/update, DELETE/delete. Using the correct status codes (201, 204, 404, 422) makes your API self-documenting.

sideRight.title: Setting up and structuring an Express application

children.title: Router modules for separating concerns by resource
children.description: express.Router() creates a mini-app with its own middleware stack. Mounting separate routers for /users, /posts, and /auth keeps each file focused on one resource.

children.title: Environment variables with dotenv and the config pattern
children.description: Store secrets (DB URLs, JWT secrets) in .env files loaded by dotenv. Never commit .env — add it to .gitignore and document required variables in a .env.example.

sideRight.title: Request validation and centralized error handling

children.title: Input validation with express-validator or Joi
children.description: Never trust req.body. Validate shape, type, and constraints at the route level before hitting business logic or the database. express-validator chains directly on route definitions.

children.title: Centralized error middleware with four-argument signature
children.description: Express error middleware has the signature (err, req, res, next). Register it last with app.use() and throw or call next(err) from anywhere in the chain to reach it.

mern.title: MongoDB & Mongoose
mern.description: Persist your data. Learn NoSQL database design, connect MongoDB to your Express app, and model data with Mongoose schemas.
topics: NoSQL vs SQL — when to use MongoDB
CRUD operations with the MongoDB driver

resources.label: Web Dev Simplified — MongoDB Crash Course

mern.challenge: Add a MongoDB database to your blog API — store posts and authors with a proper schema and relationships.

sideLeft.title: NoSQL document model versus relational tables

children.title: Embedding versus referencing documents in MongoDB
children.description: Embed related data (comments inside posts) when it's always read together; reference with ObjectId when the related data is large or shared. Choosing wrong is expensive to fix.

children.title: MongoDB BSON types and the ObjectId structure
children.description: MongoDB stores documents as BSON (binary JSON), which includes types like ObjectId, Date, and Int64 that JSON lacks. ObjectId encodes a timestamp, making it sortable by insertion order.

sideLeft.title: Mongoose schema validation and middleware hooks

children.title: Schema types, validators, and default values in Mongoose
children.description: Mongoose schemas define the shape of documents with type constraints, required fields, and defaults. This validation runs before save, giving you a data integrity layer above the raw driver.

children.title: Mongoose pre/post middleware for cross-cutting logic
children.description: pre('save') and post('find') hooks run before/after specific operations. Use them for hashing passwords, audit logging, or populating virtuals — logic that should always run regardless of caller.

sideRight.title: MongoDB Atlas setup and connection management

children.title: Connecting with mongoose.connect and handling connection events
children.description: Pass the Atlas connection string to mongoose.connect() and listen for 'connected' and 'error' events. In production, use connection pooling settings and retry logic.

children.title: CRUD operations with Mongoose model methods
children.description: Model.create(), .find(), .findById(), .findByIdAndUpdate(), .deleteOne() are the primary query methods. Always await them and handle the null case for find-by-ID lookups.

children.title: MongoDB query operators: $eq, $in, $gte, $regex
children.description: MongoDB's query language uses operator objects inside filter documents. $in for array membership, $gte/$lte for ranges, and $regex for text search are the most commonly needed.

children.title: Creating indexes to avoid full collection scans
children.description: Without an index, MongoDB scans every document. Add schema.index({ field: 1 }) in Mongoose or db.collection.createIndex() in Atlas for any field you filter or sort by frequently.

mern.title: Authentication & Security
mern.description: Secure your app. Implement user registration and login, hash passwords, issue JWTs, and protect routes.
mern.challenge: Add full auth to your blog API — register, login, JWT issuance, and protected post creation routes.

children.title: Why bcrypt uses work factor and salting to resist brute force
children.description: bcrypt's adaptive cost factor makes hashing intentionally slow. Each password gets a random salt before hashing, so identical passwords produce different hashes and rainbow tables are useless.

children.title: JWT structure: header, payload, and signature verification
children.description: A JWT is three Base64URL-encoded JSON objects separated by dots. The signature is computed from header+payload using a secret; the server verifies by re-computing it. Anyone can decode the payload — never put sensitive data in it.

sideLeft.title: HTTP-only cookies versus localStorage for token storage

children.title: Why XSS can steal localStorage tokens but not HTTP-only cookies
children.description: JavaScript cannot read HTTP-only cookies, so an XSS attack that injects a script can steal localStorage tokens but not cookie-stored ones. HTTP-only cookies are the safer choice for auth tokens.

children.title: CSRF attacks and SameSite cookie attribute as defense
children.description: CSRF tricks a logged-in browser into making requests to your API. SameSite=Strict prevents cookies from being sent on cross-origin requests, neutralizing most CSRF attacks.

children.title: Signing and verifying JWTs with jsonwebtoken
children.description: jwt.sign({userId}, secret, {expiresIn}) creates a token; jwt.verify(token, secret) validates it and returns the payload. Always set expiresIn and implement refresh token rotation.

children.title: Auth middleware: extracting and verifying the Bearer token
children.description: Read the Authorization header, strip 'Bearer ', verify the JWT, attach the decoded user to req.user, then call next(). Attach this middleware only to routes that need protection.

children.title: Configuring cors middleware with an origin allowlist
children.description: The cors package sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers. Restrict the origin option to your front-end URL in production — never use '*' when credentials are involved.

children.title: OWASP Top 10 essentials: injection, broken auth, and IDOR
children.description: The OWASP Top 10 catalogs the most critical web app vulnerabilities. Injection (SQL/NoSQL), broken authentication, and insecure direct object references are the three most likely to affect a MERN app.

mern.title: Full Stack Integration
mern.description: Connect your React frontend to your Express backend. Handle API calls, loading states, caching, and global state across the full stack.
topics: Handling auth tokens on the client
Proxy config for local dev (Vite / CRA)
mern.challenge: Build a full MERN blog — React frontend that talks to your Express/MongoDB backend with auth, protected routes, and optimistic UI.

sideLeft.title: Server state versus client state: why the distinction matters

children.title: Why server state is async, shared, and can go stale
children.description: Server state (API data) is owned by the server; your local copy can be outdated the moment you fetch it. Libraries like React Query model this explicitly with cache, stale time, and background refetch.

children.title: React Query cache: staleTime, cacheTime, and invalidation
children.description: staleTime controls how long data is considered fresh; cacheTime controls how long it's kept in memory after unmounting. queryClient.invalidateQueries() marks data stale and triggers a background refetch.

sideLeft.title: Zustand's subscription model versus Context re-renders

children.title: How Zustand avoids the Context re-render problem with selectors
children.description: Zustand components only re-render when the specific slice they select changes, unlike Context where any value change re-renders all consumers. Use selector functions: useStore(s => s.count).

sideRight.title: Axios setup and interceptors for auth headers

children.title: Creating an Axios instance with baseURL and interceptors
children.description: Create a single Axios instance with your API's base URL. Add a request interceptor to inject the Authorization header and a response interceptor to handle 401s and token refresh.

children.title: useMutation and optimistic updates in React Query
children.description: useMutation gives you mutateAsync, isPending, and onMutate for optimistic updates. In onMutate, cancel in-flight queries, snapshot old data, and apply the optimistic update immediately.

sideRight.title: Connecting React to Express in development and production

children.title: Vite proxy configuration for local development
children.description: Set server.proxy in vite.config.ts to forward /api requests to your Express server. This avoids CORS issues in development and mirrors how reverse proxies work in production.

children.title: Handling auth tokens: storing and refreshing on the client
children.description: Store access tokens in memory (a module-level variable or Zustand), not localStorage. Store the refresh token in an HTTP-only cookie and hit a /refresh endpoint before expiry.

mern.title: Testing
mern.description: Write tests that give you confidence. Cover your React components, API endpoints, and async logic.
topics: React Testing Library — render, queries, events
mern.challenge: Write unit + integration tests for your blog app — cover components, API routes, and auth flow.

sideLeft.title: Testing philosophy: unit, integration, and end-to-end

children.title: The testing pyramid and where to invest testing effort
children.description: The testing pyramid recommends many unit tests, fewer integration tests, and few E2E tests. Unit tests are fastest and cheapest; E2E tests are slow but catch real user-facing bugs.

children.title: What to mock and what not to mock in unit tests
children.description: Mock external dependencies (HTTP calls, databases) in unit tests; test them for real in integration tests. Over-mocking makes tests pass even when integration is broken.

children.title: Query hierarchy: getByRole first, test IDs as last resort
children.description: RTL's query priority mirrors how users find UI: by role, label, placeholder, text, then test ID. Using getByRole catches accessibility regressions automatically.

sideRight.title: Writing Jest unit and integration tests

children.title: describe/it blocks, beforeEach setup, and assertion matchers
children.description: Jest's describe groups related tests; beforeEach runs setup before each. Matchers like toEqual, toHaveBeenCalledWith, and toThrow cover most assertion needs.

children.title: Supertest for testing Express endpoints without a running server
children.description: Supertest wraps your Express app and makes real HTTP requests in-process. Use it to test route handlers, middleware, and response shapes without starting a server.

children.title: Testing async components with waitFor and findBy queries
children.description: findBy* queries return a promise that retries until the element appears. waitFor lets you assert on async state changes. Never use arbitrary timeouts — use these utilities instead.

children.title: Running jest with --coverage and integrating into GitHub Actions
children.description: jest --coverage generates a coverage report. Add a GitHub Actions workflow step to run tests on every push and fail the PR if coverage drops or tests fail.

mern.title: Deployment & DevOps
mern.description: Ship your app to the world. Deploy your frontend and backend, automate with CI/CD, and containerize with Docker.
topics: Deploying Express API to Railway / Render
GitHub Actions — CI pipeline on push

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana — Docker for Beginners

mern.challenge: Deploy your full MERN blog app — frontend on Vercel, API on Railway, MongoDB on Atlas, with a GitHub Actions CI pipeline.

sideLeft.title: Docker containers and the image layer model

children.title: How Docker images are built from layered filesystem snapshots
children.description: Each Dockerfile instruction adds a read-only layer on top of the previous one. Layers are cached; changing a lower instruction invalidates all layers above it. Order instructions to maximize cache hits.

resources.label: Docker Docs — Best Practices for Dockerfiles

children.title: Multi-stage builds for lean production images
children.description: A multi-stage Dockerfile uses one stage to build (with dev dependencies) and a second stage to run (copying only the compiled output). This can reduce image size from 1GB to under 100MB.

children.title: Continuous integration versus continuous deployment distinctions
children.description: CI automatically runs tests on every commit to ensure the branch is always green. CD goes further and automatically deploys passing builds. GitHub Actions can handle both in a single workflow file.

resources.label: GitHub Actions Docs — Understanding GitHub Actions

sideRight.title: Deploying MERN apps to cloud platforms

children.title: Vercel deployment: project settings, env vars, and preview URLs
children.description: Connect your GitHub repo to Vercel; it deploys on every push and creates preview URLs for PRs. Add environment variables in the Vercel dashboard — never in the repo.

children.title: Railway for deploying Express APIs with managed env vars
children.description: Railway auto-detects Node apps, injects a PORT env variable, and provisions databases in the same project. Connect MongoDB Atlas via a DATABASE_URL variable for the data layer.

sideRight.title: GitHub Actions workflow for a MERN project

children.title: Writing a workflow that tests and deploys on merge to main
children.description: A workflow YAML defines triggers (push to main), jobs (test, build, deploy), and steps. Use actions/checkout, actions/setup-node, and run: npm test before any deploy step.

children.title: Production environment variables and secret management
children.description: Store secrets in GitHub Actions Secrets (Settings → Secrets → Actions). Reference them as ${{ secrets.MY_SECRET }} in the workflow. Never echo secrets or log them.

pern.title: Web Fundamentals
pern.description: Every PERN app ships HTML, CSS and JavaScript to the browser. Learn how pages are structured, styled and made interactive before touching any framework.
pern.challenge: Build a responsive landing page with a working contact form using only HTML and CSS.

sideLeft.title: How browsers render HTML, CSS, and JavaScript

children.title: The critical rendering path and render-blocking resources
children.description: Browsers parse HTML into DOM and CSS into CSSOM, combine them, then layout and paint. Understanding this tells you why script placement and stylesheet ordering affect page speed.

children.title: Semantic HTML elements and the accessibility tree
children.description: Semantic elements like <nav>, <main>, and <article> map to accessibility roles that screen readers and search engines use. Using <div> for everything makes your app inaccessible.

sideLeft.title: CSS layout: the box model, flexbox, and grid

children.title: box-sizing: border-box and why it simplifies layout math
children.description: By default, width excludes padding and border. border-box includes them, making width predictable. Setting * { box-sizing: border-box } is standard practice in every PERN project.

children.title: CSS Grid for two-dimensional page layouts
children.description: Grid lets you define rows and columns simultaneously with grid-template-areas. It's the right tool for page-level layout; use flexbox inside cells for one-dimensional component layout.

children.title: querySelector, textContent, and classList for DOM updates
children.description: These three APIs cover 80% of vanilla DOM manipulation. classList.toggle(), classList.add/remove() let you drive visual state changes from JavaScript without touching inline styles.

children.title: Responsive design with media queries and relative units
children.description: Use rem for font sizes (scales with user preferences), % and vw/vh for layout, and min-width media queries for breakpoints. These make sites readable at any screen size.

sideRight.title: Browser DevTools for debugging HTML, CSS, and network

children.title: Inspecting and live-editing styles in the Styles panel
children.description: The DevTools Styles panel lets you toggle CSS rules, add new declarations, and see which rule wins the cascade. Computed panel shows the final resolved value for any property.

children.title: Network tab: reading request/response headers and timing
children.description: The Network tab records every HTTP request. Check status codes, headers, and the waterfall timing to diagnose slow pages or unexpected API calls.

pern.title: Modern JavaScript & TypeScript
pern.description: Write the kind of JavaScript a PERN codebase actually uses, then add static types with TypeScript to catch bugs before runtime.
topics: Array & object methods — map, filter, reduce
pern.challenge: Convert a small JavaScript utility library to TypeScript with full type coverage and no 'any'.

sideLeft.title: TypeScript's type system under the hood

children.title: Structural typing: why TypeScript matches shapes, not names
children.description: TypeScript uses structural typing — if object A has all the properties of type T, A is assignable to T regardless of its declared type. This differs from nominal typing in Java/C#.

children.title: Type narrowing with typeof, instanceof, and discriminated unions
children.description: TypeScript narrows types inside conditional branches. A discriminated union uses a shared literal field (type: 'success' | 'error') to let TypeScript know which shape you have in each branch.

sideLeft.title: The JavaScript event loop and async semantics

children.title: Promises, async/await, and the microtask queue
children.description: async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises. Each await suspends execution and resumes in a microtask when the promise settles. Uncaught Promise rejections become unhandledRejection events.

sideRight.title: TypeScript syntax patterns in PERN projects

children.title: Interfaces versus type aliases: when to use each
children.description: Interfaces are open (can be extended via declaration merging) and work well for object shapes. Type aliases are more flexible (can represent unions, primitives, tuples). In PERN, use interfaces for API shapes and types for unions.

children.title: Generics for reusable API response and pagination types
children.description: Generic types like ApiResponse<T> or PaginatedResult<T> let you type API wrappers once and reuse them for every endpoint. This eliminates 'any' while keeping code DRY.

children.title: Key tsconfig options: strict, target, module, and paths
children.description: strict enables all strictness flags including strictNullChecks. target sets the JS output version; module controls the module system. Paths lets you use @/components instead of relative imports.

children.title: Utility types: Partial, Required, Pick, Omit, and Record
children.description: TypeScript's built-in utility types transform existing types. Partial<T> makes all fields optional (useful for update DTOs); Omit<T, 'password'> creates a safe user type without sensitive fields.

pern.title: React Fundamentals
pern.description: Build the 'R' in PERN. Think in components, manage local state, and render dynamic UIs with React and modern hooks.
pern.challenge: Build a task board that fetches, adds, filters and deletes tasks against a mock API.

children.title: How React diffs the virtual DOM to minimize real DOM changes
children.description: React builds a virtual DOM tree, compares it to the previous render, and applies only the minimal mutations to the real DOM. This diffing is the reason React is fast even with frequent state updates.

children.title: Unidirectional data flow and why it simplifies debugging
children.description: Data flows down through props; child components communicate up via callback functions passed as props. This single direction makes state changes traceable and bugs easy to locate.

children.title: Why state updates are asynchronous and stale closures occur
children.description: useState's setter is asynchronous — the new value isn't available until the next render. Using the state value in an effect or event handler may capture a stale closure; use the functional updater form to avoid this.

children.title: useEffect dependencies and the cleanup pattern
children.description: The dependency array controls when effects re-run. Every reactive value used inside the effect must be listed. Return a cleanup function to cancel subscriptions, timers, or fetches on unmount.

children.title: JSX compiles to React.createElement — implications for JSX rules
children.description: JSX transforms to function calls. A component must return a single root element (or Fragment) because a function can only return one value. Fragments avoid adding unnecessary DOM nodes.

resources.label: React Docs — Writing Markup with JSX

children.title: Lifting state up to the nearest common ancestor
children.description: When two components need the same state, lift it to their lowest common parent and pass it down via props. This is the React way of sharing state before reaching for a state manager.

resources.label: React Docs — Sharing State Between Components

sideRight.title: Fetching data from APIs in React

children.title: Data fetching in useEffect: loading, data, and error states
children.description: Fetch data inside useEffect, track loading/error/data in state, and handle cleanup by aborting the fetch on unmount using AbortController. This pattern is what React Query automates.

resources.label: React Docs — You Might Not Need an Effect

children.title: Rendering lists with key props derived from stable IDs
children.description: Keys must be stable, unique, and not index-based when items can reorder. React uses keys to decide which list items changed, added, or removed during reconciliation.

pern.title: React Advanced & State
pern.description: Scale your React app with routing, global state and server-state caching — the patterns real production frontends rely on.
pern.challenge: Add multi-page routing, a global theme store, and cached server data to your task board.

sideLeft.title: How React Router v6 renders nested route trees

children.title: Nested routes and the Outlet component for shared layouts
children.description: React Router v6 uses an <Outlet> placeholder inside a parent route's component to render the matched child route. This replaces the old switch-based approach and makes layout composition purely declarative.

children.title: TanStack Query cache model: staleTime, cacheTime, and invalidation
children.description: staleTime controls how long a query result is considered fresh before a background refetch. Calling queryClient.invalidateQueries() marks matching queries stale and triggers an immediate background fetch.

sideLeft.title: Zustand's subscription model versus React Context

children.title: How Zustand avoids Context's re-render problem with selectors
children.description: Zustand components only re-render when the specific slice they select changes. Unlike Context where any value change re-renders every consumer, Zustand's selector pattern is surgical.

children.title: useMemo and useCallback: when memoization helps versus hurts
children.description: Memoization prevents recalculations and unnecessary child re-renders but adds comparison overhead. Only apply it when a React Profiler shows a measurable regression — premature memoization obscures code.

sideRight.title: React Router navigation APIs and protected routes

children.title: useNavigate, useParams, and useSearchParams
children.description: useNavigate replaces the history object for programmatic navigation. useParams reads dynamic URL segments; useSearchParams reads and sets query strings. Both are typed with TypeScript generics in PERN.

children.title: Building protected routes with an auth-checking wrapper
children.description: Wrap protected routes in a component that reads auth state and redirects unauthenticated users with <Navigate to='/login'>. Combine with Zustand or Context for a clean, reusable pattern.

children.title: Extracting shared logic into custom hooks
children.description: A custom hook is a function whose name starts with 'use' and that calls other hooks. Hooks like useFetch, useForm, or usePagination let you share stateful logic across pages without prop drilling.

resources.label: React Docs — Reusing Logic with Custom Hooks

children.title: React.lazy and Suspense for route-level code splitting
children.description: Wrapping a dynamic import in React.lazy() defers loading that chunk until the route is visited. Suspense shows a fallback while the chunk loads, keeping initial bundle size small.

pern.title: Node.js & Express APIs
pern.description: Build the 'E' and 'N' in PERN. Create RESTful APIs with Express, structure controllers and middleware, and handle errors cleanly.
pern.challenge: Build a REST API for a notes app with CRUD routes, validation, and centralized error handling.

sideLeft.title: The Node.js event loop and non-blocking I/O model

children.title: How libuv delegates I/O so Node can handle concurrent connections
children.description: Node offloads file system and network I/O to libuv's thread pool. The event loop picks up callbacks when they complete, letting a single thread serve thousands of concurrent HTTP connections.

resources.label: Node.js Docs — The Node.js Event Loop

children.title: Express middleware chain: how next() passes control
children.description: Every Express handler receives (req, res, next). Calling next() passes control to the next middleware in the stack; calling next(err) jumps to error-handling middleware. Middleware order is execution order.

children.title: Resource-based URLs and correct HTTP verb semantics
children.description: REST URLs name resources (/posts/42), not actions (/getPost). GET reads, POST creates, PATCH partially updates, PUT replaces, DELETE removes. Correct verbs let clients predict behaviour and enable caching.

children.title: HTTP status codes: 2xx success, 4xx client error, 5xx server error
children.description: 201 Created on POST success, 204 No Content on DELETE, 400 Bad Request for invalid input, 401 Unauthorized vs 403 Forbidden, 422 Unprocessable Entity for validation failures. Correct codes make APIs self-documenting.

sideRight.title: Structuring an Express application for a PERN project

children.title: Router modules and controller separation by resource
children.description: express.Router() creates a mini-app. Mount separate routers for /users, /posts, /auth under their respective prefixes. Keep route files thin — delegate logic to controller functions.

children.title: Environment configuration with dotenv and config validation
children.description: Load env vars with dotenv.config() before anything else. Validate required variables at startup with a schema (envalid or Zod) so the app fails fast with a clear error instead of crashing at runtime.

sideRight.title: Input validation and centralized error handling

children.title: Request body validation with express-validator
children.description: Chain validators on route definitions: body('email').isEmail(), body('age').isInt({min:0}). Call validationResult(req) at the top of the handler and return 422 if there are errors.

children.title: Four-argument error middleware registered last in app.use()
children.description: Error middleware has signature (err, req, res, next). Register it after all routes. Call next(err) from anywhere in the chain to skip to it and return a consistent JSON error shape.

pern.title: Relational Databases & SQL
pern.description: PERN is relational at its core. Learn to model data into tables and query it fluently with SQL before wiring it to Node.
pern.challenge: Design a normalized schema for a blog (users, posts, comments, tags) and write the queries to power its pages.

children.title: Normalization: eliminating redundancy across 1NF, 2NF, 3NF
children.description: Normalization splits data into tables to eliminate redundancy. 1NF: atomic values; 2NF: no partial dependencies; 3NF: no transitive dependencies. Over-normalizing hurts read performance; under-normalizing hurts consistency.

children.title: Primary keys, foreign keys, and referential integrity
children.description: Primary keys uniquely identify rows. Foreign keys enforce referential integrity — a foreign key value must exist in the referenced table or be NULL. Violating this raises a constraint error, not a silent bug.

children.title: How the query planner executes JOIN operations
children.description: INNER JOIN returns only rows that match in both tables; LEFT JOIN returns all left rows with NULLs for unmatched right rows. The planner chooses hash join, merge join, or nested loop based on table sizes and indexes.

children.title: GROUP BY with aggregate functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG
children.description: GROUP BY collapses rows with the same value into one group. Aggregate functions then compute a value per group. HAVING filters groups after aggregation, unlike WHERE which filters before.

children.title: SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY, LIMIT: the core query structure
children.description: Every SQL query starts with SELECT columns FROM table. Add WHERE to filter rows, ORDER BY to sort, and LIMIT/OFFSET for pagination. Logical order differs from execution order — WHERE runs before SELECT.

children.title: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE with WHERE clauses
children.description: Always include a WHERE clause in UPDATE and DELETE. An UPDATE without WHERE updates every row; a DELETE without WHERE empties the table. Wrap destructive operations in a transaction.

sideRight.title: Designing the blog schema for the PERN challenge

children.title: Junction tables for many-to-many: posts_tags example
children.description: A post can have many tags; a tag can belong to many posts. Model this with a posts_tags junction table with foreign keys to both sides. Never store comma-separated IDs in a column.

children.title: psql CLI basics: connecting, listing tables, and running queries
children.description: \l lists databases, \c dbname connects, \dt lists tables, \d tablename shows schema. Use psql for quick inspection and ad-hoc queries without a GUI client.

pern.title: PostgreSQL in Depth
pern.description: Go beyond basic SQL into the features that make PostgreSQL powerful — indexing, transactions, JSON and performance.
pern.challenge: Add indexes and use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to cut a slow blog query's execution time, then store post metadata in JSONB.

sideLeft.title: PostgreSQL internals: indexes and the query planner

children.title: How B-tree indexes speed up equality and range queries
children.description: A B-tree index stores column values in a balanced tree, enabling O(log n) lookups instead of full table scans. Partial indexes (WHERE active = true) are smaller and faster for filtered queries.

children.title: EXPLAIN ANALYZE: reading cost estimates and actual timings
children.description: EXPLAIN shows the query plan with estimated costs; EXPLAIN ANALYZE executes the query and shows actual row counts and timings. Seq Scan on a large table is the signal to add an index.

children.title: ACID properties: why atomicity and isolation matter for data integrity
children.description: Atomicity guarantees all or nothing; if one statement in a transaction fails, all changes roll back. Isolation levels control how concurrent transactions see each other's uncommitted changes.

children.title: JSONB columns for flexible semi-structured data
children.description: JSONB stores JSON as binary, allowing GIN-indexed queries on nested fields. Use it for metadata or user-defined attributes rather than adding columns to a rigid schema.

children.title: Creating roles, databases, and granting privileges
children.description: CREATE ROLE appuser WITH LOGIN PASSWORD '...'; GRANT CONNECT ON DATABASE mydb TO appuser; GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA public TO appuser. Least-privilege roles prevent an app bug from dropping tables.

children.title: Views and CTEs for reusable query logic
children.description: A VIEW stores a named query you can SELECT from like a table. A CTE (WITH clause) is a named subquery scoped to one statement. Both avoid duplicating complex joins across application code.

children.title: Window functions: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, and LAG over partitions
children.description: Window functions compute values across a set of rows related to the current row without collapsing them into a group. ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY created_at) assigns a rank per user.

children.title: Index selection: composite versus partial indexes
children.description: Composite indexes (col_a, col_b) satisfy queries that filter on col_a alone or both columns, but not col_b alone. Partial indexes only index rows matching a condition, making them smaller and faster.

pern.title: Data Access Layer
pern.description: Connect Express to PostgreSQL through a typed data layer. Run migrations, model relations, and avoid SQL injection.
pern.challenge: Replace raw SQL in your notes API with Prisma, add a migration, and wrap a multi-step write in a transaction.

sideLeft.title: How Prisma generates type-safe queries from your schema

children.title: Prisma schema as the single source of truth for types and migrations
children.description: Prisma reads the schema.prisma file to generate both the TypeScript client and migration SQL. Changing a field in the schema generates a migration diff — you never write ALTER TABLE manually.

children.title: Connection pooling with node-postgres and why it matters
children.description: Creating a new PostgreSQL connection per request takes ~50ms and limits concurrency. A pool reuses connections. The pg Pool class manages min/max pool size and queues queries when all connections are in use.

children.title: Why string interpolation in SQL is a critical security vulnerability
children.description: Concatenating user input into a SQL string allows attackers to inject arbitrary SQL. Parameterized queries ($1, $2 in pg; Prisma's typed methods) send values separately from the query structure, preventing injection.

sideRight.title: Using Prisma in a Node/Express project

children.title: Prisma CRUD: findMany, create, update, delete with filters
children.description: prisma.post.findMany({ where: { published: true }, orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' } }) is fully typed. Prisma autocompletes field names and operators based on your schema.

children.title: Nested writes: creating related records in one operation
children.description: Prisma nested writes let you create a post and its tags in a single call with prisma.post.create({ data: { tags: { create: [...] } } }). This runs as a transaction automatically.

sideRight.title: Migrations and seeding in a PERN project

children.title: prisma migrate dev versus prisma migrate deploy
children.description: migrate dev generates and applies a migration locally; it also regenerates the Prisma client. migrate deploy applies pending migrations in production without generating new ones — use it in CI/CD pipelines.

children.title: Seeding the database with prisma/seed.ts
children.description: Add a seed script at prisma/seed.ts and register it in package.json prisma.seed field. Run prisma db seed to populate development data. Use upsert in seed scripts to keep them idempotent.

pern.title: Auth, Security & Validation
pern.description: Secure the full stack. Implement registration and login, protect routes, validate input, and follow OWASP basics.
pern.challenge: Add register/login with hashed passwords and JWTs, validate every request body with Zod, and protect write routes.

sideLeft.title: Password hashing and JWT security internals

children.title: bcrypt's adaptive work factor and per-password salting
children.description: bcrypt applies a salt to the password before hashing, meaning identical passwords produce different hashes. The work factor (cost) makes each hash computation intentionally slow, raising the cost of brute-force attacks.

children.title: JWT structure: header.payload.signature and what to put in each
children.description: The signature is HMAC-SHA256(base64(header) + '.' + base64(payload), secret). The payload is not encrypted — anyone can decode it. Never store passwords, PII, or secrets in JWT payloads.

sideLeft.title: Web security: XSS, CSRF, and secure cookies

children.title: HTTP-only cookies prevent XSS from stealing auth tokens
children.description: JavaScript cannot read HTTP-only cookies, so an XSS payload that injects a script cannot exfiltrate the auth token. Combine with Secure (HTTPS only) and SameSite=Strict for defense in depth.

children.title: Zod schema validation: parsing untrusted input at the boundary
children.description: Zod schemas define the expected shape and constraints. z.parse() throws on invalid input; z.safeParse() returns a result object. Define schemas once and share them between server validation and client forms.

sideRight.title: Implementing JWT auth in a PERN Express API

children.title: Signing JWTs with jsonwebtoken and setting expiry
children.description: jwt.sign({ userId, role }, process.env.JWT_SECRET, { expiresIn: '15m' }) creates a short-lived access token. Store the refresh token in an HTTP-only cookie and issue a new access token via a /refresh endpoint.

children.title: Auth middleware: verify Bearer token and attach user to req
children.description: Read the Authorization header, strip 'Bearer ', call jwt.verify(), and attach the decoded payload to req.user. Apply this middleware to any router that requires authentication.

sideRight.title: Security hardening with Zod, Helmet, and rate limiting

children.title: helmet middleware for secure HTTP response headers
children.description: helmet sets Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, and other headers that defend against clickjacking, MIME sniffing, and XSS. Add it as the first middleware in your Express app.

children.title: express-rate-limit to prevent brute-force on auth endpoints
children.description: Apply rate limiting specifically to /login and /register — not the whole API. Limit to 10 requests per 15 minutes per IP. Return 429 Too Many Requests with a Retry-After header.

pern.title: Testing, CI/CD & Deployment
pern.description: Ship your PERN app with confidence. Test the stack, containerize it, automate CI, and deploy frontend, API and database.
topics: Deploying React to Vercel & API to Railway/Render
Managed Postgres & connection strings in prod
pern.challenge: Containerize the full app with Docker Compose, add a CI pipeline that runs tests, and deploy it end to end.

sideLeft.title: Testing strategy for a full-stack PERN application

children.title: Testing pyramid: unit tests fast, integration tests real, E2E minimal
children.description: Unit tests cover pure functions and isolated logic; integration tests hit the real database; E2E tests drive a real browser. Integration tests give the highest confidence per effort in a PERN app.

children.title: Docker Compose for reproducible test environments
children.description: A docker-compose.yml with Node and PostgreSQL services makes the environment reproducible on any machine. Spin it up with docker compose up -d before running integration tests in CI.

children.title: Multi-stage Dockerfiles for lean Node production images
children.description: Use a build stage to install devDependencies and compile; copy only the dist folder and node_modules from a prod install into the final stage. This shrinks the image from GB to MB.

children.title: GitHub Actions workflow: test, build, and deploy on merge
children.description: A workflow triggers on push to main, runs vitest, builds the Docker image, pushes to a registry, and deploys. Secrets are stored in repository settings and injected as environment variables.

sideRight.title: Writing tests for the PERN stack

children.title: Vitest unit tests for utility functions and Zod schemas
children.description: Test pure helper functions (formatDate, paginateQuery) and Zod schema validation edge cases. These are fast, have no dependencies, and run in milliseconds.

children.title: Supertest integration tests for Express endpoints
children.description: Supertest binds your Express app to a random port and makes real HTTP requests in-process. Test the full request/response cycle including validation, auth middleware, and database writes.

sideRight.title: Deployment: React to Vercel, API to Railway, Postgres to managed host

children.title: Vercel project settings, build output, and environment variables
children.description: Set the root directory to your frontend folder, output directory to dist (Vite) or build (CRA). Add environment variables in the Vercel dashboard — they're injected at build time for VITE_* prefixed vars.

children.title: Running Prisma migrations in production deployments
children.description: Add a release command (Procfile or Railway start command) of prisma migrate deploy && node dist/index.js. This applies pending migrations before the new server version starts.

t3.title: TypeScript and Modern JavaScript Foundations
t3.description: This layer builds the type-safe JavaScript foundation the entire T3 stack relies on. Strong typing skills make every later tool in the stack easier to use and far less error prone.
topics: Static types, interfaces, and type aliases
t3.challenge: Build a typed command-line task tracker that stores tasks in a JSON file.

sideLeft.title: TypeScript's type system: structural typing and inference

children.title: Structural typing: assignability is based on shape, not name
children.description: TypeScript checks whether an object has the required properties, not whether it was declared as a specific type. This means an object literal with the right fields satisfies an interface even without an explicit declaration.

children.title: Discriminated unions and exhaustive type narrowing
children.description: A discriminated union uses a shared literal field (kind: 'success' | 'error') to let TypeScript narrow to the right variant in each branch. Combined with never in a default case, the compiler catches missing branches.

sideLeft.title: Generics, utility types, and async patterns

children.title: Generic functions and the extends constraint
children.description: Generics let you write one function that works with many types. The extends keyword constrains what types are accepted: function getField<T extends { id: number }>(obj: T) guarantees T has an id.

children.title: Async/await, Promise types, and error handling with unknown
children.description: Async functions return Promise<T>. In TypeScript 4+, catch clause errors are typed as unknown rather than any, forcing you to narrow before using them. Use instanceof Error before accessing .message.

sideRight.title: TypeScript tooling and tsconfig for a T3 project

children.title: Key tsconfig options: strict, moduleResolution, and paths
children.description: strict enables all strictness flags including strictNullChecks. moduleResolution: bundler matches how Vite/Next resolve imports. paths aliases like @/* to src/* remove relative import chains.

children.title: Utility types: Partial, Omit, Pick, and ReturnType in practice
children.description: Partial<User> makes all fields optional for update DTOs. Omit<User, 'passwordHash'> creates a safe public user type. ReturnType<typeof myFn> extracts a function's return type without repeating it.

sideRight.title: Modern JavaScript patterns used throughout T3

children.title: ES modules, destructuring, and optional chaining
children.description: T3 uses ESM throughout. Destructuring extracts props cleanly in components; optional chaining (user?.profile?.avatar) prevents null reference errors when traversing nested objects from API responses.

children.title: Node.js module system and package.json type field
children.description: Setting type: module in package.json makes .js files use ESM. The T3 stack uses this with .ts extensions and tsx for JSX. Understanding module resolution prevents import errors at build time.

t3.title: React Fundamentals
t3.description: This layer covers building component-based user interfaces with React and TypeScript. Mastering components, props, and state is essential before adding the rest of the stack.
t3.challenge: Convert the task tracker into a React single-page app with add and complete actions.

sideLeft.title: React's rendering model and the component lifecycle

children.title: How React reconciles the virtual DOM and batches renders
children.description: React builds a virtual DOM, diffs it against the previous tree, and applies minimal DOM mutations. React 18 batches all state updates by default — even inside timeouts and native event handlers.

children.title: Hooks rules: why they can't be called conditionally
children.description: React tracks hooks by call order on each render. Calling a hook inside an if-block would change the count between renders, corrupting the internal state array. The linter rule enforces this at dev time.

sideLeft.title: TypeScript integration with React components and hooks

children.title: Typing component props with interfaces and React.FC
children.description: Define a props interface and annotate the component: function Card({ title }: CardProps). Avoid React.FC — it hides the return type and makes component typing less explicit.

children.title: Typing useState, useRef, and event handlers
children.description: useState<User | null>(null) explicitly types nullable state. useRef<HTMLInputElement>(null) types the ref target. Event handlers use React.ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement> for the event type.

sideRight.title: Building components with hooks and controlled forms

children.title: useState and useEffect: state updates, stale closures, and cleanup
children.description: Calling the setter doesn't change state until the next render. Effects that reference state values in their closure will see stale values unless listed in the dependency array.

children.title: Controlled inputs and form handling in TypeScript React
children.description: A controlled input's value is driven by React state. Type the onChange handler as (e: React.ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement>) => void and update state with e.target.value.

resources.label: React Docs — Reacting to Input with State

sideRight.title: List rendering, composition, and prop patterns

children.title: Rendering lists with stable key props from entity IDs
children.description: Keys help React identify which items changed, added, or removed. Use entity IDs from the database, not array indices. Index-based keys break reconciliation when items are reordered.

children.title: Component composition with children and render props
children.description: Pass JSX as the children prop for layout components (Card, Modal, Layout). Render props pass a function as a prop to give the parent control over what gets rendered inside.

resources.label: React Docs — Passing JSX as Children

t3.title: Next.js App Router
t3.description: This layer introduces Next.js and its App Router for routing, layouts, and rendering strategies. It is the framework backbone that ties the T3 client and server together.
t3.challenge: Scaffold the task tracker as a Next.js app with separate list and detail routes.

children.title: Server components versus client components: execution boundary
children.description: Server components run only on the server — they can access databases and secrets directly but cannot use hooks or browser APIs. 'use client' marks the boundary where code runs in the browser.

resources.label: Next.js Docs — Server and Client Components

children.title: Static rendering, dynamic rendering, and streaming with Suspense
children.description: Static routes are rendered at build time and cached. Dynamic routes opt out of caching (cookies, searchParams). Streaming with Suspense sends the static shell immediately and streams dynamic content as it resolves.

sideLeft.title: File-based routing and the layout system

children.title: How layout.tsx, page.tsx, and loading.tsx compose the route tree
children.description: layout.tsx wraps page.tsx for a route segment. Nested layouts compose without re-mounting parent layouts on navigation. loading.tsx automatically wraps page.tsx in a Suspense boundary.

children.title: Data fetching in server components: fetch with cache control
children.description: In server components, fetch() extends the native API with cache: 'force-cache' (default static), 'no-store' (dynamic), or next: { revalidate: 60 } (ISR). This controls how Next.js caches the response.

sideRight.title: Setting up and navigating a Next.js App Router project

children.title: Dynamic routes with [slug] and catch-all [...slug] segments
children.description: A file named [id]/page.tsx matches /posts/123 and provides params.id. Catch-all segments [...slug] match any depth. generateStaticParams() pre-generates dynamic routes at build time.

children.title: The Link component and useRouter for client-side navigation
children.description: <Link href='/about'> prefetches the route in the background and navigates without a full page reload. useRouter() from 'next/navigation' (not 'next/router') provides push, replace, and back in App Router.

sideRight.title: Metadata, error boundaries, and loading states

children.title: Generating SEO metadata with the Metadata API
children.description: Export a metadata object or generateMetadata function from any layout.tsx or page.tsx to set title, description, and Open Graph tags. Dynamic metadata can fetch data server-side.

children.title: error.tsx and not-found.tsx for route-level error handling
children.description: error.tsx must be a client component ('use client') and receives error and reset props. not-found.tsx renders when notFound() is called from a server component. Both scope their UI to the nearest route segment.

t3.title: Styling with Tailwind CSS
t3.description: This layer teaches utility-first styling to build responsive interfaces quickly. Tailwind keeps styles consistent and co-located with components across the project.
topics: Utility classes and the box model
t3.challenge: Restyle the task tracker with Tailwind into a polished, fully responsive layout.

children.title: Tailwind purges unused classes at build time via content scanning
children.description: Tailwind scans files listed in content: [] and removes any class not found in the source. This is why dynamically constructed class strings (bg-${color}-500) won't work — the scanner can't see them.

children.title: The cascade in Tailwind: last class wins within the same property
children.description: Tailwind classes are all single-purpose — each sets one CSS property. When two classes set the same property, the one that appears later in the stylesheet wins, which is determined by Tailwind's generated order, not your JSX order.

sideLeft.title: Design tokens and the theme system

children.title: Customizing the theme: colors, spacing, and breakpoints
children.description: Extend the theme in tailwind.config.ts to add brand colors, custom spacing values, or additional breakpoints. Use extend: {} to add to the defaults rather than replace them entirely.

children.title: Dark mode with the class strategy and prefers-color-scheme
children.description: With darkMode: 'class', add dark: variants that only apply when a dark class is on the html element. Toggle it via JavaScript to implement a manual dark mode toggle independent of the OS setting.

sideRight.title: Tailwind layout and responsive design patterns

children.title: Responsive breakpoints: mobile-first with sm, md, lg, xl prefixes
children.description: Tailwind is mobile-first — unprefixed classes apply at all sizes. sm:flex applies only at ≥640px. Build layouts for small screens first, then override with prefixed classes for larger viewports.

children.title: Flexbox and grid utilities: flex, grid, gap, col-span
children.description: flex + items-center + justify-between handles most one-dimensional layouts. grid grid-cols-3 gap-4 handles card grids. col-span-2 makes a cell span multiple columns.

sideRight.title: Reusable component patterns with Tailwind in T3

children.title: Using clsx and tailwind-merge to compose conditional classes
children.description: clsx conditionally joins class strings. tailwind-merge (twMerge) deduplicates conflicting Tailwind classes — combine them in a cn() utility used throughout T3 and shadcn/ui projects.

children.title: Applying variants with the group and peer modifiers
children.description: Add the group class to a parent, then use group-hover:text-white on children to style them on parent hover. peer lets adjacent siblings style each other — useful for checkbox-driven label styles.

resources.label: Tailwind Docs — Hover, Focus, and Other States

t3.title: End-to-End Type Safety with tRPC
t3.description: This layer connects client and server with fully typed API calls and no schemas to hand-write. tRPC removes a whole class of bugs by sharing types across the boundary.
t3.challenge: Replace the task tracker fetch calls with typed tRPC queries and mutations.

sideLeft.title: How tRPC eliminates the client-server type boundary

children.title: tRPC's type inference: how server types flow to the client
children.description: tRPC exports the router's AppRouter type. The client imports it as a type-only import, so there's zero runtime cost. TypeScript infers input and output shapes from the router definition — no code generation needed.

children.title: Zod input schemas in tRPC procedures: parsing untrusted input
children.description: Every tRPC procedure with .input(schema) calls zod.parse() on the incoming data before your resolver runs. Invalid input throws a TRPCError with code BAD_REQUEST, which the client receives as a typed error.

resources.label: tRPC Docs — Input and Output Validation

sideLeft.title: tRPC middleware and context for auth and request data

children.title: Context: passing the session and database client to procedures
children.description: tRPC context is created per request (createContext) and injected into every procedure. Put the session and Prisma client in context so any procedure can access them without importing globals.

children.title: Protected procedures with tRPC middleware
children.description: A protectedProcedure wraps the base procedure with middleware that checks ctx.session and throws UNAUTHORIZED if missing. Composing protected procedures ensures auth is never forgotten on a mutation.

children.title: Queries versus mutations: when to use each in tRPC
children.description: Queries are for reading data (GET semantics); mutations are for writes (POST/PUT/DELETE semantics). TanStack Query treats them differently — queries are cached and refetched; mutations trigger manual invalidation.

children.title: Merging routers: splitting tRPC into feature-based sub-routers
children.description: Create separate routers for posts, users, comments and merge them into the root router. The client accesses them as trpc.posts.list.useQuery() — the nesting mirrors the router structure.

sideRight.title: Using tRPC on the client with TanStack Query

children.title: useQuery and useMutation hooks generated by the tRPC client
children.description: trpc.post.list.useQuery() returns { data, isLoading, error } with full types. trpc.post.create.useMutation() returns { mutate, isPending }. These are thin wrappers around TanStack Query's hooks.

children.title: Cache invalidation after mutations with utils.invalidate()
children.description: After a mutation, call utils.post.list.invalidate() inside onSuccess to mark that query stale and trigger a background refetch. This keeps the UI consistent without a page reload.

t3.title: Database Modeling with Prisma
t3.description: This layer covers defining your data model and querying it with a type-safe ORM. Prisma turns your schema into typed client code that pairs naturally with tRPC.
t3.challenge: Persist tasks in PostgreSQL through Prisma models wired into your tRPC routers.

sideLeft.title: Prisma's schema-first approach and type generation

children.title: How Prisma generates a typed client from schema.prisma
children.description: Running prisma generate reads schema.prisma and emits TypeScript types for every model, field, and relation. The client's methods are fully typed — autocomplete knows your field names and their types.

children.title: Prisma relations: one-to-many and many-to-many in the schema
children.description: A one-to-many relation adds a foreign key field and a relation field on both models. Many-to-many uses an implicit join table (Prisma manages it) or explicit when the join table has extra fields.

sideLeft.title: Prisma migrations and the development workflow

children.title: prisma migrate dev: generating, naming, and applying migrations
children.description: prisma migrate dev computes the diff between your schema and the database, writes a migration SQL file, applies it, and regenerates the client. Each migration file is committed to source control.

resources.label: Prisma Docs — Develop with Prisma Migrate

children.title: N+1 query problem and how to fix it with Prisma's include
children.description: Fetching a list of posts and then querying each post's author separately causes N+1 database queries. Prisma's include: { author: true } fetches all authors in a single JOIN query.

sideRight.title: Prisma CRUD operations in tRPC procedures

children.title: findMany with where, orderBy, take, and skip for pagination
children.description: prisma.post.findMany({ where: { authorId }, orderBy: { createdAt: 'desc' }, take: 10, skip: page * 10 }) is the standard pagination pattern. Cursor-based pagination scales better for large tables.

children.title: Prisma transactions for multi-step atomic writes
children.description: prisma.$transaction([op1, op2]) runs both operations atomically. If either fails, both roll back. Use interactive transactions for conditional logic inside the transaction block.

sideRight.title: Prisma Studio and seeding for development

children.title: Prisma Studio: browsing and editing data visually
children.description: npx prisma studio opens a web UI to browse tables, filter rows, and edit records. It's invaluable during development for inspecting the database state without writing SQL.

children.title: Writing an idempotent seed script with upsert
children.description: Use prisma.user.upsert({ where: { email }, update: {}, create: {...} }) in seed scripts so they can be run repeatedly without creating duplicate records. Register the script in package.json prisma.seed.

t3.title: Authentication and Sessions
t3.description: This layer adds user accounts, login providers, and protected routes. Authentication is required before the app can store data that belongs to specific users.
t3.challenge: Add GitHub login so each user only sees and edits their own tasks.

sideLeft.title: OAuth flow and session management in NextAuth

children.title: How OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow works with a provider
children.description: The user clicks login, gets redirected to the provider (GitHub/Google), grants permission, and the provider redirects back with an auth code. NextAuth exchanges the code for tokens server-side, hiding them from the browser.

children.title: JWT strategy versus database sessions: trade-offs
children.description: JWT sessions are stateless and don't need a sessions table — but you can't instantly revoke them. Database sessions are revocable immediately but add a DB query per request. T3 defaults to JWT with the Prisma adapter for OAuth tokens.

sideLeft.title: Protecting tRPC procedures and Next.js routes

children.title: Reading the session in server components with getServerSession
children.description: getServerSession(authOptions) returns the session in a server component or API route without an HTTP round-trip. Use it in tRPC context creation and in server component layouts that need the user.

children.title: Role-based authorization in protected tRPC procedures
children.description: Extend the session type to include a role field via module augmentation in next-auth.d.ts. In protectedProcedure middleware, check ctx.session.user.role and throw FORBIDDEN for unauthorized roles.

sideRight.title: Configuring NextAuth in a T3 app

children.title: Setting up the Prisma adapter for user and session storage
children.description: The Prisma adapter creates Account, Session, User, and VerificationToken tables from the schema. Add the models from the NextAuth Prisma schema docs and run a migration to provision them.

children.title: Configuring GitHub and Google OAuth providers
children.description: Register an OAuth app in GitHub/Google to get a client ID and secret. Pass them to the providers array in authOptions. Store them as environment variables — never hard-code credentials.

children.title: useSession hook for accessing session in client components
children.description: useSession() returns { data: session, status: 'loading' | 'authenticated' | 'unauthenticated' }. Wrap the app in SessionProvider to make useSession available to all client components.

children.title: signIn and signOut functions and redirect behaviour
children.description: signIn('github', { callbackUrl: '/dashboard' }) initiates OAuth and redirects to /dashboard on success. signOut({ callbackUrl: '/' }) clears the session cookie and redirects to the homepage.

t3.title: Advanced State and Data Fetching
t3.description: This layer covers caching, mutations, and optimistic updates for a snappy user experience. Understanding the React Query cache that powers tRPC unlocks reliable real-time-feeling apps.
t3.challenge: Add optimistic create and delete with automatic cache invalidation to the task tracker.

sideLeft.title: TanStack Query's cache model and server state semantics

children.title: staleTime, gcTime, and the background refetch lifecycle
children.description: staleTime determines how long a query result is fresh — during this window, no refetch happens. After staleTime, the cached data is served instantly while a background refetch runs. gcTime controls when unused cache entries are garbage-collected.

children.title: Query invalidation versus refetch: when to use each
children.description: invalidateQueries marks a query stale and triggers a background refetch if the query has active observers. refetchQueries forces an immediate refetch. Prefer invalidation after mutations — it's more surgical.

sideLeft.title: Optimistic updates and rollback on error

children.title: Optimistic updates: apply immediately, rollback on mutation failure
children.description: In onMutate, cancel in-flight queries, snapshot the current cache, and apply the optimistic change. In onError, restore the snapshot. In onSettled, invalidate to sync with the server's truth.

sideRight.title: Advanced TanStack Query patterns in T3

children.title: Infinite queries for cursor-based pagination with fetchNextPage
children.description: useInfiniteQuery provides fetchNextPage, hasNextPage, and isFetchingNextPage. The getNextPageParam function extracts the cursor from the last page. Intersect an invisible div with IntersectionObserver to trigger load-more.

children.title: Prefetching data in server components for instant client hydration
children.description: Call queryClient.prefetchQuery in a server component and dehydrate the cache with dehydrate(queryClient). Pass it to HydrationBoundary on the client so the initial render has data without a loading state.

sideRight.title: Global state versus server state in T3

children.title: When to use Zustand for client-only global state
children.description: Use TanStack Query for anything from the server. Use Zustand for UI state that doesn't come from an API: modal open/closed, theme, sidebar expanded. Mixing them for server data leads to stale data bugs.

children.title: Mutation lifecycle callbacks: onMutate, onError, onSuccess, onSettled
children.description: TanStack Query mutations provide four lifecycle callbacks. onMutate runs before the request for optimistic updates. onError receives the error and rollback context. onSettled always runs — use it for final invalidation.

t3.title: Forms, Validation, and Reusable UI
t3.description: This layer builds robust forms with shared validation logic across client and server. Consistent validation and accessible components raise the quality of the whole product.
t3.challenge: Build a validated task editor form that reuses one Zod schema on client and server.

sideLeft.title: Shared Zod schemas across client and server

children.title: Why sharing schemas eliminates client-server validation drift
children.description: Define one Zod schema and import it in both the tRPC procedure (server validation) and the React Hook Form resolver (client validation). Any constraint change propagates to both sides automatically.

children.title: Zod's refinement and transform methods for complex validation
children.description: z.string().refine(val => isValidSlug(val), { message: 'Invalid slug' }) adds custom validators. z.string().transform(s => s.trim()) normalizes data during parsing. Both can be composed.

sideLeft.title: React Hook Form architecture: uncontrolled inputs with a single re-render

children.title: Why RHF uses refs instead of state to avoid per-keystroke re-renders
children.description: React Hook Form stores input values in refs (not state), so typing doesn't trigger re-renders. The component only re-renders on submit or when watch() is used. This is significantly faster than controlled inputs for large forms.

sideRight.title: Wiring React Hook Form with Zod and tRPC

children.title: zodResolver: connecting a Zod schema to useForm
children.description: Pass zodResolver(schema) as the resolver option to useForm. RHF will call schema.safeParse() on submit and populate errors from Zod's error map — no manual validation logic needed.

children.title: register, handleSubmit, and formState.errors in practice
children.description: register('email') returns ref, onChange, onBlur, and name to spread on an input. handleSubmit(onSubmit) validates on submit and calls onSubmit with typed data. formState.errors contains Zod's error messages.

sideRight.title: Accessible form UI and user feedback patterns

children.title: Associating error messages with inputs for screen readers
children.description: Use aria-describedby on the input pointing to the error message's id. This ensures screen readers announce the error when the input receives focus, making form validation accessible.

children.title: Optimistic toast notifications with react-hot-toast
children.description: Show a loading toast when a mutation starts and replace it with success/error on settlement. toast.promise(mutateAsync(data), { loading, success, error }) handles all three states in one call.

t3.title: Testing and Deployment
t3.description: This layer covers automated testing and shipping the app to production. Reliable tests and a smooth deploy pipeline turn your project into a real maintainable product.
t3.challenge: Add a test suite and deploy the finished task tracker to Vercel with a hosted database.

sideLeft.title: Testing strategy for a T3 application

children.title: Unit testing tRPC procedures with the caller API
