children.description: tRPC exposes a createCaller API that lets you call procedures directly in tests without HTTP. Pass a mock context with a fake session and Prisma client to test procedure logic in isolation.

children.title: End-to-end testing with Playwright: browser automation concepts
children.description: Playwright controls a real browser and can test the full stack. It supports multiple browsers, auto-waits for elements, and captures traces on failure. Write E2E tests for critical user flows only — they're slow.

sideLeft.title: Production deployment concepts for Next.js on Vercel

children.title: Vercel's build output: static, SSR, and Edge runtime
children.description: Vercel deploys statically generated pages to a CDN, SSR pages to serverless functions, and Edge runtime code to edge nodes. Understanding this guides caching decisions and cold start trade-offs.

sideRight.title: Writing and running tests for a T3 project

children.title: Vitest configuration for a Next.js project with path aliases
children.description: Configure vitest.config.ts with the alias entries from tsconfig paths. Use @vitejs/plugin-react for JSX support. Set environment: 'jsdom' for component tests that need the DOM.

children.title: Testing React components with Testing Library and mock server
children.description: Use @testing-library/react to render components with createTRPCReact mocked via msw or a tRPC mock. Query by role, interact with userEvent, and assert on visible text rather than implementation details.

sideRight.title: Deploying T3 to Vercel with a production database

children.title: Environment variables in Vercel: build-time versus runtime
children.description: NEXT_PUBLIC_* variables are inlined at build time into the client bundle. Server-only variables (DATABASE_URL, NEXTAUTH_SECRET) are injected at runtime. Never expose server secrets with the NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix.

children.title: Running prisma migrate deploy in the Vercel build step
children.description: Add prisma migrate deploy to the Vercel build command before next build. This applies any pending migrations to the production database before the new code goes live.

fullstack-engineer.title: Web Fundamentals
fullstack-engineer.description: This layer establishes the core building blocks of every web page and app. Solid fundamentals in markup, styling, and scripting are prerequisites for all full stack work.
topics: CSS layout with flexbox and grid
fullstack-engineer.challenge: Build a static personal portfolio page with responsive layout and a contact section.

sideLeft.title: How browsers parse and render pages

children.title: DOM, CSSOM, and the critical rendering path
children.description: Browsers parse HTML into a DOM tree and CSS into a CSSOM tree, merge them into a render tree, then layout and paint. Render-blocking CSS and JS delay the first paint — understanding this drives performance decisions.

children.title: Semantic HTML and the accessibility tree
children.description: Semantic elements like <nav>, <main>, and <button> map directly to ARIA roles used by assistive technologies. Choosing semantically correct elements is a prerequisite for accessible web applications.

sideLeft.title: CSS layout: box model, flexbox, and grid

children.title: The box model and box-sizing: border-box
children.description: Every element is a box with content, padding, border, and margin. border-box makes width include padding and border, eliminating the most common layout arithmetic bugs.

children.title: Flexbox for one-dimensional layouts, Grid for two-dimensional
children.description: Flexbox handles a row or column of items; Grid handles both axes simultaneously. Using Grid for page layout and Flexbox inside components is the standard combination.

sideRight.title: Responsive design and JavaScript DOM manipulation

children.title: Mobile-first media queries with min-width breakpoints
children.description: Write base styles for small screens and layer on breakpoints upward with min-width. This produces leaner CSS than desktop-first overrides and aligns with how most users first encounter web apps.

children.title: DOM manipulation: querySelector, classList, and events
children.description: querySelector selects elements; classList.toggle/add/remove drives state-based styling; addEventListener binds user interaction. These are the primitives every framework compiles down to.

sideRight.title: Browser DevTools and the Fetch API

children.title: Network tab: inspecting requests, responses, and status codes
children.description: The Network tab shows every HTTP request made by the page. Inspect status codes, headers, payloads, and timing to debug API calls and catch unexpected requests.

children.title: fetch() with async/await and proper error handling
children.description: fetch() only rejects on network failure — you must check response.ok for 4xx/5xx errors. Always await response.json() separately and handle both network and HTTP errors explicitly.

fullstack-engineer.title: Frontend with React
fullstack-engineer.description: This layer teaches building dynamic interfaces with React components and hooks. React is the foundation for the interactive client of the full stack application.
fullstack-engineer.challenge: Build the frontend of a recipe app that lists and filters recipes from a mock API.

sideLeft.title: React's virtual DOM and component model

children.title: How React diffs the virtual DOM to minimize real DOM mutations
children.description: React maintains a virtual DOM tree and reconciles it with the previous render on each state change, applying only the minimal real DOM mutations. This diffing is what makes React fast with frequent updates.

children.title: Unidirectional data flow: props down, callbacks up
children.description: React data flows in one direction — parent to child via props, child to parent via callback functions. This makes state changes traceable and avoids the tangled two-way bindings of older frameworks.

sideLeft.title: useState, useEffect, and the hooks model

children.title: Why state updates are asynchronous and batched in React 18
children.description: React 18 batches all state updates — even inside setTimeout and native event handlers. Calling setState doesn't update state until the next render, so reading state immediately after setting it gives the old value.

children.title: useEffect cleanup prevents memory leaks from subscriptions and timers
children.description: Return a cleanup function from useEffect to cancel any subscriptions, timers, or in-flight fetches when the component unmounts or the effect re-runs. Omitting cleanup is the most common React memory leak.

sideRight.title: Building components with Vite and React

children.title: Vite dev server: HMR, fast cold starts, and the build output
children.description: Vite uses native ES modules in development for instant HMR without bundling. The production build uses Rollup. Understanding the distinction explains why import.meta.env vars work only at build time.

children.title: Client-side routing with React Router v6
children.description: React Router v6 uses nested route definitions and <Outlet> for layout composition. <Link> navigates without a page reload; useNavigate handles programmatic navigation from event handlers.

children.title: Data fetching pattern in useEffect with loading and error state
children.description: Fetch in useEffect, track loading/data/error in useState, and cancel the request on unmount with AbortController. This manual pattern is what React Query and SWR automate.

resources.label: React Docs — You Might Not Need an Effect

children.title: Lifting state to the nearest common ancestor
children.description: When two sibling components need shared data, lift the state to their lowest common parent. Pass the data down as props and the updater function as a callback prop.

resources.label: React Docs — Sharing State Between Components

fullstack-engineer.title: Backend with Node and Express
fullstack-engineer.description: This layer covers building HTTP servers and REST endpoints with Node and Express. The backend is where business logic and data access live for the application.
fullstack-engineer.challenge: Create an Express API that serves recipes and supports create and read endpoints.

sideLeft.title: Node.js runtime and the Express request cycle

children.title: Node's event loop: non-blocking I/O on a single thread
children.description: Node delegates I/O to libuv's thread pool and processes callbacks in the event loop. This lets a single Node process serve thousands of concurrent HTTP connections without threads.

children.title: Express middleware chain: execution order and next()
children.description: Express processes middleware in registration order. Each function receives (req, res, next) — calling next() passes control forward; calling next(err) jumps to error-handling middleware registered last.

children.title: Resource-based URLs, HTTP verbs, and idempotency
children.description: REST URLs name resources (/recipes/42), not actions. GET is idempotent and safe; POST is neither; PUT is idempotent but not safe; DELETE is idempotent. Correct semantics enable caching and retry logic.

children.title: HTTP status codes: choosing the right code for each response
children.description: 200 OK for reads, 201 Created for POST success, 204 No Content for DELETE, 400 for bad input, 401 for missing auth, 403 for forbidden, 404 for not found, 422 for validation failures.

children.title: Router modules: separating routes by resource
children.description: express.Router() creates a mini-app with its own middleware. Mount separate routers for /recipes, /users, and /auth. Keep route files thin — delegate business logic to controller functions.

children.title: Centralized error middleware with the four-argument signature
children.description: Register (err, req, res, next) middleware after all routes. Call next(err) from anywhere in the chain — route handlers, async wrappers, or other middleware — to reach it and return a consistent error shape.

sideRight.title: Environment configuration and async error handling

children.title: dotenv for environment variables with .env.example documentation
children.description: Load secrets with dotenv.config() before importing other modules. Commit .env.example with placeholder values so teammates know what variables are required without exposing real secrets.

children.title: Wrapping async route handlers to catch rejected promises
children.description: Express doesn't catch async errors automatically. Wrap handlers in a catchAsync(fn) utility that calls next(err) on rejection, forwarding errors to the error middleware without try-catch in every handler.

fullstack-engineer.title: Relational Databases and SQL
fullstack-engineer.description: This layer teaches modeling and querying data in a relational database. Persistent storage with SQL is the backbone of most production applications.
fullstack-engineer.challenge: Move recipe storage into PostgreSQL with tables for recipes and ingredients.

children.title: Normalization: splitting data to eliminate redundancy
children.description: 1NF requires atomic column values. 2NF removes partial dependencies on composite keys. 3NF removes transitive dependencies. Over-normalizing hurts read performance; under-normalizing causes update anomalies.

children.title: Primary keys, foreign keys, and referential integrity constraints
children.description: Foreign keys enforce that a referenced row exists. ON DELETE CASCADE automatically removes child rows when the parent is deleted. ON DELETE RESTRICT prevents deletion when children exist.

children.title: How JOIN operations combine rows from multiple tables
children.description: INNER JOIN returns only rows with matching values in both tables. LEFT JOIN keeps all left rows, filling NULLs for unmatched right rows. The query planner chooses the join algorithm based on table size and indexes.

children.title: GROUP BY with aggregate functions and the HAVING clause
children.description: GROUP BY collapses rows with the same value into one group. WHERE filters individual rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after aggregation. Both can coexist in the same query.

sideRight.title: Writing SQL and connecting to PostgreSQL from Node

children.title: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE with parameterized queries
children.description: Always use parameterized queries ($1, $2 in node-postgres) — never string interpolation. This prevents SQL injection and lets the database cache query plans for better performance.

children.title: Connection pooling with the pg Pool class
children.description: Creating a new connection per request takes ~50ms. pg.Pool reuses connections across requests and queues queries when the pool is full. Set max pool size to match your database's connection limit.

sideRight.title: Indexes and constraints for data integrity

children.title: Creating indexes on columns used in WHERE and JOIN clauses
children.description: Without an index, PostgreSQL scans every row. CREATE INDEX ON recipes(category_id) speeds up queries that filter by category. Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE to confirm the index is being used.

children.title: UNIQUE and CHECK constraints for data integrity at the database level
children.description: UNIQUE constraints prevent duplicate emails or slugs. CHECK constraints enforce domain rules (price > 0) at the database level, catching bugs that slip past application validation.

fullstack-engineer.title: Connecting Frontend and Backend
fullstack-engineer.description: This layer wires the React client to the Express API over HTTP. Understanding the full request lifecycle is what makes you a true full stack engineer.
fullstack-engineer.challenge: Connect the React recipe app to your live API for full create and list flows.

sideLeft.title: HTTP and the browser's same-origin policy

children.title: Why the browser blocks cross-origin requests by default
children.description: The same-origin policy prevents scripts on one origin from reading responses from another. CORS is the server-controlled mechanism that opts into allowing cross-origin requests with specific headers.

children.title: Preflight OPTIONS requests and the CORS negotiation flow
children.description: Non-simple requests (DELETE, PUT, custom headers) trigger a preflight OPTIONS request. The browser checks the server's CORS headers before sending the actual request. A missing or wrong header blocks the real call.

sideLeft.title: API client organization and loading states

children.title: Centralizing API calls in a services layer
children.description: Keeping fetch calls in dedicated service files (api/recipes.js) separates concerns and makes it easy to swap the HTTP client or add request interceptors without touching component code.

children.title: Pagination with query parameters: page, limit, and offset
children.description: Pass page and limit as query params (/recipes?page=2&limit=10). The server calculates offset = (page-1)*limit and returns total count alongside the data so the client can render a page count.

sideRight.title: Connecting React to an Express API in development

children.title: Vite proxy configuration to avoid CORS in development
children.description: Set server.proxy in vite.config.ts to forward /api requests to Express. This makes the browser think both apps are on the same origin, eliminating CORS issues without touching the production configuration.

children.title: cors middleware on Express: origin allowlist and credentials
children.description: Use the cors npm package with origin: 'https://yourfrontend.com'. Enable credentials: true when the frontend sends cookies or Authorization headers. Never use origin: '*' with credentials.

sideRight.title: Managing async UI state: loading, error, and empty

children.title: Modeling all four fetch states: idle, loading, success, error
children.description: A robust data-fetching component handles all four states. Use a status: 'idle' | 'loading' | 'success' | 'error' field in state to drive conditional rendering without boolean flag combinatorial explosion.

children.title: Error boundaries for catching render errors in React subtrees
children.description: Error boundaries catch JavaScript errors in any child component and display a fallback UI instead of crashing the whole app. They must be class components but can be wrapped in a functional component helper.

fullstack-engineer.title: Authentication and Authorization
fullstack-engineer.description: This layer adds user accounts, secure login, and protected routes. Authentication is essential before users can own and manage their own data.
fullstack-engineer.challenge: Add signup and login so users can save private recipe collections.

children.title: Session-based auth: server stores state, cookie holds session ID
children.description: The server creates a session record and sends the session ID in a Set-Cookie header. On each request, the browser sends the cookie and the server looks up the session. Revocation is instant but requires a session store.

children.title: JWT-based auth: stateless tokens with HMAC signatures
children.description: JWTs encode the user identity in a signed token — no server-side storage needed. The server verifies the signature on every request. Revocation is harder; short expiry with refresh tokens is the standard mitigation.

children.title: bcrypt's adaptive cost factor and per-password salting
children.description: bcrypt applies a random salt before hashing, making identical passwords produce different hashes. The cost factor (rounds) makes each hash deliberately slow, raising the cost of brute-force attacks.

children.title: HTTP-only cookies prevent XSS from stealing auth tokens
children.description: JavaScript cannot read HTTP-only cookies, making them immune to XSS token theft. Combine with SameSite=Strict (CSRF protection) and Secure (HTTPS only) for a hardened cookie configuration.

sideRight.title: Implementing auth in Express and React

children.title: bcrypt.hash and bcrypt.compare for password hashing
children.description: bcrypt.hash(password, 12) hashes with cost 12 — high enough to be slow without making login frustrating. bcrypt.compare(plain, hash) is timing-safe; never use === to compare passwords.

children.title: JWT auth middleware: verify Bearer token and attach user to req
children.description: Read Authorization header, strip 'Bearer ', call jwt.verify(token, secret). Attach the decoded payload to req.user. Apply this middleware to protected routers, not globally.

sideRight.title: Role-based access control and React route guards

children.title: Express middleware for role-based authorization
children.description: After the auth middleware attaches req.user, add a requireRole('admin') middleware that checks req.user.role and calls next(new ForbiddenError()) if the role doesn't match.

children.title: ProtectedRoute wrapper in React Router v6
children.description: Wrap protected routes in a component that reads auth state and returns <Navigate to='/login' replace> for unauthenticated users. Pass the desired URL as state so login can redirect back after success.

fullstack-engineer.title: API Design and Advanced Backend
fullstack-engineer.description: This layer deepens backend skills with validation, layered architecture, and performance. Well-designed APIs scale better and are far easier to maintain.
fullstack-engineer.challenge: Refactor the recipe API into layered services with validation and rate limiting.

sideLeft.title: API design: layered architecture and validation

children.title: Service and repository layers for separation of concerns
children.description: Routes receive requests, controllers orchestrate logic, services hold business rules, repositories handle database queries. Each layer depends only on the layer below it, making unit testing and swapping implementations straightforward.

children.title: Input validation at the API boundary prevents invalid data propagation
children.description: Validate every piece of incoming data before it reaches business logic or the database. A validation error at the boundary with a clear message is far easier to debug than a constraint violation deep in the stack.

children.title: HTTP caching: Cache-Control, ETag, and conditional requests
children.description: Cache-Control: max-age=3600 tells clients and proxies to cache the response for an hour. ETags let clients make conditional requests — if the resource hasn't changed, the server returns 304 Not Modified with no body.

children.title: Database query optimization: indexes, EXPLAIN, and N+1 avoidance
children.description: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on slow queries to spot sequential scans on large tables. Add indexes on filtered and joined columns. Avoid N+1 by fetching related data in a single JOIN query instead of one query per row.

sideRight.title: Input validation, rate limiting, and security headers

children.title: express-validator for chained field validation on routes
children.description: Chain validators: body('email').isEmail().normalizeEmail(). Call validationResult(req) at the top of the handler and return 422 with the error array if validation failed.

children.title: Helmet for security headers and express-rate-limit for brute-force protection
children.description: Helmet sets Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, and other defensive headers in one line. Rate-limit auth endpoints to 10 requests per 15 minutes to prevent credential stuffing.

children.title: Structured logging with pino or morgan
children.description: Structured logs (JSON output) are machine-parseable by log aggregators like Datadog or CloudWatch. Use pino for high-performance structured logging in production; morgan for quick HTTP request logging in development.

children.title: Process error handlers: uncaughtException and unhandledRejection
children.description: Listen for process.on('uncaughtException') and process.on('unhandledRejection') to log and gracefully shutdown rather than silently crash. Never resume execution after uncaughtException.

fullstack-engineer.title: Containers with Docker
fullstack-engineer.description: This layer teaches packaging the app and its database into reproducible containers. Docker removes works-on-my-machine problems and prepares the app for deployment.
fullstack-engineer.challenge: Containerize the API, frontend, and database and run them with Docker Compose.

sideLeft.title: Docker containers and the image layer model

children.title: Images, containers, and the union filesystem layer model
children.description: A Docker image is a stack of read-only filesystem layers. Running an image creates a container with a thin writable layer on top. Sharing base layers across images means a node:20 base is pulled only once.

children.title: Multi-stage Dockerfiles for lean production Node images
children.description: Use a build stage to install devDependencies and compile TypeScript. Copy only dist/ and a fresh npm ci --omit=dev into the final stage. This can reduce image size from 1GB+ to under 200MB.

children.title: Docker Compose service discovery via container name DNS
children.description: Services in the same Compose network reach each other by service name: DB_HOST=postgres works because Compose creates a DNS entry for the postgres service. No IP addresses needed.

children.title: Named volumes for persistent PostgreSQL data across container restarts
children.description: Without a volume, container data is lost when the container stops. A named volume mounts a host directory into the container, persisting the database even after docker compose down.

sideRight.title: Writing a Dockerfile for a Node/Express app

children.title: Dockerfile instruction order: COPY package.json before source code
children.description: Copy package.json and package-lock.json first, run npm ci, then copy source. This caches the npm install layer — it only re-runs when dependencies change, not on every code change.

children.title: Using .dockerignore to exclude node_modules and .env
children.description: A .dockerignore file prevents node_modules, .env, and .git from being copied into the build context. Without it, the build context transfer is slow and your secrets end up in the image layer history.

sideRight.title: Docker Compose for local full-stack development

children.title: docker compose up, down, logs, and exec commands
children.description: docker compose up -d starts all services detached. docker compose logs -f api streams logs from the api service. docker compose exec postgres psql -U user db opens a database shell.

children.title: Health checks and depends_on for service startup ordering
children.description: depends_on with condition: service_healthy makes the API wait until the Postgres container's health check passes before starting, preventing connection-refused errors on cold start.

fullstack-engineer.title: Testing the Full Stack
fullstack-engineer.description: This layer covers automated testing across the backend and frontend. A reliable test suite catches regressions and gives confidence to keep shipping.
fullstack-engineer.challenge: Write unit, API, and end-to-end tests covering the recipe app core flows.

sideLeft.title: Testing philosophy and the testing pyramid

children.title: Unit, integration, and E2E tests: cost versus confidence trade-offs
children.description: Unit tests are fast and cheap but test components in isolation. Integration tests hit the real database and give higher confidence. E2E tests drive a real browser and catch full-stack bugs but are slow and brittle.

children.title: What to mock and what to test for real
children.description: Mock external services (email, third-party APIs) in tests. Use a real test database for integration tests — mocking the database hides bugs at the SQL or ORM level that only appear in production.

children.title: Playwright's auto-wait: how it retries assertions until stable
children.description: Playwright auto-waits for elements to be visible, enabled, and stable before interacting. This eliminates most flaky test sleeps — use expect(locator).toBeVisible() instead of arbitrary wait times.

sideRight.title: Writing tests for the full stack

children.title: Jest unit tests: describe, it, beforeEach, and matchers
children.description: Group related tests with describe, set up shared state in beforeEach, and assert with matchers like toEqual, toThrow, and toHaveBeenCalledWith. Run jest --watch during development for instant feedback.

children.title: Supertest for Express endpoint integration tests
children.description: Supertest binds your Express app in-process and makes real HTTP requests. Test the full request pipeline including validation middleware, auth, and database writes against a test database.

sideRight.title: React component testing and E2E with Playwright

children.title: React Testing Library: query by role, interact with userEvent
children.description: RTL's getByRole mirrors how users and screen readers find UI. Use @testing-library/user-event for realistic interactions (type, click, keyboard) rather than firing synthetic events directly.

children.title: Playwright page object model for maintainable E2E tests
children.description: Encapsulate page interactions in a Page Object class (LoginPage, RecipePage). Tests become readable sequences of high-level actions; selector updates happen in one place when the UI changes.

fullstack-engineer.title: Deployment and CI/CD
fullstack-engineer.description: This layer covers shipping the containerized app to the cloud with an automated pipeline. Continuous delivery is what turns a project into a maintained production service.
fullstack-engineer.challenge: Set up a CI/CD pipeline that builds, tests, and deploys the recipe app to the cloud.

children.title: Continuous integration: automated testing on every push
children.description: CI runs tests automatically on every branch push and pull request. A green CI badge means the branch hasn't broken any tests — it's the gate before merging. GitHub Actions is the most common CI runtime.

children.title: Container registries: pushing and pulling images for deployment
children.description: Build the Docker image in CI, tag it with the git SHA, and push it to a registry (Docker Hub, ECR, GHCR). The deployment step pulls the exact versioned image, giving reproducible deployments.

children.title: Managed PostgreSQL versus self-hosted: operational trade-offs
children.description: Managed databases (RDS, Railway Postgres, Supabase) handle backups, patching, and replication. Self-hosting gives you control but requires operational expertise. For most full-stack projects, managed wins on cost and time.

children.title: Zero-downtime deployments with rolling updates
children.description: A rolling deployment starts new instances before draining old ones. The load balancer routes traffic only to healthy new instances. Run database migrations before deploying to ensure both versions are compatible.

sideRight.title: GitHub Actions workflow for a full-stack app

children.title: Workflow triggers, jobs, and steps syntax
children.description: on: push: branches: [main] triggers on push. Jobs run in parallel by default; use needs: [test] to sequence them. Steps use actions (actions/checkout) or run: shell commands.

children.title: Service containers in Actions: running PostgreSQL for integration tests
children.description: Use services: postgres: image: postgres in a workflow job to spin up a real PostgreSQL container for integration tests. Pass connection details as environment variables to your test runner.

children.title: Environment variables and secrets in Vercel and Railway
children.description: Add environment variables in the platform dashboard — they're injected at build time (VITE_* for client) or runtime (server secrets). Never commit .env files; use .env.example for documentation.

children.title: Monitoring with structured logs and health check endpoints
children.description: Expose a GET /healthz endpoint that checks DB connectivity and returns 200 or 503. Platform load balancers hit this to determine if the instance should receive traffic. Log structured JSON so alerts can query specific fields.

mean.title: JavaScript and Web Fundamentals
mean.description: This layer grounds you in the JavaScript and web basics that the whole MEAN stack shares. A strong foundation makes both the Angular frontend and Node backend approachable.
mean.challenge: Build an interactive checklist page using plain JavaScript and the DOM.

sideLeft.title: JavaScript language internals used throughout MEAN

children.title: The event loop: call stack, microtasks, and the task queue
children.description: JavaScript is single-threaded. The event loop processes the call stack first, then flushes all microtasks (Promise callbacks), then picks one macrotask (setTimeout). This ordering explains async sequencing bugs.

children.title: Closures and lexical scoping in JavaScript
children.description: A closure is a function that retains access to its outer scope after that scope has returned. They underpin Angular services, RxJS operators, and Node module patterns — understanding them prevents variable sharing bugs.

children.title: Promises: .then chaining, .catch, and Promise.all
children.description: Promise.then returns a new Promise, enabling chains. .catch at the end catches any rejection in the chain. Promise.all rejects immediately if any input rejects — use Promise.allSettled when you need all results.

children.title: async/await: syntactic sugar over Promises with try/catch
children.description: async functions always return a Promise. await suspends execution until the Promise settles. Wrap in try/catch to handle rejections — unhandled async errors will crash Node or cause silent failures in Angular.

sideRight.title: ES6+ syntax used across the MEAN stack

children.title: Destructuring, spread, and rest in function arguments
children.description: Destructuring unpacks arrays and objects inline. Spread copies or merges collections. Rest parameters (...args) collect remaining arguments. All three appear constantly in Angular component code and Node handlers.

children.title: ES modules: import/export and their differences from CommonJS
children.description: ESM is statically analyzed at parse time; CommonJS require is dynamic. Angular uses ESM; older Node defaults to CommonJS. Understanding the boundary prevents interop errors when mixing packages.

sideRight.title: DOM manipulation and responsive layout basics

children.title: DOM events, querySelector, and classList
children.description: querySelector returns the first matching element; querySelectorAll returns a NodeList. classList.toggle/add/remove drives state-based styling. These are the primitives Angular's renderer abstracts over.

children.title: Flexbox and responsive layout with media queries
children.description: flex + items-center + justify-between handles most component-level layout. Mobile-first min-width media queries layer breakpoints upward. These apply whether you use plain CSS or Angular CDK layouts.

mean.title: TypeScript for Angular
mean.description: This layer introduces TypeScript, which Angular uses throughout. Understanding types and decorators prepares you for Angular's structured, class-based approach.
mean.challenge: Rewrite the checklist logic as typed TypeScript classes and interfaces.

sideLeft.title: TypeScript's type system for Angular development

children.title: Structural typing and why shape matters more than name
children.description: TypeScript checks structure, not declared type names. An object with all required properties satisfies an interface regardless of how it was created. This is why Angular can inject any compatible class.

children.title: Decorators: how Angular uses them to attach metadata
children.description: TypeScript decorators are functions that annotate classes, methods, or properties at design time. Angular's @Component, @Injectable, and @NgModule use decorators to register metadata the Angular compiler reads.

sideLeft.title: Classes and access modifiers in TypeScript

children.title: Public, private, protected, and readonly modifiers
children.description: private prevents access outside the class at compile time. readonly prevents reassignment after construction. Angular services use private for dependencies and public for template-accessible properties.

children.title: Generics in TypeScript: type-safe reusable code
children.description: Generics let one function or class work with multiple types. HttpClient.get<User[]>('/api/users') infers the response type. Without generics, you'd need a cast or accept 'any' everywhere.

sideRight.title: TypeScript tooling for an Angular project

children.title: tsconfig.json: strict, target, and Angular-specific options
children.description: Angular projects set strict: true, target: ES2022, and experimentalDecorators: true in tsconfig.json. The Angular CLI generates this; understanding it prevents cryptic compiler errors when upgrading.

children.title: Utility types: Partial, Required, Pick, Omit, Record
children.description: Partial<User> makes all fields optional — useful for update DTOs. Omit<User, 'password'> creates a safe public type. Record<string, number> types a dictionary. These eliminate redundant type definitions.

children.title: Interfaces for data shapes, enums for fixed value sets
children.description: Use interfaces for API response and domain object shapes. Use const enums for fixed value sets like TaskStatus or UserRole — they compile away to literal values with no runtime overhead.

children.title: TypeScript module augmentation and declaration merging
children.description: Declaration merging lets you extend existing interfaces in separate files. Angular libraries use this to extend the global Window type or add properties to Express's Request interface.

mean.title: Angular Fundamentals
mean.description: This layer covers building component-driven applications with the Angular framework. Angular provides the structured frontend for the MEAN stack.
mean.challenge: Build an Angular task board with components, routing, and a task service.

sideLeft.title: Angular's architecture and change detection model

children.title: Zone.js and how Angular knows when to run change detection
children.description: Zone.js monkey-patches async APIs (setTimeout, fetch, DOM events) to notify Angular when async work completes. Angular then runs change detection to update the view. Understanding this explains why some async code doesn't trigger updates.

children.title: Dependency injection: the hierarchical injector tree
children.description: Angular's DI system resolves providers by walking up the injector tree — component → module → root. A service provided in root is a singleton; provided in a component it's scoped to that component's subtree.

sideLeft.title: Component lifecycle hooks and template binding

children.title: ngOnInit vs constructor: when to initialize versus inject
children.description: The constructor should only inject dependencies. ngOnInit runs after Angular sets input properties — put data fetching and initialization logic there. ngOnDestroy is the place to unsubscribe from Observables.

children.title: Data binding: interpolation, property binding, event binding, two-way
children.description: {{ value }} interpolates text. [property]='value' sets DOM properties. (event)='handler()' listens for events. [(ngModel)]='value' is syntactic sugar for both together — two-way binding.

sideRight.title: Building Angular components and using directives

children.title: Structural directives: *ngIf, *ngFor, and ng-template
children.description: *ngIf conditionally renders an element; *ngFor iterates an array. Both are syntactic sugar for <ng-template> with an implicit template reference. The asterisk prefix signals Angular to rewrite them.

children.title: Angular CLI: generate, serve, build, and test commands
children.description: ng generate component my-comp creates the component, spec, and module entry. ng serve starts the dev server with HMR. ng build --configuration production produces an optimized output bundle.

children.title: Creating and injecting services with @Injectable
children.description: @Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' }) makes the service a singleton available app-wide. Inject it via the constructor: constructor(private taskService: TaskService). Never new a service manually.

children.title: Angular Router: RouterModule, routes array, and routerLink
children.description: Define routes as { path: 'tasks', component: TaskListComponent } and configure RouterModule.forRoot(routes). Use [routerLink]='/tasks' for navigation and <router-outlet> as the render target.

mean.title: Reactive Programming with RxJS
mean.description: This layer teaches observables and reactive data flow that power Angular's HTTP and forms. RxJS lets you handle async streams cleanly across the app.
topics: Common operators like map and filter
mean.challenge: Add live search filtering to the task board using RxJS observables.

children.title: Observables versus Promises: lazy, cancellable, multi-value streams
children.description: A Promise executes immediately and resolves once. An Observable is lazy — it starts only when subscribed — and can emit multiple values over time. HTTP calls emit once; form valueChanges emits on every keystroke.

children.title: Hot versus cold Observables and multicasting with Subject
children.description: Cold Observables create a new execution per subscriber (HttpClient.get). Hot Observables share one execution across all subscribers. Subject is both an Observable and Observer — use it to bridge imperative code to reactive streams.

children.title: Transformation operators: map, switchMap, mergeMap, and concatMap
children.description: switchMap cancels the previous inner Observable when a new source value arrives — ideal for search typeahead. mergeMap runs all inner Observables concurrently. concatMap queues them. Choosing wrong causes race conditions.

children.title: Memory leaks from unsubscribed Observables
children.description: Observables that never complete (interval, Subject) leak memory if not unsubscribed. Use the async pipe in templates (auto-unsubscribes on destroy) or the takeUntil pattern with a destroy$ Subject.

sideRight.title: Using RxJS in Angular components and services

children.title: The async pipe: automatic subscription and unsubscription in templates
children.description: {{ items$ | async }} subscribes to the Observable when the component renders and automatically unsubscribes when it destroys. It also triggers change detection when a new value arrives — no manual subscribe needed.

children.title: BehaviorSubject for shared state in Angular services
children.description: BehaviorSubject holds a current value and emits it immediately to new subscribers. Use it in a service to share state across components: private state$ = new BehaviorSubject<Task[]>([]); expose state$.asObservable().

sideRight.title: Live search with debounce and distinctUntilChanged

children.title: debounceTime and distinctUntilChanged for efficient search inputs
children.description: debounceTime(300) waits 300ms after the last keystroke before emitting. distinctUntilChanged suppresses emissions when the value hasn't changed. Together they reduce API calls on rapid typing.

children.title: Combining streams: combineLatest and forkJoin
children.description: combineLatest emits whenever any source emits, with the latest values from all sources. forkJoin waits for all Observables to complete and emits their last values — like Promise.all for Observables.

mean.title: Node and Express Backend
mean.description: This layer covers building the API server with Node and Express. Express is the application framework that serves data to the Angular frontend.
mean.challenge: Build an Express API exposing CRUD routes for the task board.

sideLeft.title: Node.js runtime and Express middleware model

children.title: Node's non-blocking I/O and why CPU-bound work blocks the event loop
children.description: Node's event loop handles I/O callbacks asynchronously, but synchronous CPU-bound code (parsing, encryption) blocks it for all requests. Offload heavy CPU work to worker threads or a separate service.

resources.label: Node.js Docs — Don't Block the Event Loop

children.title: Express middleware execution order and error propagation with next()
children.description: Express processes middleware in registration order. Calling next() passes to the next handler; next(err) skips to error middleware. Route-specific middleware runs before the route handler.

sideLeft.title: REST API conventions for a MEAN backend

children.title: Resource-based URL design and correct HTTP verb semantics
children.description: URLs name resources (/tasks/42), not actions. GET reads without side effects, POST creates, PATCH partially updates, DELETE removes. Consistent semantics let Angular's HttpClient cache and retry correctly.

children.title: CORS configuration for Angular consuming an Express API
children.description: Angular runs on a different port than Express in development. Configure CORS with the origin set to the Angular dev server URL. In production, restrict it to the deployed frontend domain.

sideRight.title: Structuring and running a MEAN Express API

children.title: Router modules, controllers, and the service layer pattern
children.description: Keep routes thin: express.Router() handles routing, controller functions orchestrate, service functions contain business logic. This separation makes each layer independently testable.

children.title: Environment config with dotenv and startup validation
children.description: dotenv loads .env into process.env. Validate required variables at startup — fail fast with a clear error rather than a cryptic undefined crash at runtime hours later.

children.title: express-validator for route-level request body validation
children.description: Chain validators on routes: body('title').notEmpty().trim(). Call validationResult(req) and return 422 with the error array. This keeps validation co-located with the route it protects.

children.title: Centralized error middleware and async error wrappers
children.description: Register (err, req, res, next) middleware last. Wrap async handlers in a catchAsync utility to forward rejected Promises to next(err) without try-catch in every route.

mean.title: MongoDB and Mongoose
mean.description: This layer teaches storing and querying data in MongoDB through Mongoose models. The document database completes the data layer of the MEAN stack.
mean.challenge: Persist tasks in MongoDB using Mongoose models wired into your Express routes.

sideLeft.title: MongoDB document model and schema design

children.title: Embedding versus referencing: choosing the right data model
children.description: Embed subdocuments when they are always read with the parent and are bounded in size (comments on a task). Reference with ObjectId when the related data is large, shared, or queried independently.

children.title: BSON types: ObjectId, Date, and Int64 beyond JSON
children.description: MongoDB stores BSON which includes types JSON lacks — ObjectId for IDs, Date for timestamps, Int32/Int64 for integers. ObjectId encodes a 4-byte timestamp, making documents sortable by insertion time.

sideLeft.title: Mongoose schema validation and middleware hooks

children.title: Schema types, validators, required, and default values
children.description: Mongoose schemas define shape with type, required, unique, and default. Validation runs before save, giving an application-level data integrity layer on top of MongoDB's schemaless storage.

children.title: Pre/post hooks for cross-cutting concerns like password hashing
children.description: userSchema.pre('save', async function() { if (this.isModified('password')) this.password = await bcrypt.hash(this.password, 12); }) hashes passwords automatically without callers remembering to do it.

children.title: Model.find(), findById(), create(), findByIdAndUpdate()
children.description: These four methods cover most CRUD operations. findByIdAndUpdate with { new: true, runValidators: true } returns the updated document and runs schema validators. Always handle null returns for findById.

children.title: Query operators: $in, $gte, $regex, and $or for filtering
children.description: Task.find({ status: { $in: ['open', 'in-progress'] }, dueDate: { $lte: new Date() } }) uses MongoDB operators. Mongoose translates these into the MongoDB query language automatically.

children.title: mongoose.connect with connection event handling
children.description: Call mongoose.connect(MONGODB_URI) once at startup and listen for 'connected' and 'error' events. In production, set serverSelectionTimeoutMS and enable retry on failure for resilience.

children.title: Mongoose populate for resolving ObjectId references
children.description: Task.find().populate('assignee', 'name email') replaces the ObjectId with the referenced User document fields. This avoids manual joins while keeping documents normalized.

mean.title: Connecting Angular to the API
mean.description: This layer wires the Angular client to the Express and MongoDB backend. Connecting the layers makes the application a working end-to-end product.
mean.challenge: Connect the Angular task board to your live API for full CRUD over HTTP.

sideLeft.title: Angular HttpClient and reactive HTTP patterns

children.title: How HttpClient returns cold Observables for each request
children.description: HttpClient.get() returns a cold Observable — it doesn't make the HTTP request until subscribed. Each subscription triggers a new request. Use shareReplay(1) to multicast a single request to multiple subscribers.

children.title: HTTP interceptors for centralized auth header injection
children.description: An HttpInterceptor runs for every request. Implement intercept(req, next) to clone the request with an Authorization header and return next.handle(modifiedReq). Register it as a provider in the app.

sideLeft.title: Error handling in Observable-based HTTP calls

children.title: catchError operator: handling HTTP errors in the service layer
children.description: pipe(catchError(err => { console.error(err); return throwError(() => err); })) intercepts errors in the Observable stream. Handle 401s globally in an interceptor; handle domain errors in the service.

children.title: Environment-based API URL configuration in Angular
children.description: Angular's environment.ts / environment.prod.ts lets you define apiUrl per environment. The CLI swaps files at build time with --configuration production, keeping dev and prod URLs separate without runtime branching.

sideRight.title: Making typed HTTP calls with Angular HttpClient

children.title: Typing HTTP responses with generics: HttpClient.get<Task[]>()
children.description: HttpClient methods accept a type parameter: this.http.get<Task[]>('/api/tasks') returns Observable<Task[]>. This gives you full TypeScript inference on the response without manual type assertions.

children.title: Loading and error state management with the async pipe
children.description: Expose tasks$ = this.taskService.getTasks() and bind with *ngIf='tasks$ | async as tasks; else loading'. The async pipe subscribes, unwraps, and auto-unsubscribes — no manual subscribe or unsubscribe.

children.title: retryWhen and retry operators for transient API failures
children.description: pipe(retry({ count: 3, delay: 1000 })) retries a failed HTTP request up to 3 times with a 1-second delay. Only retry idempotent requests (GET) — retrying a POST could create duplicates.

children.title: takeUntilDestroyed for automatic unsubscription in Angular 16+
children.description: takeUntilDestroyed() automatically completes an Observable when the component is destroyed. It replaces the boilerplate destroy$ Subject / takeUntil pattern and integrates with Angular's DestroyRef.

mean.title: Authentication with JWT
mean.description: This layer secures the stack with token-based login and protected routes. Authentication lets users manage their own private task data.
mean.challenge: Add JWT login with Angular route guards so users see only their own tasks.

sideLeft.title: JWT auth security: token structure and storage

children.title: JWT header.payload.signature: what each part contains
children.description: The header specifies the algorithm (HS256). The payload holds claims (userId, role, exp). The signature is HMAC of both, using the server secret. Anyone can decode the payload — never put passwords or PII in it.

children.title: Token storage: why memory > localStorage > HTTP-only cookie trade-offs
children.description: Memory (JS variable) is safest from XSS but lost on page refresh. HTTP-only cookies are immune to XSS but vulnerable to CSRF. localStorage is convenient but readable by any JS on the page. Choose based on your threat model.

sideLeft.title: Angular route guards for client-side protection

children.title: CanActivate guard: redirecting unauthenticated users at the router level
children.description: A CanActivate guard checks auth state and returns true to allow navigation or a UrlTree to redirect. Inject the Router to create the redirect: return this.router.createUrlTree(['/login']).

children.title: CanActivate is client-side only — server routes must always verify tokens
children.description: Route guards protect the UI experience but anyone can bypass them with DevTools. Every API endpoint must independently verify the JWT. Never rely solely on the Angular guard to protect data.

sideRight.title: Implementing JWT auth on the Express backend

children.title: Signing and verifying JWTs with jsonwebtoken
children.description: jwt.sign({ userId, role }, secret, { expiresIn: '15m' }) creates the token. jwt.verify(token, secret) validates it and returns the payload. Set short expiry on access tokens and use refresh tokens for longevity.

children.title: Auth middleware: extract Bearer token and attach user to req
children.description: Split Authorization header on ' ', verify the second part, attach decoded payload to req.user, call next(). On verification failure, call next(new UnauthorizedError()) to reach error middleware.

sideRight.title: Angular auth service and HTTP interceptor

children.title: Auth service: storing token, exposing currentUser$, and signOut
children.description: Store the access token in a BehaviorSubject<string | null>. Expose isAuthenticated$ as currentUser$.pipe(map(Boolean)). The signOut method sets the subject to null and navigates to /login.

children.title: JWT interceptor: injecting the Authorization header on every request
children.description: In the interceptor's intercept method, read the token from AuthService, clone the request with headers: req.headers.set('Authorization', `Bearer ${token}`), and forward the cloned request.

mean.title: Advanced Angular and Forms
mean.description: This layer covers reactive forms, state management, and performance patterns. These skills make complex Angular apps maintainable and responsive.
mean.challenge: Build a validated reactive form for creating and editing tasks with subtasks.

children.title: FormControl, FormGroup, and FormArray: the form model tree
children.description: Reactive Forms represent form state as a tree of AbstractControl objects. FormControl tracks a single input, FormGroup tracks a set of controls, FormArray tracks a dynamic list. The tree mirrors the form's UI hierarchy.

children.title: Synchronous and async validators: how validation runs
children.description: Synchronous validators run on every value change and return a ValidationErrors object or null. Async validators (debounced API calls) return an Observable or Promise. Status transitions through PENDING while async validators run.

sideLeft.title: Angular change detection and OnPush strategy

children.title: OnPush change detection: only check on new inputs or events
children.description: Default change detection checks every component on every browser event. ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush only checks the component when an @Input reference changes, an Observable emits, or an event fires inside it. This dramatically reduces checks in large apps.

children.title: Lazy loading feature modules to reduce the initial bundle
children.description: loadChildren: () => import('./tasks/tasks.module').then(m => m.TasksModule) defers loading the module until the route is visited. This reduces the initial bundle size and speeds up first paint.

sideRight.title: Building reactive forms with validation in Angular

children.title: FormBuilder service for concise form group creation
children.description: Inject FormBuilder and use fb.group({ title: ['', [Validators.required, Validators.maxLength(100)]], dueDate: [''] }) instead of manually constructing FormGroup and FormControl instances.

children.title: Displaying field errors: touched, dirty, and invalid flags
children.description: Show errors only when the field is touched (user has left the field) and invalid: *ngIf='form.get("title").touched && form.get("title").invalid'. This avoids showing errors on untouched fields.

sideRight.title: Dynamic form arrays and state management patterns

children.title: FormArray for dynamic subtask lists with add/remove controls
children.description: FormArray.push(fb.control('', Validators.required)) adds a new field at runtime. FormArray.removeAt(index) removes one. Iterate with formArray.controls in the template to render each control.

children.title: NgRx signals or BehaviorSubject for cross-component state
children.description: For state shared across multiple components, a service with a BehaviorSubject is the simplest solution. NgRx is warranted when state mutations become complex enough to need a reducer and action log.

mean.title: Testing and Deployment
mean.description: This layer covers testing the app and deploying it to production. Tests and a hosted environment turn the project into a real, reliable product.
mean.challenge: Add tests and deploy the full MEAN task board with a hosted Atlas database.

children.title: TestBed: Angular's testing module for component and service isolation
children.description: TestBed.configureTestingModule() creates an isolated Angular module for each test. It compiles components, provides mocks for services, and sets up the DI context without bootstrapping the full app.

children.title: Jasmine spy objects for mocking Angular service dependencies
children.description: jasmine.createSpyObj('TaskService', ['getTasks', 'createTask']) creates a mock with spied methods. Use returnValue(of(mockData)) to return an Observable from the spy. This isolates the component from real HTTP calls.

children.title: Angular production build: ng build and the dist/ output
children.description: ng build --configuration production runs the Angular compiler with optimizations: tree-shaking, minification, and Ahead-of-Time (AOT) compilation. The output in dist/ is static HTML/CSS/JS served by any web server.

children.title: MongoDB Atlas: managed clusters, IP allowlists, and connection strings
children.description: Atlas handles provisioning, backups, and scaling. Allowlist your server's IP (or 0.0.0.0/0 for dynamic IPs). The connection string goes in your Node app's env var — never hard-coded in source.

sideRight.title: Writing Angular unit and integration tests

children.title: Testing components: fixture, debugElement, and detectChanges
children.description: fixture.detectChanges() triggers Angular's change detection so the template reflects current state. Use fixture.debugElement.query(By.css('.task-list')) to find DOM elements in tests.

children.title: HttpClientTestingModule and HttpTestingController for HTTP mocks
children.description: Import HttpClientTestingModule and inject HttpTestingController. Call controller.expectOne('/api/tasks') to assert the request was made, then flush mock data to resolve the Observable.

sideRight.title: Deploying Node and Angular to production

children.title: Serving the Angular dist/ from Express as a static SPA
children.description: app.use(express.static('dist/app-name')) serves the Angular build. Add a catch-all route: app.get('*', (req, res) => res.sendFile('index.html')) so Angular's router handles all non-API URLs.

children.title: PM2 for Node process management in production
children.description: PM2 keeps Node running after crashes, enables zero-downtime reloads (pm2 reload app), and provides log management. Use pm2 ecosystem.config.js to define app name, script path, and environment variables.

jamstack.title: Web Foundations and the JAMstack Model
jamstack.description: This layer covers web basics and the core idea of pre-rendered markup with APIs. Understanding the JAMstack philosophy frames every tool you learn later.
jamstack.challenge: Build a static landing page that pulls a quote from a public API.

children.title: Semantic HTML elements and accessibility basics
children.description: Semantic elements like <article>, <nav>, and <section> communicate meaning to browsers and assistive technologies. Using them correctly improves SEO and screen-reader support.

children.title: Static versus dynamic site rendering models
children.description: Static sites serve pre-built HTML from a CDN with no server computation at request time. Dynamic sites generate HTML on each request, adding latency but enabling personalisation.

children.title: JAMstack philosophy and the decoupled architecture
children.description: JAMstack decouples the front-end from the back-end by pre-rendering markup and using APIs for dynamic behaviour. This improves security, scalability, and developer experience.

children.title: How content delivery networks cache and serve assets
children.description: CDNs distribute copies of your static assets across globally located edge nodes so users fetch content from the nearest server. This drastically reduces time-to-first-byte.

children.title: Working with third-party REST APIs in the browser
children.description: The browser Fetch API lets you request data from external services at runtime. Understanding JSON responses and async/await patterns is essential for enriching static pages.

children.title: CSS Flexbox and Grid layout systems hands-on
children.description: Flexbox handles one-dimensional layouts while Grid handles two-dimensional ones. Together they replace older float-based approaches and are supported universally.

resources.label: CSS-Tricks: A Complete Guide to Grid

children.title: Responsive design with media queries and fluid units
children.description: Media queries let you apply different styles at different viewport widths. Fluid units like rem, vw, and percentages reduce the number of breakpoints you need.

children.title: ES6 features: arrow functions, destructuring, modules
children.description: Modern JavaScript syntax like arrow functions, template literals, and ES modules is used throughout every JAMstack framework. Mastering these reduces boilerplate significantly.

children.title: Browser DevTools for debugging and network inspection
children.description: Chrome DevTools lets you inspect DOM, debug JS, and profile network requests. The Network tab is particularly useful for verifying API calls in JAMstack apps.

jamstack.title: React and Component Thinking
jamstack.description: This layer teaches building reusable UI with React, the basis for both Next.js and Gatsby. Component thinking is central to modern JAMstack frameworks.
jamstack.challenge: Rebuild the landing page as reusable React components with a quote card.

children.title: Component model, props, and unidirectional data flow
children.description: React components are pure functions that accept props and return JSX. Data flows down from parent to child, making state changes predictable and traceable.

resources.label: react.dev: Passing props to a component

children.title: State management with useState and useReducer hooks
children.description: useState stores simple local state while useReducer suits more complex state transitions. Both trigger re-renders when values change, keeping the UI in sync.

children.title: Composition patterns and reusable component design
children.description: Composing small, focused components instead of large monolithic ones improves readability and reuse. Patterns like render props and compound components solve common composition challenges.

children.title: useEffect for data fetching and lifecycle side effects
children.description: useEffect runs after render and is used to fetch data, subscribe to events, or synchronise with external systems. The dependency array controls when the effect re-runs.

children.title: Custom hooks for encapsulating reusable stateful logic
children.description: Custom hooks are plain functions prefixed with "use" that can call other hooks. They let you extract and share stateful logic without changing your component hierarchy.

resources.label: react.dev: Reusing logic with custom hooks

children.title: Setting up a React project with Vite or Create React App
children.description: Vite is the modern choice for fast hot-module replacement and lean builds. Create React App is the classic option with a batteries-included setup.

resources.label: react.dev: Start a new React project

children.title: JSX syntax rules and conditional rendering patterns
children.description: JSX compiles to React.createElement calls and follows JavaScript expression rules inside curly braces. Conditional rendering uses ternary expressions or short-circuit evaluation.

children.title: Fetching API data with useEffect and handling loading states
children.description: A standard pattern loads data in useEffect, stores it in state, and renders a loading spinner until the response arrives. Always handle errors with a try-catch block.

children.title: React project structure and file organisation conventions
children.description: Grouping files by feature rather than by type (components/, hooks/, utils/) scales better as projects grow. Co-locating tests and styles with their component reduces navigation overhead.

jamstack.title: Static Sites with Next.js
jamstack.description: This layer covers static and hybrid rendering with Next.js. Next.js is a leading framework for fast, SEO-friendly JAMstack sites.
jamstack.challenge: Create a statically generated blog index with a Next.js page per post.

children.title: Static generation versus server-side rendering trade-offs
children.description: Static generation (getStaticProps) builds pages at deploy time for maximum CDN cacheability. SSR (getServerSideProps) generates pages per-request for real-time data.

children.title: Incremental Static Regeneration revalidation concepts
children.description: ISR lets you update static pages after deploy without a full rebuild by setting a revalidate interval. Pages are regenerated in the background when stale, keeping content fresh.

children.title: Metadata API and SEO configuration in Next.js App Router
children.description: The App Router Metadata API lets you export a metadata object from any layout or page to control title, description, and Open Graph tags. Dynamic metadata supports per-page SEO.

children.title: File-system routing and dynamic route segments explained
children.description: Next.js maps files in the pages or app directory directly to URL routes. Square-bracket filenames like [slug].tsx define dynamic segments that receive URL params as props.

children.title: App Router layouts, loading, and error boundaries
children.description: Layouts in the App Router persist UI across navigations and can nest. loading.tsx and error.tsx files provide built-in streaming and error boundary support for each route segment.

children.title: next/image component for automatic image optimisation
children.description: The next/image component automatically generates WebP, resizes for the viewport, and lazy-loads images. It avoids layout shift by requiring explicit width and height props.

children.title: next/font for self-hosted Google Fonts without layout shift
children.description: next/font downloads Google Fonts at build time and serves them from your own domain, eliminating the extra DNS lookup and preventing font-induced layout shifts.

children.title: Server Components data fetching with async/await
children.description: React Server Components allow direct async data fetching inside the component body without useEffect. The data is fetched on the server, reducing client-side JavaScript.

children.title: Caching and revalidation strategies with fetch options
children.description: Next.js extends the native fetch API with cache and next.revalidate options. Setting revalidate controls how often cached data is refreshed without a full redeploy.

jamstack.title: Gatsby and the Data Layer
jamstack.description: This layer introduces Gatsby and its GraphQL data layer for sourcing content. Gatsby excels at building content-rich, image-heavy static sites.
jamstack.challenge: Build a Gatsby photo gallery sourcing images and metadata through GraphQL.

children.title: Gatsby build process and the data layer overview
children.description: Gatsby builds a GraphQL data layer at compile time by sourcing from files, CMSs, and APIs. Pages then query this layer statically, producing pre-rendered HTML for every route.

children.title: Static queries versus page queries and their scope
children.description: Page queries receive variables from the page context and suit dynamic routes. Static queries via useStaticQuery hook work in any component but cannot accept variables.

children.title: Programmatic page creation with createPages API
children.description: The createPages API in gatsby-node.js lets you generate pages from data at build time. You provide a template component and inject context variables passed into the page query.

children.title: How source plugins pull external data into Gatsby GraphQL
children.description: Source plugins fetch data from third-party services like CMSs or APIs and create GraphQL nodes. Gatsby provides official plugins for Contentful, WordPress, and the filesystem.

children.title: Writing GraphQL queries with Gatsby GraphiQL explorer
children.description: GraphiQL is an in-browser IDE available at localhost:8000/___graphql during development. It provides autocomplete and schema introspection to help you craft correct queries quickly.

children.title: gatsby-plugin-image for responsive image processing
children.description: gatsby-plugin-image generates multiple resolutions and formats at build time and ships the StaticImage and GatsbyImage components. Images are automatically lazy-loaded with blur-up placeholders.

children.title: Gatsby configuration with gatsby-config.js and plugins
children.description: gatsby-config.js defines site metadata, active plugins, and their options. The plugin order matters for transformer plugins that depend on source plugin output.

children.title: Creating blog post templates with dynamic slug routing
children.description: A common Gatsby pattern creates a template component and loops over Markdown or CMS nodes in gatsby-node.js to generate one page per post. The slug comes from the node path or a custom field.

children.title: gatsby-transformer-remark for Markdown content parsing
children.description: gatsby-transformer-remark converts Markdown files sourced by gatsby-source-filesystem into queryable GraphQL nodes. You can extend it with remark plugins for syntax highlighting and more.

jamstack.title: Headless CMS with Contentful
jamstack.description: This layer teaches managing content in a headless CMS and pulling it into your site. Decoupling content from code lets non-developers update the site safely.
jamstack.challenge: Move the blog content into Contentful and render posts from the CMS.

children.title: Content types, fields, and validation in Contentful
children.description: Content types define the schema for your content entries, similar to database tables. Each field has a type constraint and optional validations like required or unique.

children.title: Preview versus published environments and content workflow
children.description: Contentful separates preview and delivery API keys. The preview API returns draft entries, enabling staging previews before content is published to production.

children.title: Rich text field rendering in React components
children.description: Rich text fields store structured content as a node tree. The @contentful/rich-text-react-renderer package converts this tree into React elements with customisable node renderers.

children.title: Content Delivery API vs Content Management API differences
children.description: The CDA is a read-only, CDN-backed API for fetching published content in production. The CMA has write access and is used for migrations, seeding, and editorial tooling.

children.title: GraphQL API for typed content queries in Contentful
children.description: Contentful exposes a per-space GraphQL endpoint that mirrors your content types as strongly typed queries. This enables co-locating data requirements with components.

children.title: Fetching entries with the Contentful JS SDK
children.description: The contentful npm package wraps the CDA with typed methods like getEntries and getEntry. Filtering by content type and selecting specific fields reduces payload size.

children.title: Querying linked assets and nested references from CDA
children.description: Contentful entries can reference other entries and assets. Setting the include depth parameter resolves linked entries inline, avoiding waterfall requests for nested content.

children.title: Contentful Image API for on-demand image transformation
children.description: Contentful assets are served through a CDN that accepts query parameters for width, height, format, and quality. This enables responsive images without a separate image service.

children.title: gatsby-source-contentful plugin setup and configuration
children.description: gatsby-source-contentful pulls all published entries into the Gatsby GraphQL layer. You configure it with your space ID and access token in gatsby-config.js.

jamstack.title: APIs and Serverless Functions
jamstack.description: This layer adds dynamic behavior through serverless functions and APIs. Serverless logic provides the A in JAMstack without managing servers.
jamstack.challenge: Add a serverless contact form and a newsletter signup function to the blog.

children.title: Serverless execution model and cold start trade-offs
children.description: Serverless functions run in isolated, stateless containers spun up on demand. Cold starts add latency on the first invocation but the model eliminates server management entirely.

children.title: HTTP request and response structure in serverless handlers
children.description: Netlify and Vercel functions receive an event object with headers, query params, and body. The handler returns a status code, headers, and body just like a standard HTTP server.

children.title: Environment variables and secrets management in functions
children.description: API keys must never be bundled into client-side code. Serverless functions access secrets through environment variables configured in the deployment platform dashboard.

children.title: Rate limiting strategies and handling 429 API responses
children.description: External APIs enforce rate limits and return 429 Too Many Requests when exceeded. Implementing exponential backoff and request queuing prevents cascading failures.

children.title: CORS configuration for serverless function endpoints
children.description: Browser requests to serverless functions from a different origin require CORS headers. You must set Access-Control-Allow-Origin and handle the preflight OPTIONS request.

children.title: Proxying third-party API calls through serverless functions
children.description: A serverless proxy function receives client requests, adds secret API keys from environment variables, and forwards the call. This keeps credentials off the client entirely.

children.title: Form submission handling without a backend server
children.description: Serverless functions can receive form POST requests, validate input, and send emails via services like SendGrid or Mailgun. Netlify Forms provides zero-config form handling as an alternative.

sideRight.title: Next.js API Routes and Edge Functions

children.title: Next.js Route Handlers as serverless API endpoints
children.description: Files in the app/api directory export named HTTP method handlers that become serverless functions on Vercel. They share the same Node.js environment as the rest of the app.

children.title: Edge Functions for low-latency personalisation and middleware
children.description: Edge Functions run on the CDN edge, close to the user, in a lightweight V8 runtime. They are ideal for A/B testing, redirects, and auth checks with sub-millisecond overhead.

jamstack.title: Authentication and Identity
jamstack.description: This layer adds user accounts and gated content to an otherwise static site. Identity lets you offer members-only areas without a traditional backend.
jamstack.challenge: Add member login that unlocks a subscriber-only section of the blog.

children.title: OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect flows explained
children.description: OAuth 2.0 delegates authorisation to an identity provider while OpenID Connect adds an identity layer on top. The authorisation code flow with PKCE is the safest choice for SPAs.

children.title: JWT structure, signing, and verification fundamentals
children.description: A JWT contains a base64-encoded header, payload, and signature. The server verifies the signature with a secret or public key, so no session lookup is needed per request.

resources.label: jwt.io: Introduction to JSON Web Tokens

children.title: Session cookies versus token-based authentication comparison
children.description: Session cookies store a server-side session ID in a HttpOnly cookie; tokens are self-contained and stateless. Cookies provide better security defaults for web apps; tokens suit APIs and mobile clients.

children.title: Role-based access control and gated content patterns
children.description: RBAC assigns roles like admin or member to users, and each route or resource checks the role before granting access. Storing roles in JWT claims enables stateless authorisation.

children.title: Protecting serverless API routes from unauthenticated requests
children.description: Serverless functions must validate the JWT on every request since they are stateless. Libraries like jsonwebtoken verify the token and reject requests without a valid bearer token.

children.title: NextAuth.js setup with GitHub and Google OAuth providers
children.description: NextAuth.js (Auth.js) integrates into the Next.js App Router with a single route handler. Configuring OAuth providers requires creating app credentials in the provider dashboard.

children.title: Netlify Identity for zero-config JAMstack authentication
children.description: Netlify Identity provides sign-up, login, and JWT issuance with no backend code. The netlify-identity-widget handles the UI flow and exposes user data to serverless functions via context.

children.title: Building login and signup forms with validation
children.description: Login forms send credentials to an auth endpoint and store the returned token in a cookie or memory. Client-side validation improves UX while server-side validation enforces security.

children.title: Next.js Middleware for protecting authenticated routes
children.description: Middleware runs before every matched route and can redirect unauthenticated users to the login page. Checking a session cookie or verifying a JWT in middleware is the recommended pattern.

jamstack.title: Performance and SEO
jamstack.description: This layer focuses on making JAMstack sites fast and discoverable. Performance and SEO are core selling points of the JAMstack approach.
jamstack.challenge: Audit and optimize the blog to reach strong Lighthouse scores across the board.

children.title: LCP, CLS, and INP metrics and what affects them
children.description: Largest Contentful Paint measures loading, Cumulative Layout Shift measures visual stability, and Interaction to Next Paint measures responsiveness. Each has a specific good/needs improvement/poor threshold.

children.title: Code splitting and lazy loading to reduce bundle size
children.description: Code splitting breaks JavaScript into smaller chunks loaded on demand. React.lazy and dynamic imports defer non-critical code, reducing the initial bundle and improving LCP.

children.title: SEO structured data with JSON-LD and schema.org markup
children.description: JSON-LD embedded in a <script type="application/ld+json"> tag signals content type to search engines. Schema.org types like Article, Product, and BreadcrumbList enable rich snippets.

children.title: Sitemaps and robots.txt for crawl budget management
children.description: An XML sitemap lists every URL you want indexed and helps search engines discover new pages quickly. robots.txt controls which paths crawlers are allowed to visit.

resources.label: Google: Build and submit a sitemap

children.title: Open Graph and Twitter Card meta tags for social sharing
