children.description: Open Graph meta tags control how your pages appear when shared on Facebook, LinkedIn, and Slack. Twitter Card tags do the same for Twitter and require og:image to be at least 600px wide.

children.title: Image formats WebP and AVIF for smaller file sizes
children.description: WebP provides roughly 30% smaller files than JPEG at equivalent quality, and AVIF compresses even further. next/image and gatsby-plugin-image generate both formats automatically.

children.title: Preloading critical fonts and eliminating render-blocking resources
children.description: Fonts block rendering unless preloaded or loaded asynchronously. Using font-display: swap prevents invisible text and the next/font module handles preloading automatically.

children.title: Running Lighthouse CI in GitHub Actions for regressions
children.description: Lighthouse CI runs automated audits on every pull request and fails the build if performance scores drop below a threshold. Configuration is defined in a lighthouserc.json file.

children.title: Chrome DevTools Performance panel for diagnosing bottlenecks
children.description: The Performance panel records a flame chart of JS execution, layout, and paint events. Long tasks blocking the main thread are the primary cause of poor INP scores.

jamstack.title: E-commerce and Dynamic Data
jamstack.description: This layer adds commerce and richer dynamic data to a static foundation. It shows how JAMstack scales from blogs to real transactional sites.
jamstack.challenge: Add a small store to the site with CMS products and Stripe checkout.

children.title: Product catalogue modelling in a headless CMS
children.description: Product content types store name, description, price, and images as structured fields. Variants like size or colour can be modelled as nested entries or JSON fields depending on the CMS.

children.title: Cart state management with Context API or Zustand
children.description: Cart state must persist across route changes, so it lives above the router in a React Context or a lightweight store like Zustand. localStorage can hydrate the cart on page refresh.

children.title: Stripe payment intents and the checkout session flow
children.description: Stripe Checkout Sessions are server-created objects that produce a hosted payment page URL. On success, Stripe redirects to your success URL with a session ID for order confirmation.

children.title: Stripe webhooks for reliable post-payment event processing
children.description: Webhooks send signed POST requests to your serverless function when payment events like checkout.session.completed occur. Verifying the Stripe-Signature header prevents spoofed events.

children.title: Order confirmation emails with serverless and SendGrid
children.description: After a successful payment webhook, a serverless function can trigger a transactional email via SendGrid or Resend. The email template includes order details from the Stripe session.

children.title: Stripe JS SDK and stripe-js client-side integration
children.description: The @stripe/stripe-js package loads Stripe.js asynchronously and exposes methods for creating payment elements. Always load it from Stripe CDN to satisfy PCI compliance.

children.title: Stripe CLI for local webhook testing and event replay
children.description: stripe listen forwards live webhook events to your localhost serverless function. stripe trigger fires test events so you can verify your webhook handler without real payments.

children.title: Creating Stripe Checkout Sessions in serverless functions
children.description: A serverless function uses the Stripe Node SDK to create a Checkout Session with line_items derived from the cart. The session URL is returned to the client for redirect.

children.title: Reading Stripe session data on the success page
children.description: The success URL can include {CHECKOUT_SESSION_ID} as a template variable. Your success page fetches this session ID from a serverless proxy to display the confirmed order details.

jamstack.title: Deployment and Continuous Delivery
jamstack.description: This layer covers deploying and automating builds for JAMstack sites. Git-based continuous delivery is what makes JAMstack workflows so smooth in production.
jamstack.challenge: Deploy the full site to Netlify with deploy previews and CMS-triggered builds.

children.title: Git-based deployments and trunk-based development workflow
children.description: Netlify and Vercel deploy automatically when you push to your configured branch. Trunk-based development keeps the main branch always deployable by using short-lived feature branches.

children.title: Deploy previews for pull request review and testing
children.description: Deploy previews generate a unique URL for every pull request so stakeholders can review changes before merge. Each preview runs the full build pipeline in an isolated environment.

children.title: Build hooks for triggering deploys from CMS content changes
children.description: A build hook is a unique URL that triggers a new deploy when called via HTTP POST. Contentful and Sanity webhooks can call this URL whenever content is published.

children.title: Instant rollbacks to previous deploys on Netlify
children.description: Netlify retains every previous deploy and lets you one-click restore any prior version. This provides a safety net if a new deploy introduces a regression caught after release.

children.title: Error monitoring with Sentry for production JAMstack apps
children.description: Sentry captures JavaScript exceptions and serverless function errors with full stack traces and context. Integrating source maps lets Sentry show the original TypeScript source of minified errors.

children.title: netlify.toml build commands, publish directory, and plugins
children.description: netlify.toml defines your build command, publish directory, and environment-specific overrides. Netlify Build Plugins extend the build pipeline with hooks like onPreBuild and onPostBuild.

children.title: Managing build environment variables per deploy context
children.description: Netlify supports different environment variable values per context (production, deploy-preview, branch-deploy). This lets you point staging deploys at a different CMS environment or API endpoint.

children.title: Configuring a custom domain and automatic TLS on Netlify
children.description: Netlify provisions a free Let's Encrypt certificate automatically when you add a custom domain. You add a CNAME or A record in your DNS provider pointing to Netlify's load balancer.

children.title: Netlify redirect rules and _redirects file configuration
children.description: The _redirects file in your publish directory maps old URLs to new ones with optional HTTP status codes. Rules support wildcards and splats for flexible migration patterns.

django-react.title: Python Fundamentals
django-react.description: This layer establishes core Python skills that Django is built on. A firm grasp of the language makes the framework far easier to learn.
django-react.challenge: Write a Python script that manages a simple address book in memory.

children.title: Variables, Types, and Control Flow
children.description: Python uses dynamic typing with built-in types like int, str, list, and dict. Control flow is expressed with if/elif/else, for, and while using indentation instead of braces.

children.title: Functions and Module System
children.description: Functions are first-class objects in Python, supporting default arguments, *args, and **kwargs. Modules let you split code into reusable files importable with the import statement.

children.title: List Comprehensions and Dict Patterns
children.description: Comprehensions provide concise syntax for creating lists, sets, and dicts from iterables. They are idiomatic Python and often replace explicit for loops.

children.title: Classes, Objects, and Inheritance
children.description: Python classes use __init__ for construction and support single and multiple inheritance. Special dunder methods let you customise behaviour for operators and built-in functions.

children.title: Exception Handling with try/except
children.description: Python uses try/except/finally blocks to handle runtime errors gracefully. You can raise custom exceptions by subclassing the built-in Exception class.

children.title: Virtual Environments with venv
children.description: venv creates isolated Python environments so project dependencies do not conflict. Activate the environment before installing packages to keep your global Python clean.

children.title: Installing Packages with pip
children.description: pip is the standard Python package manager. Use pip install -r requirements.txt to reproduce an environment and pip freeze to capture current dependencies.

children.title: Interactive REPL and Debugging
children.description: The Python REPL lets you experiment with code instantly. Use the built-in breakpoint() function or pdb module to step through code and inspect state at runtime.

children.title: Code Style with PEP 8
children.description: PEP 8 is the official Python style guide covering naming conventions, line length, and whitespace. Tools like flake8 and black automate style checking and formatting.

django-react.title: Django Basics
django-react.description: This layer introduces the Django framework, its project structure, and the request cycle. Django provides the batteries-included backend for the stack.
django-react.challenge: Build a Django app that lists blog posts rendered from server-side views.

children.title: Projects vs Apps in Django
children.description: A Django project is the top-level configuration container; apps are self-contained modules of functionality. Good Django design keeps each app focused on a single domain.

children.title: URL Routing and Views
children.description: Django's URL dispatcher maps URL patterns to view functions or class-based views. Views receive an HttpRequest, perform logic, and return an HttpResponse.

children.title: Request/Response Cycle in Django
children.description: Every Django request passes through middleware, the URL resolver, and finally a view before returning a response. Understanding this cycle is key to debugging and extending behaviour.

resources.label: Django Docs — Request and Response Objects

children.title: Django Template Language Basics
children.description: Django's template language uses {{ variables }} and {% tags %} to render dynamic HTML. Template inheritance via {% extends %} and {% block %} promotes code reuse.

children.title: Settings and Project Configuration
children.description: settings.py controls databases, installed apps, middleware, and more. Use environment variables or django-environ to avoid hard-coding secrets.

children.title: Serving Static Files in Development
children.description: Django's staticfiles app collects CSS, JS, and images during development. Configure STATIC_URL and STATICFILES_DIRS, then use {% load static %} in templates.

children.title: Django Admin Interface
children.description: The Django admin provides an auto-generated CRUD UI for your models. Registering models with admin.site.register and customising ModelAdmin unlocks powerful data management.

children.title: Starting a Project with django-admin
children.description: django-admin startproject and manage.py startapp scaffold the initial directory structure. Use manage.py runserver for the development server with auto-reload.

children.title: Middleware and the Django Pipeline
children.description: Middleware are hooks that process requests before they reach views and responses before they leave. Django ships with middleware for sessions, CSRF, auth, and security headers.

django-react.title: Django Models and the ORM
django-react.description: This layer covers modeling data and querying it with the Django ORM. The ORM is how Django apps store and retrieve persistent data.
django-react.challenge: Model posts and comments in PostgreSQL and query them through the ORM.

children.title: Defining Models and Field Types
children.description: A Django model maps to a database table; each field corresponds to a column. Django provides CharField, IntegerField, DateTimeField, and many more with built-in validation.

children.title: Relationships: ForeignKey, ManyToMany
children.description: ForeignKey creates a many-to-one relationship stored as a database foreign key. ManyToManyField uses a join table and lets you query related objects from either side.

children.title: Aggregations and Annotations
children.description: Django ORM supports Count, Sum, Avg, and other SQL aggregates via annotate() and aggregate(). They execute in the database rather than Python for maximum performance.

children.title: QuerySet API: filter, exclude, order_by
children.description: QuerySets are lazy; they only hit the database when evaluated. Chaining filter(), exclude(), and order_by() builds complex queries without writing raw SQL.

children.title: Database Migrations Workflow
children.description: makemigrations generates migration files from model changes; migrate applies them. Always review auto-generated migrations before running them in production.

children.title: Connecting Django to PostgreSQL
children.description: Install psycopg2 and set DATABASES in settings.py to use PostgreSQL. Use environment variables for credentials and never commit them to version control.

children.title: Raw SQL and Database Functions
children.description: For complex queries, Django allows raw() and connection.execute(). Database functions like Coalesce, Cast, and Extract let you push logic to the database layer.

children.title: Django Shell for ORM Exploration
children.description: manage.py shell opens a Python REPL with Django pre-configured. Use it to test queries, inspect data, and verify model behaviour before writing view code.

children.title: django-extensions and Model Diagrams
children.description: django-extensions adds shell_plus (auto-imports all models) and graph_models (generates entity-relationship diagrams). Both speed up ORM development significantly.

django-react.title: Building APIs with Django REST Framework
django-react.description: This layer teaches exposing your data as a REST API with DRF. The API is the bridge that lets a React frontend talk to Django.
django-react.challenge: Expose CRUD API endpoints for posts and comments using DRF viewsets.

children.title: Serializers and Data Validation
children.description: DRF serializers convert querysets and model instances to Python dicts for JSON rendering and deserialise incoming data with validation. ModelSerializer auto-generates fields from a model.

children.title: Views, ViewSets, and Generic Views
children.description: APIView gives full control over HTTP method handling. Generic views like ListCreateAPIView reduce boilerplate for common patterns. ViewSets combine related views into a single class.

children.title: Permissions and Authentication Classes
children.description: DRF separates authentication (who are you?) from permissions (what can you do?). Built-in classes include IsAuthenticated, IsAdminUser, and per-object permission hooks.

children.title: Pagination and Filtering Strategies
children.description: DRF ships with PageNumberPagination, LimitOffsetPagination, and CursorPagination. django-filter integrates with DRF to add field-level query-param filtering.

children.title: Throttling and Rate Limiting APIs
children.description: DRF throttling limits how many requests a user or IP can make in a time window. AnonRateThrottle and UserRateThrottle can be configured globally or per view.

children.title: Routers and Auto URL Generation
children.description: DRF Routers automatically generate URL patterns for ViewSets. DefaultRouter adds a browsable API root; SimpleRouter is lighter for production-only APIs.

children.title: Browsable API for Development
children.description: The browsable API renders HTML views of your endpoints in a browser, making manual testing fast. It also surfaces serializer validation errors in a readable format.

children.title: Auto-Generating OpenAPI Schemas
children.description: DRF can generate OpenAPI 3 schemas with the generateschema command. Integrating drf-spectacular gives richer schema output and Swagger/ReDoc UI pages.

children.title: Testing DRF Endpoints with APIClient
children.description: DRF's APIClient extends Django's test client with helpers for JSON requests and authentication. Use it in Django's TestCase or with pytest-django.

django-react.title: React Frontend
django-react.description: This layer covers building the React client that consumes the Django API. React provides the interactive frontend half of the stack.
django-react.challenge: Build a React frontend that lists posts fetched from the Django API.

children.title: Components, Props, and Composition
children.description: React UIs are trees of components. Props are immutable inputs passed from parent to child; composition lets you build complex UIs from small, focused pieces.

children.title: State Management with useState and useEffect
children.description: useState gives components local mutable state; useEffect synchronises components with external systems like APIs. Understanding the dependency array prevents stale closures.

resources.label: React Docs — State: A Component's Memory

children.title: Client-Side Routing with React Router
children.description: React Router maps URL paths to components without full page reloads. BrowserRouter, Routes, and Route form the core; useNavigate and useParams handle programmatic navigation.

children.title: Controlled Inputs and Form Handling
children.description: Controlled components keep form values in React state, making validation and submission straightforward. Uncontrolled inputs via useRef are useful for file inputs and third-party integrations.

resources.label: React Docs — Reacting to Input with State

children.title: Fetching API Data with useEffect
children.description: Calling fetch or Axios inside useEffect is the baseline pattern for loading remote data. Always handle loading and error states to provide a good user experience.

resources.label: React Docs — You Might Not Need an Effect

children.title: Scaffolding with Vite or Create React App
children.description: Vite provides near-instant dev server startup and HMR via ES modules. It is the recommended choice for new projects over the older Create React App.

children.title: Organising a React Codebase
children.description: Group files by feature rather than type for scalability. Co-locate component, styles, and tests. Use an index.js barrel file per feature to simplify imports.

children.title: React DevTools Browser Extension
children.description: React DevTools lets you inspect the component tree, view props and state, and profile render performance. It is essential for debugging complex component hierarchies.

children.title: ESLint and Prettier for React Projects
children.description: eslint-plugin-react-hooks enforces the Rules of Hooks and detects missing dependencies. Prettier provides opinionated formatting and integrates with most editors.

django-react.title: Connecting React and Django
django-react.description: This layer wires the React client and Django API together over HTTP. Integrating the two halves produces a working full stack application.
django-react.challenge: Wire the React app to create, edit, and delete posts through the Django API.

children.title: CORS Configuration in Django
children.description: Browsers block cross-origin requests by default. Install django-cors-headers and add CorsMiddleware to allow your React dev server's origin to reach the Django API.

children.title: CSRF Tokens and API Safety
children.description: When using session auth, Django requires a CSRF token on unsafe methods. DRF's SessionAuthentication enforces this; JWT-based APIs can disable it for stateless clients.

children.title: Loading and Error State Patterns
children.description: Model async data with three states: loading, success, and error. Use useState flags or a unified status enum to drive conditional rendering and spinner/error components.

resources.label: React Docs — Updating State Based on Previous State

children.title: Posting and Updating Data to APIs
children.description: For POST, PUT, and PATCH requests set Content-Type: application/json and send a JSON body. On success, either refetch the list or merge the response into local state.

children.title: Client-Side Pagination with APIs
children.description: DRF pagination metadata (count, next, previous) lets the frontend render page controls. Track the current page in state and fetch the corresponding URL on navigation.

children.title: Creating an Axios Instance with Base URL
children.description: Centralise API configuration by creating an Axios instance with baseURL and default headers. Export it from a single api.js file imported across your React features.

children.title: Axios Interceptors for Error Handling
children.description: Request interceptors can attach auth headers automatically; response interceptors can centralise error handling and token refresh logic before errors reach components.

children.title: Environment Variables in Vite and Django
children.description: Vite exposes variables prefixed with VITE_ via import.meta.env. Django reads from os.environ or a .env file via python-decouple. Never commit .env files to git.

children.title: Proxy Configuration for Local Development
children.description: Vite's server.proxy setting can forward /api requests to Django during development, eliminating CORS issues entirely in the local environment.

django-react.title: Authentication with Tokens
django-react.description: This layer adds secure login and protected endpoints across the stack. Authentication lets users own and manage their own content.
django-react.challenge: Add JWT login so users can manage only their own posts on both sides.

children.title: Token vs JWT Authentication
children.description: DRF's built-in token auth stores tokens in the database; JWTs are self-contained and stateless. Simple JWT is the most popular library for JWT auth with DRF.

children.title: Login and Registration API Endpoints
children.description: A login endpoint validates credentials and returns a token. Registration creates a user and optionally returns a token for immediate login. Use DRF serializers for validation.

children.title: Protecting API Views with Permissions
children.description: Set DEFAULT_AUTHENTICATION_CLASSES and DEFAULT_PERMISSION_CLASSES in DRF settings to enforce auth globally. Override per view with authentication_classes and permission_classes attributes.

children.title: Access Tokens, Refresh Tokens, and Rotation
children.description: Short-lived access tokens reduce exposure if stolen. Refresh tokens exchange for new access tokens; Simple JWT supports rotating refresh tokens for added security.

children.title: Logout and Token Blacklisting
children.description: JWTs cannot be invalidated until expiry without a blacklist. Simple JWT's blacklist app stores invalidated refresh tokens in the database to enable true logout.

children.title: Storing Tokens: localStorage vs Cookies
children.description: localStorage is simple but vulnerable to XSS. HttpOnly cookies protect tokens from JavaScript but require CSRF handling. Choose based on your threat model.

children.title: Auth Context and useContext Hook
children.description: Wrap the app in an AuthContext provider that stores the current user and token. Components consume the context with useContext to conditionally render or redirect.

resources.label: React Docs — Passing Data Deeply with Context

children.title: Protected Route Component Pattern
children.description: Wrap private routes in a component that checks auth state and redirects to /login if unauthenticated. React Router v6's Outlet makes nested protected layouts clean.

children.title: Axios Interceptor for Token Refresh
children.description: An Axios response interceptor catches 401 errors, calls the refresh endpoint, updates stored tokens, and retries the original request transparently to the calling code.

django-react.title: Advanced Django
django-react.description: This layer covers performance, background tasks, and advanced backend features. These skills prepare a Django app to handle real production workloads.
django-react.challenge: Add image uploads and a background email task when a new post is published.

children.title: select_related and prefetch_related
children.description: select_related reduces N+1 queries for ForeignKey/OneToOne by using SQL JOINs. prefetch_related uses separate queries with Python-side joining, suited for ManyToMany and reverse FK.

children.title: Django Signals for Decoupled Logic
children.description: Signals like post_save and pre_delete let you trigger side effects when models change without coupling the model to that logic. Use sparingly to avoid hidden control flow.

children.title: Custom Management Commands
children.description: Subclass BaseCommand in a management/commands/ directory to add manage.py commands. Useful for data migrations, scheduled jobs, and admin utilities.

children.title: Celery Task Queue Architecture
children.description: Celery offloads time-consuming work (emails, image processing) to background workers. Tasks are functions decorated with @shared_task and dispatched via a message broker.

resources.label: Celery Docs — First Steps with Django

children.title: File and Media Upload Handling
children.description: Django's FileField and ImageField manage uploads via MEDIA_ROOT and MEDIA_URL. Use django-storages for production uploads to S3 or other cloud storage.

children.title: Configuring Django Cache with Redis
children.description: Set CACHES in settings.py to use django-redis as the backend. Cache expensive query results or rendered fragments with cache.set() and cache.get().

children.title: Redis Data Structures and Use Cases
children.description: Redis lists and sorted sets are ideal for leaderboards and queues. Strings with expiry work for rate limiting and session storage alongside Django's cache framework.

children.title: Celery Beat for Periodic Tasks
children.description: Celery Beat is a scheduler that dispatches tasks on a cron or interval schedule. Store the schedule in the database with django-celery-beat for dynamic configuration.

children.title: Monitoring Celery with Flower
children.description: Flower is a real-time web dashboard for Celery that shows active tasks, worker status, and task history. Run it as a separate process alongside your workers.

django-react.title: Advanced React State and UX
django-react.description: This layer improves the frontend with caching, data fetching libraries, and polished UX. Mature client patterns make the app feel fast and reliable.
django-react.challenge: Add React Query caching and optimistic post updates to the frontend.

children.title: React Query for Server State
children.description: TanStack Query separates server state from UI state, handling caching, background refetching, and loading/error states automatically. useQuery and useMutation are its core hooks.

children.title: Cache Invalidation and Stale-While-Revalidate
children.description: Query invalidation with queryClient.invalidateQueries refetches data after mutations. The staleTime option controls how long cached data is considered fresh before background refetching.

children.title: Optimistic Updates for Instant Feedback
children.description: Optimistic updates apply changes to the cache immediately and roll back on error. This makes mutations feel instant and improves perceived performance on slow connections.

children.title: Global State with Zustand or Context
children.description: For UI state shared across routes (modals, theme, auth), lightweight stores like Zustand avoid Redux boilerplate. Keep global state minimal; prefer React Query for server data.

children.title: Schema Validation with Zod and React Hook Form
children.description: React Hook Form minimises re-renders with uncontrolled inputs and a subscription model. Zod provides TypeScript-first schema validation that integrates via the @hookform/resolvers package.

children.title: ARIA Roles and Keyboard Navigation
children.description: Use semantic HTML first; add ARIA roles only when necessary. Ensure interactive components are keyboard-accessible with visible focus styles and correct tab order.

children.title: Accessible Component Libraries
children.description: Headless UI libraries like Radix UI and Reach UI provide fully accessible, unstyled primitives for modals, dropdowns, and tooltips. Style them with Tailwind or CSS modules.

children.title: Code Splitting with React.lazy and Suspense
children.description: React.lazy() and Suspense enable route-level code splitting, reducing initial bundle size. Vite automatically produces separate chunks for lazy-loaded components.

children.title: useMemo and useCallback for Performance
children.description: useMemo caches expensive computed values; useCallback stabilises function references to prevent unnecessary child re-renders. Profile first with React DevTools before optimising.

django-react.title: Testing and Deployment
django-react.description: This layer covers testing the stack and deploying it to production. Tests and a real deployment pipeline turn the project into a maintainable product.
django-react.challenge: Add tests, containerize the app, and deploy the Django and React blog to the cloud.

children.title: Django Testing with pytest and pytest-django
children.description: pytest-django provides fixtures like db and client for testing Django views, models, and APIs. Use APIClient for DRF endpoint tests with authentication helpers.

children.title: React Component Testing with Vitest
children.description: Vitest runs in the same Vite pipeline as your app for fast unit and component tests. Use @testing-library/react to render components and assert on what the user sees.

children.title: Mocking API Calls with MSW
children.description: Mock Service Worker intercepts network requests at the service worker level so tests and Storybook use the same mocks as production code without modifying application code.

children.title: Dockerizing Django and React
children.description: Write a multi-stage Dockerfile for the React build and a separate one for Django/Gunicorn. Use docker-compose to orchestrate the app, database, Redis, and Nginx together.

children.title: Environment Variables and Secrets in Docker
children.description: Pass secrets via environment variables in docker-compose.yml or .env files. For production, use Docker secrets or a secrets manager like AWS Secrets Manager.

children.title: Gunicorn WSGI Server Configuration
children.description: Gunicorn is the standard WSGI server for Django in production. Configure worker count (2×CPU+1), worker class, and timeout. Run it behind Nginx, never exposed directly.

children.title: Nginx as Reverse Proxy and Static Server
children.description: Nginx serves collected static files and React build output directly, and proxies /api requests to Gunicorn. This avoids Python touching static file requests entirely.

children.title: collectstatic and WhiteNoise for Static Files
children.description: Run manage.py collectstatic before deployment to gather all static files. WhiteNoise lets Django serve compressed static files without a dedicated CDN, simplifying deployment.

children.title: Deploying to a Cloud Platform
children.description: Platforms like Railway, Render, and Fly.io can deploy a Docker Compose stack with minimal configuration. For AWS, use ECS with RDS and ElastiCache for a scalable setup.

rails-dev.title: Ruby Fundamentals
rails-dev.description: This layer builds core Ruby skills that the Rails framework depends on. Comfort with Ruby idioms makes Rails feel natural and productive.
rails-dev.challenge: Write a Ruby program that manages a small library catalog using classes.

children.title: Variables, methods, and blocks in Ruby
children.description: Ruby uses dynamic typing with local, instance, class, and global variables. Blocks are anonymous closures passed to methods using do...end or curly braces.

children.title: Arrays, hashes, and Enumerable module
children.description: Ruby arrays and hashes are first-class objects with rich APIs. The Enumerable module provides map, select, reduce, and dozens of iteration helpers.

children.title: Symbols, ranges, and exception handling
children.description: Symbols are immutable identifiers commonly used as hash keys. Ranges represent sequences and Ruby uses begin/rescue/ensure for structured exception handling.

children.title: Classes, modules, and mixins pattern
children.description: Ruby classes support single inheritance while modules enable mixin behavior, allowing shared methods across unrelated class hierarchies without duplication.

children.title: Gems and Bundler dependency management
children.description: RubyGems is the package manager for Ruby libraries. Bundler manages gem dependencies via a Gemfile, ensuring consistent environments across machines.

children.title: Installing Ruby with rbenv or RVM
children.description: rbenv and RVM are version managers that let you install and switch between multiple Ruby versions per project, preventing conflicts between applications.

children.title: IRB interactive Ruby shell usage
children.description: IRB (Interactive Ruby) is the built-in REPL for experimenting with Ruby expressions, testing methods, and debugging code snippets quickly.

children.title: Writing idiomatic Ruby with style guides
children.description: The RuboCop style guide enforces community-standard Ruby idioms. Following conventions like method naming, block usage, and guard clauses improves readability.

children.title: Debugging Ruby code with Pry and byebug
children.description: Pry is a powerful IRB alternative with syntax highlighting and introspection. byebug provides step-through debugging with breakpoints in any Ruby application.

rails-dev.title: Rails Basics and MVC
rails-dev.description: This layer introduces Rails conventions, the MVC pattern, and the request cycle. Understanding Rails structure is the foundation for everything else.
rails-dev.challenge: Generate a Rails app that lists and shows articles using controllers and views.

children.title: Model-View-Controller pattern in Rails
children.description: Rails strictly enforces MVC separation: models handle data logic, controllers handle request flow, and views render HTML responses. This structure makes large codebases maintainable.

resources.label: Rails Guides: Getting Started with Rails

children.title: Convention over configuration Rails principle
children.description: Rails minimizes configuration by assuming sensible defaults. Naming conventions for files, classes, and database tables eliminate repetitive boilerplate setup code.

children.title: Rails project directory structure overview
children.description: A Rails app is organized into app/, config/, db/, and lib/ directories. Understanding this layout helps you locate models, routes, views, and configuration quickly.

children.title: Routes, controllers, and resourceful routing
children.description: config/routes.rb maps HTTP verbs and URLs to controller actions. RESTful resources routing generates the seven standard actions automatically with one declaration.

resources.label: Rails Guides: Routing from the Outside In

children.title: Rails generators and scaffolding workflow
children.description: Rails generators automate creating models, controllers, views, and migrations. Scaffolding produces a full CRUD interface so you can prototype features rapidly.

children.title: Rails console for live app exploration
children.description: bin/rails console opens an IRB session with your full Rails environment loaded, allowing you to query the database, test methods, and inspect objects interactively.

children.title: Development server and request logging
children.description: bin/rails server starts the Puma web server locally. Rails logs every request with SQL queries and timing to help you trace issues during development.

children.title: ERB templates and layout inheritance
children.description: Rails uses ERB (Embedded Ruby) for HTML templates. Layouts define the shared structure and individual view templates are yielded inside them per action.

children.title: Flash messages and redirect patterns
children.description: Flash is a session-based mechanism for passing short messages between requests, typically used to show success or error notices after form submissions and redirects.

rails-dev.title: Active Record and Databases
rails-dev.description: This layer teaches modeling data and querying it with Active Record. The ORM is how Rails apps persist and relate their data.
rails-dev.challenge: Model articles and comments in PostgreSQL with validations and associations.

children.title: Models, migrations, and schema evolution
children.description: Active Record models map to database tables automatically by name convention. Migrations are Ruby classes that incrementally alter the schema in a version-controlled way.

children.title: Active Record associations and relationships
children.description: belongs_to, has_many, has_one, and has_many :through declare relationships between models. Rails auto-generates join queries and association helper methods.

children.title: Model validations for data integrity
children.description: Active Record validations run before saving records, ensuring data meets requirements like presence, uniqueness, format, and custom conditions.

children.title: Query interface and named scopes
children.description: Active Record provides a chainable query DSL: where, order, limit, joins, and includes. Named scopes encapsulate reusable query conditions as class methods.

resources.label: Rails Guides: Active Record Query Interface

children.title: CRUD operations with Active Record
children.description: Active Record provides create, read, update, and delete operations through methods like new, save, find, update, and destroy with automatic SQL generation.

resources.label: Rails Guides: CRUD with Active Record

children.title: Switching SQLite to PostgreSQL in Rails
children.description: Production Rails apps typically use PostgreSQL for its robustness and features. Switching requires updating the Gemfile, database.yml, and providing a connection string.

children.title: Database seeds and test data setup
children.description: db/seeds.rb provides a standard location for populating the database with initial or sample data using Active Record create calls.

children.title: Database console and schema inspection
children.description: bin/rails dbconsole opens the native database CLI. db/schema.rb is the authoritative source for current table structure generated from migrations.

children.title: Using PgHero and Bullet for query analysis
children.description: Bullet detects N+1 queries and missing eager loading during development. PgHero provides a web dashboard for inspecting PostgreSQL query performance and index usage.

rails-dev.title: Views, Forms, and Assets
rails-dev.description: This layer covers building the server-rendered frontend with views, forms, and the asset pipeline. Polished views turn a working backend into a usable product.
rails-dev.challenge: Add forms to create and edit articles with validation feedback and styled layouts.

children.title: ERB syntax and view rendering lifecycle
children.description: ERB embeds Ruby expressions inside HTML using <%= %> and <% %>. Rails renders the action template first, then wraps it in the application layout via yield.

children.title: Partials and view helpers for reuse
children.description: Partials are reusable view fragments prefixed with underscore. Helper methods defined in app/helpers/ extract view logic into testable Ruby methods.

children.title: Flash messages and user feedback patterns
children.description: Flash notices and alerts provide one-request messages after redirects. Rendering them in the layout ensures they appear after any form submission.

children.title: Form helpers and strong parameters security
children.description: form_with generates secure HTML forms tied to model objects. Strong parameters require explicit permit declarations to prevent mass assignment vulnerabilities.

resources.label: Rails Guides: Action View Form Helpers

children.title: Nested forms and complex data submission
children.description: accepts_nested_attributes_for allows forms to create or update associated models in one submission. fields_for renders nested form fields for the association.

children.title: Rails asset pipeline with Sprockets
children.description: The asset pipeline concatenates, minifies, and fingerprints CSS, JavaScript, and image files. Sprockets handles dependency resolution and cache-busting digests.

children.title: Importing CSS with cssbundling-rails
children.description: cssbundling-rails integrates Sass, Tailwind CSS, or PostCSS into the asset pipeline. It runs a Node.js build step and outputs to app/assets/builds.

children.title: importmap-rails for JavaScript modules
children.description: importmap-rails uses browser-native import maps to load JavaScript modules without a bundler. It pins gems and npm packages directly to CDN or local paths.

children.title: jsbundling-rails with esbuild or webpack
children.description: jsbundling-rails adds a JavaScript bundling step for projects needing npm packages. esbuild is the fastest option while webpack offers the most configuration.

rails-dev.title: Authentication and Authorization
rails-dev.description: This layer adds user accounts and access control to the application. Authentication lets users own and manage their own content securely.
topics: User signup and login with Devise
rails-dev.challenge: Add login so users can only edit and delete the articles they authored.

children.title: Sessions, cookies, and stateful authentication
children.description: Rails sessions store a signed, encrypted cookie on the client. The session hash persists data between requests, enabling login state without re-authentication each time.

children.title: Password hashing with bcrypt security
children.description: bcrypt is an adaptive hashing algorithm resistant to brute-force attacks. Rails has_secure_password uses bcrypt to hash passwords automatically before storage.

children.title: Authorization roles and permissions design
children.description: Authorization determines what authenticated users can do. Role-based access control assigns permissions to roles like admin and user, checked before sensitive actions.

children.title: User signup, login, and logout flow
children.description: A typical Rails auth flow creates a user on signup, verifies credentials on login, stores the user ID in session, and destroys the session on logout.

children.title: Protecting controller actions with before_action
children.description: before_action filters run before controller actions to enforce authentication. Redirecting unauthenticated users and halting the action prevents unauthorized access.

children.title: Devise gem for full-featured authentication
children.description: Devise is a modular authentication solution providing sign-up, sign-in, password reset, email confirmation, and session timeout out of the box.

children.title: OmniAuth for third-party OAuth providers
children.description: OmniAuth provides a standardized interface for OAuth2 with providers like Google, GitHub, and Facebook. Devise integrates via the devise-omniauth strategy.

children.title: Pundit policy objects for authorization
children.description: Pundit uses plain Ruby policy classes to define authorization rules per model. Controllers call authorize to check policies, keeping logic out of controllers.

children.title: CanCanCan ability-based authorization
children.description: CanCanCan defines all permissions in a single Ability class using can and cannot rules. load_and_authorize_resource auto-loads and checks permissions for REST actions.

rails-dev.title: Interactivity with Hotwire
rails-dev.description: This layer brings modern interactivity to Rails without a heavy JavaScript framework. Hotwire is the Rails-native way to build fast, reactive interfaces.
rails-dev.challenge: Use Turbo and Stimulus to add inline comment posting without full page reloads.

children.title: Turbo Drive for fast page navigation
children.description: Turbo Drive intercepts link clicks and form submissions, replacing only the page body over fetch requests. This eliminates full page reloads for a SPA-like feel.

children.title: Turbo Frames for partial page updates
children.description: Turbo Frames wrap page sections so only that section updates on navigation. This enables tab switching, inline editing, and pagination without JavaScript.

children.title: Turbo Streams for server-pushed DOM updates
children.description: Turbo Streams deliver CRUD actions (append, prepend, replace, remove) from the server via HTTP responses or WebSocket broadcasts, updating multiple page targets.

children.title: Stimulus controllers and data attributes
children.description: Stimulus connects JavaScript controller classes to HTML elements via data-controller attributes. It adds behavior progressively without replacing server-rendered HTML.

children.title: Real-time features with Action Cable WebSockets
children.description: Action Cable integrates WebSockets into Rails using channels. Turbo Streams over Action Cable enables live broadcasting of DOM updates to multiple connected clients.

children.title: Installing Hotwire with turbo-rails gem
children.description: turbo-rails integrates the Turbo JavaScript library into the Rails asset pipeline and provides view helpers, tag builders, and Broadcastable model concern.

children.title: stimulus-rails gem and controller generation
children.description: stimulus-rails adds Stimulus to the importmap or bundler setup and provides rails generate stimulus to scaffold controller files in app/javascript/controllers.

children.title: Inline editing with Turbo Frames workflow
children.description: Replace static content with an edit form in-place using a Turbo Frame wrapping both the display and edit states. The frame updates only the wrapped area on response.

children.title: Broadcasting live updates from Active Record
children.description: The Broadcastable concern mixes into models to broadcast Turbo Stream messages on create, update, and destroy, pushing changes to all subscribed clients automatically.

resources.label: Rails Guides: Action Cable with Turbo

rails-dev.title: Background Jobs and Mailers
rails-dev.description: This layer covers running work in the background and sending email. Offloading slow tasks keeps the app responsive and reliable.
rails-dev.challenge: Send a welcome email through a background job when a user signs up.

children.title: Active Job abstraction and adapter pattern
children.description: Active Job provides a unified interface for background job processing. You define jobs in app/jobs/ and swap backend adapters (Sidekiq, Resque) without changing job code.

children.title: Job retries, error handling, and dead queues
children.description: Active Job supports automatic retries with exponential backoff via retry_on. discard_on drops jobs that fail with specific errors that should not be retried.

children.title: Action Mailer email composition and delivery
children.description: Action Mailer creates mailer classes that generate HTML and plain-text emails. deliver_later enqueues email delivery as a background job through Active Job.

children.title: Email templates HTML and text multipart
children.description: Mailer views live in app/views/mailer_name/ and Rails renders both HTML and plain text versions. Multipart emails ensure compatibility with all email clients.

children.title: Mailer previews for development email testing
children.description: Action Mailer previews let you render emails in the browser at /rails/mailers without actually sending them, speeding up email template iteration.

children.title: Sidekiq worker processes and Redis backend
children.description: Sidekiq uses Redis as a job queue store and runs multi-threaded worker processes. It processes significantly more jobs per second than thread-per-process alternatives.

children.title: Sidekiq Web UI for job monitoring
children.description: Sidekiq ships with a Rack-based web interface showing queue depths, processed counts, failed jobs, and retry queues. Mount it in routes.rb behind authentication.

children.title: Scheduling recurring jobs with sidekiq-cron
children.description: sidekiq-cron adds a cron-style scheduler to Sidekiq using a YAML schedule file. It integrates with the Sidekiq Web UI to show and manage scheduled jobs.

children.title: Email delivery with SendGrid and Postmark
children.description: Configure action_mailer.delivery_method with SMTP credentials from SendGrid or Postmark. Use environment variables to keep credentials out of the repository.

rails-dev.title: Building APIs with Rails
rails-dev.description: This layer covers exposing your app as a JSON API for clients and integrations. API mode broadens what your Rails backend can power.
rails-dev.challenge: Add a versioned JSON API exposing articles for an external client.

children.title: API-only Rails application architecture
children.description: rails new --api creates a slimmed-down Rails stack removing view layers and session middleware. Controllers inherit from ActionController::API for lightweight JSON responses.

resources.label: Rails Guides: Using Rails for API-only Apps

children.title: API versioning strategies and URL design
children.description: Versioning via URL namespaces (/api/v1/) or Accept headers prevents breaking changes for existing clients. Namespaced controllers in app/controllers/api/v1/ isolate versions.

children.title: Token-based authentication for API endpoints
children.description: API token auth stores a secure random token on the user and validates it from the Authorization header. JWT (JSON Web Tokens) is a stateless alternative.

children.title: CORS configuration and rate limiting
children.description: rack-cors adds CORS headers to allow browser requests from other origins. rack-attack provides IP-based rate limiting, throttling, and blocklisting middleware.

children.title: API documentation with Swagger and rswag
children.description: rswag generates OpenAPI (Swagger) documentation from RSpec request specs. The Swagger UI lets consumers explore and test API endpoints interactively.

children.title: Serializing responses with jsonapi-serializer
children.description: jsonapi-serializer generates fast JSON:API-compliant responses from Active Record objects. Attributes, relationships, and meta fields are declared in serializer classes.

children.title: Active Model Serializers for custom JSON shapes
children.description: ActiveModelSerializers allows custom JSON representations via adapter patterns. It integrates with Active Record associations for nested object serialization.

children.title: Testing APIs with request specs and Postman
children.description: RSpec request specs test full HTTP stack including routing, controllers, and serializers. Postman collections document and manually test API endpoints during development.

children.title: GraphQL APIs with graphql-ruby gem
children.description: graphql-ruby implements a GraphQL server in Rails with type-safe schema definitions, query resolution, and mutation support for flexible API data fetching.

rails-dev.title: Performance and Advanced Patterns
rails-dev.description: This layer covers optimizing queries, caching, and structuring larger Rails apps. These practices keep a growing codebase fast and maintainable.
topics: N plus one queries and eager loading
rails-dev.challenge: Profile the app, fix N plus one queries, and add caching to the article list.

children.title: N+1 queries and eager loading with includes
children.description: N+1 occurs when loading associations in a loop triggers one query per record. includes, preload, or eager_load fetches associations upfront in one or two queries.

children.title: Fragment and Russian doll caching strategies
children.description: Fragment caching stores rendered HTML partials in a cache store. Russian doll caching nests cache keys so parent caches auto-expire when children change.

children.title: Database indexing for query performance
children.description: Adding database indexes to foreign keys and frequently queried columns reduces full table scans. add_index in migrations creates indexes; EXPLAIN ANALYZE measures impact.

children.title: Service objects for complex business logic
children.description: Service objects are plain Ruby classes encapsulating a single business operation. They keep controllers thin and models focused on data concerns, improving testability.

children.title: Concerns and modules for code reuse
children.description: ActiveSupport::Concern provides a clean way to extract shared model or controller behavior into modules. Concerns live in app/models/concerns/ and app/controllers/concerns/.

children.title: Redis cache store configuration in Rails
children.description: Configure config.cache_store = :redis_cache_store with a Redis connection to get a fast, shared cache across multiple server instances with automatic expiration.

children.title: HTTP caching with ETags and Last-Modified
children.description: fresh_when and stale? helpers set ETag and Last-Modified headers so browsers can cache responses and make conditional requests, reducing server load.

children.title: Application performance monitoring with Scout APM
children.description: Scout APM instruments Rails apps to trace slow requests, N+1 queries, and memory bloat. It provides transaction traces and endpoint performance breakdowns.

children.title: rack-mini-profiler for request-level profiling
children.description: rack-mini-profiler injects a speed badge into pages showing SQL query counts, rendering time, and Ruby object allocations to identify bottlenecks in development.

rails-dev.title: Testing and Deployment
rails-dev.description: This layer covers automated testing and deploying Rails to production. A solid test suite and deploy pipeline make the app reliable and maintainable.
topics: Model and request specs with RSpec
rails-dev.challenge: Add an RSpec suite and deploy the finished Rails blog to a cloud platform.

children.title: RSpec model and request spec patterns
children.description: Model specs test validations, associations, and business logic in isolation. Request specs test the full HTTP stack end-to-end, verifying response codes and JSON bodies.

children.title: System tests with Capybara and browser drivers
children.description: Capybara drives a real browser (via Selenium or Cuprite) to test full user workflows. System specs verify JavaScript interactions, form submissions, and Turbo navigation.

children.title: FactoryBot for test data factories
children.description: FactoryBot replaces brittle fixtures with factories that build model instances with sensible defaults and trait overrides, making test setup readable and flexible.

children.title: GitHub Actions CI pipeline for Rails
children.description: A GitHub Actions workflow installs Ruby, sets up a PostgreSQL service container, runs db:schema:load, and executes the full test suite on every pull request.

children.title: Code coverage with SimpleCov reporting
children.description: SimpleCov generates line-by-line coverage reports after the test suite runs. Set a minimum coverage threshold to prevent merging code that reduces test coverage.

children.title: Dockerfile for Rails production containers
children.description: Rails 7+ includes a generated Dockerfile using a multi-stage build with a slim Ruby image. The production image compiles assets, runs migrations, and starts Puma.

children.title: Docker Compose for local development stack
children.description: Docker Compose orchestrates Rails, PostgreSQL, and Redis containers locally. Service definitions in compose.yml link containers and manage environment variables.

children.title: Deploying Rails to Render or Railway
children.description: Render and Railway offer zero-config Rails deployments from a GitHub repo. They detect the Dockerfile or buildpacks, provision PostgreSQL, and run release commands.

children.title: Kamal for zero-downtime Rails deployment
children.description: Kamal (formerly MRSK) is the official Rails deployment tool. It builds Docker images, pushes to a registry, and rolls out to servers with health-checked zero-downtime deploys.

next-fullstack.title: TypeScript and React Foundations
next-fullstack.description: This layer grounds you in TypeScript and React, the core of Next.js development. These skills underpin every feature you build in the framework.
next-fullstack.challenge: Build a typed React component library with a button, card, and input.

children.title: Types, interfaces, and type aliases
children.description: TypeScript lets you annotate variables and function signatures with static types. Understanding the difference between type aliases and interfaces is key to writing maintainable code.

children.title: Generics and utility types in TypeScript
children.description: Generics allow you to write reusable, type-safe functions and components. Built-in utility types like Partial, Pick, and Omit reduce repetitive type definitions.

children.title: Union, intersection, and narrowing types
children.description: Union types model values that can be one of several types, while type narrowing via guards lets TypeScript infer the correct shape at runtime. These patterns are essential for robust data handling.

children.title: Functional components, props, and composition
children.description: React models UI as a tree of composable functions that receive props and return JSX. Understanding component composition patterns prevents prop drilling and keeps code modular.

children.title: State management with hooks and context
children.description: useState, useReducer, and useContext are the foundational hooks for local and shared state. Knowing when to lift state versus use context keeps components predictable.

children.title: Effect hook and lifecycle understanding
children.description: useEffect synchronizes a component with external systems and replaces class lifecycle methods. Correctly specifying the dependency array prevents stale closures and infinite loops.

children.title: Configure tsconfig for a Next.js project
children.description: Next.js ships with a sensible tsconfig.json but you should understand strict mode, path aliases, and module resolution options. Correct configuration prevents hard-to-debug type errors.

children.title: Typing React event handlers and form inputs
children.description: React events wrap native browser events and have their own generic types. Typing onChange, onClick, and form submit handlers prevents runtime errors in UI interactions.

children.title: Destructuring, spread, and optional chaining
children.description: ES2020+ syntax like optional chaining (?.) and nullish coalescing (??) reduces boilerplate null checks. These patterns appear constantly in React props and API response handling.

children.title: Async/await and Promise handling patterns
children.description: Nearly all data-fetching in Next.js uses async/await. Understanding Promise.all, error handling with try/catch, and avoiding sequential awaits in loops makes data fetching efficient.

children.title: ES modules, imports, and barrel files
children.description: Next.js uses ES module syntax throughout. Organizing exports via index barrel files and understanding named vs default exports keeps large codebases navigable.

next-fullstack.title: Next.js App Router and Routing
next-fullstack.description: This layer covers routing, layouts, and navigation with the Next.js App Router. The router defines how pages and shared UI are structured.
next-fullstack.challenge: Scaffold a dashboard app with nested layouts and dynamic project routes.

children.title: File-system routing and the app directory
children.description: The Next.js App Router maps the file system directly to URL routes. Every folder with a page.tsx becomes a URL segment, making navigation structure instantly visible.

children.title: Nested layouts and shared UI segments
children.description: layout.tsx files wrap all children in a segment and persist across navigations without re-mounting. This is how persistent sidebars, headers, and auth wrappers are built.

children.title: Dynamic routes and catch-all segments
children.description: [param] folders create dynamic URL segments, while [...slug] catches all sub-paths. These patterns power product pages, blog posts, and any content-driven routing.

children.title: Route groups for layout organisation
children.description: Wrapping folders in (parentheses) creates route groups that do not affect the URL. This lets you apply different layouts to marketing pages versus authenticated dashboard routes.

children.title: loading.tsx and error.tsx boundary files
children.description: Placing loading.tsx and error.tsx alongside page.tsx automatically wraps that segment in Suspense and error boundaries. These files dramatically improve perceived performance and resilience.

children.title: Link component and programmatic navigation
children.description: The <Link> component prefetches routes and provides client-side navigation without a full page reload. useRouter from next/navigation enables imperative redirects after form submissions.

children.title: usePathname and active link highlighting
children.description: usePathname returns the current URL path so you can apply active styles to navigation links. This pattern is used in every sidebar and top navigation component.

children.title: Parallel routes and intercepting routes
children.description: Parallel routes render multiple pages in the same layout simultaneously (e.g. modals), while intercepting routes show a different UI on navigation without leaving the page.

children.title: Metadata API for SEO and Open Graph
children.description: Exporting a metadata object or generateMetadata function from any page sets title, description, and OG tags. Proper metadata is critical for search ranking and social sharing.

children.title: generateStaticParams for static dynamic routes
children.description: generateStaticParams pre-renders dynamic route segments at build time, producing static HTML for all known paths. This is essential for blog, product, and documentation sites.

next-fullstack.title: Server Components and Rendering
next-fullstack.description: This layer explains server and client components and Next.js rendering strategies. Choosing the right rendering model is key to performance and correctness.
next-fullstack.challenge: Render the dashboard data on the server with streaming and Suspense boundaries.

children.title: Server vs client component boundary rules
children.description: Server Components render only on the server and can access databases directly, while Client Components run in the browser and handle interactivity. The boundary is set by the 'use client' directive.

children.title: Static vs dynamic rendering strategies
children.description: A route is statically rendered at build time unless it uses dynamic functions like cookies() or headers(). Understanding this trade-off lets you choose the right caching strategy per route.

children.title: Streaming and progressive page hydration
children.description: Streaming lets the server send HTML in chunks as data becomes available, so users see content faster. Wrapping slow data-fetching components in <Suspense> enables this progressive loading.

children.title: Next.js caching layers and how they interact
children.description: Next.js has four caching layers: Request Memoization, Data Cache, Full Route Cache, and Router Cache. Understanding each layer's scope prevents surprising stale data bugs.

children.title: Time-based and on-demand revalidation concepts
children.description: next: { revalidate } controls how often the Data Cache is refreshed, while revalidatePath and revalidateTag clear the cache on demand. These patterns enable a 'stale-while-revalidate' UX.

children.title: fetch in Server Components with caching options
children.description: The extended fetch API in Server Components accepts a cache or next option to control memoization and revalidation. Fetching data directly in server components eliminates extra API round-trips.

children.title: Parallel data fetching with Promise.all
children.description: Awaiting multiple independent fetches sequentially wastes time. Initiating them in parallel with Promise.all reduces total load time to the duration of the slowest fetch.

children.title: use client directive and client-side fetching
children.description: Adding 'use client' at the top of a file marks it as a Client Component. For client-side data fetching, TanStack Query provides caching, refetching, and loading state management.

children.title: Suspense boundaries for incremental page loading
children.description: Wrapping async Server Components in <Suspense fallback={<Skeleton />}> lets the rest of the page render immediately. This pattern transforms a blocked page into a progressively loaded experience.

children.title: Error boundaries to gracefully handle fetch errors
children.description: error.tsx files create React error boundaries that catch thrown errors in a segment and render a fallback UI. This prevents a single failed data fetch from crashing the whole page.

next-fullstack.title: Database with Prisma
next-fullstack.description: This layer covers modeling and querying data with Prisma. The database layer gives your Next.js app persistent, typed data.
next-fullstack.challenge: Model projects and tasks in PostgreSQL and query them from server components.

children.title: Defining models, fields, and scalar types
children.description: The Prisma schema is a single source of truth for your database structure. Models map to tables, and scalar types like String, Int, DateTime map to database column types.

children.title: Relations: one-to-many and many-to-many
children.description: Prisma uses relation fields and foreign key attributes to express database relationships. Understanding @relation, implicit join tables, and relation scalar fields is essential for any real data model.

children.title: Migrations workflow and schema evolution
children.description: prisma migrate dev generates SQL migrations from schema changes and applies them locally. Understanding migration history and how to handle data migrations prevents data loss during schema changes.

children.title: Filtering, sorting, and pagination with Prisma
children.description: Prisma Client provides type-safe where, orderBy, take, and skip options for every query. Cursor-based pagination is more efficient than offset for large datasets.

children.title: Nested writes and transactional operations
children.description: Prisma supports nested create/connect/update in a single query, which keeps related records consistent. For multi-step operations, $transaction ensures atomicity.

children.title: Install Prisma and configure database connection
children.description: Prisma setup requires the prisma CLI and @prisma/client packages, and a DATABASE_URL in .env. Connecting to PostgreSQL locally with Docker is the standard development workflow.

children.title: Prisma Studio for visual database inspection
children.description: prisma studio opens a browser-based GUI to browse and edit your database records. It is the fastest way to verify that your mutations and migrations are working correctly.

children.title: Seeding the database with realistic test data
children.description: A seed script populates your development database with consistent starting data. Prisma's seed configuration in package.json lets you run prisma db seed to reset state at any time.

children.title: Singleton Prisma Client pattern in Next.js
children.description: Hot reloading in development creates multiple Prisma Client instances, exhausting connection pools. The singleton pattern in lib/prisma.ts stores the client on the global object to prevent this.

children.title: Type-safe CRUD with generated Prisma types
children.description: Prisma Client generates TypeScript types from your schema, so every query result is fully typed. This eliminates entire categories of runtime errors when reading and writing data.

next-fullstack.title: Server Actions and Mutations
next-fullstack.description: This layer teaches mutating data with server actions and route handlers. Server actions let you write secure backend logic right alongside your UI.
next-fullstack.challenge: Add create, edit, and delete tasks using server actions with Zod validation.

children.title: What server actions are and how they execute
children.description: Server Actions are async functions marked with 'use server' that run on the server and can be called from client components or forms. They eliminate the need for separate API routes for form submissions.

children.title: Form submissions with the action prop
children.description: Passing a Server Action to the HTML form action attribute submits the form directly to the server without JavaScript. This enables progressive enhancement and works even before hydration.

children.title: Revalidation and cache invalidation after mutations
children.description: After a successful mutation, calling revalidatePath or revalidateTag clears the Next.js cache for the affected routes. Without this step, the UI will show stale data after writes.

children.title: Schema validation with Zod in server actions
children.description: Parsing incoming form data with a Zod schema validates types and shapes before they reach your database. Zod provides detailed error messages that can be returned to the client.

children.title: Returning errors and success states from actions
children.description: Server Actions can return objects with error or success fields rather than throwing, giving the client UI something meaningful to display. useActionState connects this return value to form state.

resources.label: Next.js – Error Handling in Server Actions

children.title: Route handlers as REST API endpoints
children.description: route.ts files in the app directory export named HTTP method handlers (GET, POST, etc.) to create API endpoints. These are used when you need a public API consumed by external clients.

children.title: Reading request body and query parameters
children.description: Route handlers receive a NextRequest object from which you can read JSON body, search params, and headers. Always validate external input before processing.

children.title: useFormStatus for pending button states
children.description: React's useFormStatus hook reads the pending state of the nearest form, letting you disable submit buttons and show spinners without manual state management.

children.title: useOptimistic for instant UI feedback
children.description: useOptimistic immediately applies a predicted UI update before the server responds, then reconciles the real data. This pattern makes mutations feel instantaneous to users.

children.title: Zod form validation and client-side error display
children.description: Running a Zod safeParse on form data before submission catches obvious errors immediately. Displaying field-level Zod error messages guides users to fix their input without a server round-trip.

next-fullstack.title: Authentication
next-fullstack.description: This layer adds login, sessions, and protected routes to the app. Authentication enables per-user data and secure access control.
topics: Reading the session on the server
next-fullstack.challenge: Add authentication so each user sees and manages only their own projects.

children.title: OAuth flow and credential-based authentication
children.description: OAuth delegates authentication to a trusted provider like Google or GitHub, while credentials providers handle username/password directly. Understanding both flows lets you offer users multiple sign-in options.

children.title: Sessions vs JWT token strategies
children.description: Session strategy stores a reference in a server-side store and sends a session ID cookie, while JWT encodes the full session in a signed token. Each has different security and scalability trade-offs.

children.title: Protecting routes with middleware-based guards
children.description: Next.js middleware runs at the edge before a request reaches any page, making it the ideal place to redirect unauthenticated users. This centralizes auth protection without duplicating checks per page.

children.title: Prisma adapter for persisting sessions to database
children.description: The Prisma adapter stores users, sessions, and accounts in your database, enabling user lookup and multi-device session management. It requires specific Prisma model definitions in your schema.

children.title: Reading session server-side and client-side
children.description: auth() returns the session in Server Components and Server Actions, while useSession provides it in Client Components. Knowing which to use avoids unnecessary client-side session fetches.

children.title: Install and configure Auth.js with Next.js
children.description: Auth.js (formerly NextAuth.js v5) is configured in a single auth.ts file that exports auth, signIn, and signOut. The route handler at app/api/auth/[...nextauth] handles all OAuth callbacks.

children.title: Environment variables for OAuth credentials
children.description: Each OAuth provider requires a client ID and secret obtained from their developer console. AUTH_SECRET must also be set as a random string for signing tokens.

children.title: Protecting server actions by checking session
children.description: Calling auth() at the top of a Server Action and throwing if the session is null prevents unauthenticated mutations. Never rely solely on UI-level hiding to protect sensitive operations.

children.title: Role-based access control with session callbacks
children.description: The jwt and session callbacks in Auth.js let you attach custom fields like role to the session token. Checking session.user.role in middleware or server actions enforces permission levels.

children.title: Configuring sign-in and sign-out UI flows
children.description: Auth.js provides default sign-in pages, but custom pages are configured via the pages option in auth.ts. Combining signIn/signOut server actions with redirect keeps auth UX seamless.

next-fullstack.title: Styling and UI
next-fullstack.description: This layer covers building an accessible, responsive interface with Tailwind and component libraries. A clean UI makes the full stack app feel professional.
next-fullstack.challenge: Build a polished, responsive dashboard UI with reusable styled components.

children.title: Utility-first CSS and the Tailwind mental model
children.description: Tailwind replaces custom CSS with composable utility classes applied directly in HTML. This approach eliminates naming fatigue and keeps styles co-located with the markup they affect.

children.title: Responsive design with Tailwind breakpoints
children.description: Tailwind uses mobile-first breakpoint prefixes (sm:, md:, lg:) that apply styles above a minimum width. This makes responsive layouts readable and avoids media query boilerplate.

children.title: Dark mode with Tailwind class strategy
children.description: Setting darkMode: "class" in tailwind.config lets you toggle dark styles by adding a dark class to the html element. This enables user-controlled theme switching without CSS custom properties.

children.title: Semantic HTML and ARIA patterns for accessibility
children.description: Screen readers and assistive technology rely on semantic elements and ARIA attributes to understand UI structure. Building accessible components from the start is easier than retrofitting them.

children.title: Design tokens and consistent spacing scales
children.description: Tailwind's default spacing scale (4, 8, 12, 16...) creates visual consistency when used systematically. Extending the config with custom design tokens keeps branding consistent across a project.

resources.label: Tailwind CSS – Customizing the Design System

children.title: Installing and using shadcn/ui components
children.description: shadcn/ui copies accessible Radix UI components styled with Tailwind directly into your codebase, so you own and can customise every component. This approach avoids dependency lock-in.

children.title: Building reusable UI with variant patterns
children.description: The cva (class-variance-authority) library powers shadcn/ui variants and lets you define component size/color variants declaratively. This pattern keeps component APIs clean and type-safe.

children.title: Dialogs, toasts, and sheet components for UX
children.description: Modal dialogs, toast notifications, and side sheets are essential feedback patterns. shadcn/ui provides accessible, animated versions of all three built on Radix primitives.

children.title: Skeleton screens for perceived performance
children.description: Skeleton components mimic the shape of loading content to reduce perceived wait time. Combining them with Suspense boundaries makes the page feel responsive even on slow connections.

children.title: cn utility for conditional Tailwind class merging
children.description: The cn helper (combining clsx and tailwind-merge) safely merges conditional Tailwind classes without class conflicts. It is the standard pattern for dynamic styles in shadcn/ui components.

next-fullstack.title: Advanced Data and Caching
next-fullstack.description: This layer covers Next.js caching layers, revalidation, and optimistic UI. Mastering caching is what makes Next.js apps both fast and correct.
next-fullstack.challenge: Add tag-based revalidation and optimistic task updates to the dashboard.

children.title: Request memoization within a single render pass
children.description: Next.js automatically deduplicates identical fetch calls made during the same server render. This allows sibling server components to each fetch the data they need without network duplication.

children.title: Data Cache vs Full Route Cache differences
children.description: The Data Cache persists fetch results between requests, while the Full Route Cache stores rendered HTML. Knowing which layer is stale helps you apply revalidation in the right place.

children.title: Tag-based cache invalidation for fine control
children.description: fetch({ next: { tags: ['posts'] } }) associates a cache entry with a tag. Calling revalidateTag('posts') then invalidates only those entries, avoiding a full-route cache bust.

children.title: Edge runtime vs Node.js runtime trade-offs
children.description: The Edge runtime starts faster and runs globally close to users, but lacks access to Node.js APIs like the file system. Choosing the right runtime per route optimises both latency and capability.

children.title: Avoiding stale data with cache: no-store
children.description: Passing cache: 'no-store' to a fetch call opts it out of the Data Cache entirely, forcing a fresh request every time. This is necessary for routes that must always show the latest data.
