children.title: TanStack Query for client-side server state
children.description: TanStack Query manages server state in client components with automatic background refetching, stale-time control, and cache invalidation. It is the standard complement to Next.js server components for interactive data.

children.title: Optimistic updates with useMutation
children.description: TanStack Query's useMutation hook provides onMutate, onError, and onSettled callbacks for optimistic updates with rollback. This pattern gives users instant feedback while the server request completes.

children.title: Parallel and dependent queries with useQuery
children.description: Multiple useQuery calls in a component run in parallel by default. Dependent queries use the enabled option to wait for a prerequisite query before fetching.

children.title: Incremental Static Regeneration with revalidate option
children.description: Exporting revalidate = 60 from a page opts it into ISR, regenerating the static HTML at most every 60 seconds. This delivers static performance with near-real-time freshness.

children.title: On-demand revalidation via webhook route handler
children.description: A secret-protected POST route handler calls revalidatePath or revalidateTag when a CMS publishes new content. This triggers an immediate cache refresh without redeploying.

next-fullstack.title: API Routes and Integrations
next-fullstack.description: This layer covers building APIs and integrating third-party services. Real apps depend on external services for payments, email, and more.
next-fullstack.challenge: Add a paid pro plan with Stripe checkout and a webhook that updates the user.

children.title: REST conventions for route handler design
children.description: Following REST conventions (GET for reads, POST for creates, PATCH for updates, DELETE for removes) makes your API predictable for other developers and third-party integrations.

children.title: Securing API keys and secrets on the server
children.description: API keys must never be exposed to the browser. Using them only in Server Components, Server Actions, or Route Handlers ensures they stay in the server environment and out of client bundles.

children.title: Webhook signature verification to prevent spoofing
children.description: Webhooks from Stripe and other services include a cryptographic signature header. Verifying it before processing the payload prevents attackers from sending fake webhook events.

children.title: Stripe payment intents and checkout flow
children.description: Stripe Payment Intents represent a payment attempt and track its lifecycle. Understanding the server-side intent creation and client-side confirmation split is essential for PCI compliance.

children.title: File upload strategies and storage options
children.description: Files should be streamed directly to object storage like S3 or Cloudflare R2 rather than through your server. Pre-signed upload URLs let the browser upload directly, bypassing your serverless functions.

children.title: Stripe Checkout and payment link setup
children.description: Stripe Checkout handles the payment form entirely, including card validation and 3D Secure. Creating a Checkout Session from a Server Action and redirecting to the hosted page is the fastest integration path.

children.title: Handling Stripe webhooks in a route handler
children.description: Stripe sends webhook events for completed payments, subscription changes, and refunds. A POST route handler that verifies the Stripe-Signature header and updates your database is the standard pattern.

children.title: Typed environment variables with Zod validation
children.description: Parsing process.env through a Zod schema at startup catches missing or malformed environment variables before they cause runtime errors in production.

children.title: Calling third-party APIs securely from server components
children.description: Making external API calls from Server Components or Server Actions keeps API keys off the client. Using a shared SDK instance prevents opening a new connection per request.

resources.label: Next.js – Fetching Data on the Server

children.title: Rate limiting and CORS for public route handlers
children.description: Public-facing route handlers should enforce rate limits to prevent abuse and set CORS headers to restrict origins. Vercel Edge Config and upstash/ratelimit are common solutions.

next-fullstack.title: Testing and Deployment to Vercel
next-fullstack.description: This layer covers testing the app and deploying it to production on Vercel. Tests and a smooth deploy pipeline make the project a real product.
next-fullstack.challenge: Add tests and deploy the dashboard to Vercel with a hosted database and previews.

children.title: Unit testing server and client logic with Vitest
children.description: Vitest is a fast, Vite-native test runner compatible with the Jest API. Testing pure utility functions, Zod schemas, and server action logic in isolation catches regressions before they reach the browser.

children.title: Component testing with React Testing Library
children.description: React Testing Library renders components in a virtual DOM and queries them by accessible roles and labels. This encourages testing behaviour rather than implementation details.

children.title: End-to-end testing strategy with Playwright
children.description: Playwright automates real browser interactions to verify critical user flows like sign-in, checkout, and form submission. E2E tests catch integration issues that unit tests miss.

children.title: Vercel project structure and environment setup
children.description: Connecting a Git repository to Vercel enables automatic deployments on every push. Production, preview, and development environment variables are managed separately in the Vercel dashboard.

children.title: Production database setup with connection pooling
children.description: Serverless functions open a new database connection per invocation, exhausting PostgreSQL connection limits. PgBouncer or Prisma Accelerate provide connection pooling to solve this.

children.title: Writing and running Playwright E2E tests
children.description: Playwright tests live in a tests/ directory and use page.goto, page.click, and expect(page).toHaveURL matchers. The codegen tool records browser interactions and generates test code automatically.

children.title: Mocking modules and fetch calls in Vitest
children.description: vi.mock lets you replace modules like Prisma Client or fetch with controlled test doubles. This isolates the code under test from external dependencies and makes tests deterministic.

children.title: Continuous integration with GitHub Actions
children.description: Running vitest and playwright test in a GitHub Actions workflow on every pull request prevents broken code from reaching main. Caching node_modules keeps CI fast.

children.title: Preview deployments for pull request review
children.description: Vercel creates a unique deployment URL for every pull request, making it easy to review UI changes before merging. Reviewers can test against production-like infrastructure without running the app locally.

children.title: Vercel Analytics and Speed Insights monitoring
children.description: Vercel Analytics tracks real user page view data, while Speed Insights reports Core Web Vitals like LCP and CLS. Both are enabled with a single package install and require no configuration.

sveltekit-dev.title: JavaScript and TypeScript Foundations
sveltekit-dev.description: This layer grounds you in the language fundamentals Svelte builds on. Comfort with modern JavaScript and types makes Svelte development smooth.
sveltekit-dev.challenge: Build a small typed utility module with functions to format dates and currency.

children.title: Arrow functions and destructuring syntax
children.description: ES6+ shorthand features that reduce boilerplate in component code. Svelte templates rely on these patterns throughout.

children.title: ES modules import and export system
children.description: The native browser module system used by Vite and SvelteKit. Understanding named vs default exports prevents common import errors.

children.title: Promises and async/await control flow
children.description: Async patterns are essential for data fetching in SvelteKit load functions. Knowing how to chain and handle errors prevents silent failures.

children.title: Interfaces and type aliases in TypeScript
children.description: TypeScript lets you describe the shape of objects and function signatures. SvelteKit generates types for routes automatically, making this knowledge essential.

children.title: Generics and utility types overview
children.description: Generics allow reusable, type-safe abstractions. SvelteKit's typed load helpers like PageLoad use generics extensively.

children.title: Vite project structure and config file
children.description: Vite is the build tool SvelteKit uses under the hood. Knowing vite.config.ts lets you add plugins and customize the dev experience.

children.title: Hot Module Replacement during development
children.description: Vite's HMR pushes component changes to the browser without a full reload. Understanding it helps debug state loss during development.

children.title: DOM querying and event listener basics
children.description: Svelte abstracts the DOM but sometimes you need raw API access. Lifecycle functions like onMount run in the browser where DOM APIs are available.

children.title: Fetch API for HTTP requests
children.description: The native Fetch API is the basis for all data loading in SvelteKit. The framework wraps it but knowing the raw API helps debug network issues.

sveltekit-dev.title: Svelte Fundamentals
sveltekit-dev.description: This layer covers building components with Svelte's reactive, compiler-based model. Svelte is the foundation that SvelteKit extends into a full framework.
sveltekit-dev.challenge: Build a reactive counter and a reusable card component in Svelte.

children.title: Single-file components and props declaration
children.description: Svelte components live in .svelte files with script, markup, and style sections. Props are declared with $props() in Svelte 5 runes mode.

children.title: Reactivity with runes and $state
children.description: Svelte 5 introduced runes — compiler signals like $state and $derived that replace the old reactive declarations. They make data flow explicit.

children.title: Slots and component composition patterns
children.description: Slots let parent components project markup into children. They are the primary composition mechanism for building layout wrappers.

children.title: Conditional rendering with if and else blocks
children.description: {#if} blocks conditionally include DOM nodes. Unlike framework virtual DOM diffing, Svelte creates and destroys real elements.

children.title: List rendering with each blocks and keys
children.description: {#each} iterates arrays and renders repeated elements. Keys tell Svelte how to efficiently reconcile list changes.

children.title: DOM event handling with onclick and on directives
children.description: Svelte uses onclick={handler} shorthand in Svelte 5. Understanding event delegation vs direct listeners matters for performance.

children.title: Two-way binding with bind directive
children.description: bind:value syncs form inputs directly to state variables. It eliminates boilerplate event handlers for common input patterns.

children.title: Scoped CSS and global style overrides
children.description: Styles in a <style> block are scoped to the component by default. The :global() modifier breaks out of scoping when needed.

children.title: Class and style directives for dynamic styling
children.description: class:active={isActive} conditionally applies classes without string concatenation. style:color={value} applies inline styles reactively.

sveltekit-dev.title: SvelteKit Routing and Pages
sveltekit-dev.description: This layer introduces SvelteKit's file-based routing, layouts, and project structure. Routing is the backbone of building full applications with SvelteKit.
sveltekit-dev.challenge: Scaffold a notes app with a list page, dynamic note route, and shared layout.

children.title: File-based routing and the src/routes directory
children.description: SvelteKit maps the file system to URLs. A +page.svelte file inside a folder becomes the page for that route segment.

children.title: Layouts and nested layout inheritance
children.description: +layout.svelte wraps child routes with shared UI like navbars. Nested folders inherit parent layouts forming a layout tree.

children.title: Dynamic route parameters with bracket syntax
children.description: A folder named [slug] creates a dynamic segment. The parameter value is available in load functions via params.slug.

children.title: Error pages with +error.svelte boundary
children.description: +error.svelte catches errors thrown in load functions and renders a fallback. Nested error files scope the boundary to a subtree.

children.title: SvelteKit project folder structure overview
children.description: src/routes, src/lib, static, and svelte.config.js each serve a distinct purpose. Understanding the layout prevents config confusion.

children.title: Client-side navigation with anchor tags
children.description: Standard <a href> tags trigger SvelteKit's client-side router automatically. No special component is required unlike other frameworks.

children.title: Programmatic navigation with goto function
children.description: The goto() helper navigates imperatively from event handlers or after form submissions. It supports push/replace and state options.

children.title: Route groups to share layouts without URL segments
children.description: Folders wrapped in (parentheses) are route groups. They apply a layout to a subset of routes without adding a URL segment.

children.title: Rest and optional route parameter patterns
children.description: [...rest] captures multiple path segments. [[optional]] makes a segment optional. Both are useful for catch-all and i18n routes.

sveltekit-dev.title: Loading Data
sveltekit-dev.description: This layer covers loading data on the server and client with load functions. Proper data loading is essential for fast, SEO-friendly pages.
sveltekit-dev.challenge: Load notes from an external API using server load functions with typed data.

children.title: Page and layout load functions explained
children.description: +page.server.ts exports a load function that fetches data before the page renders. The returned object becomes the page's data prop.

children.title: Server-only vs universal load distinction
children.description: +page.server.ts runs only on the server. +page.ts (universal) runs on both. Server loads can safely access databases; universal loads cannot.

resources.label: SvelteKit Docs — Universal vs server load

children.title: Type-safe data with generated PageData types
children.description: SvelteKit auto-generates TypeScript types in .svelte-kit/types for each route. Importing PageData ensures load return types match component props.

children.title: Throwing error and redirect inside load functions
children.description: The error() and redirect() helpers from @sveltejs/kit are thrown inside load to abort rendering. They integrate with SvelteKit's error boundary.

children.title: Streaming data with deferred promises
children.description: Returning a promise from load without awaiting it enables streaming. The browser receives HTML immediately while awaiting the remaining data.

children.title: Accessing route params inside the load event
children.description: Load receives an event object with params, url, and cookies. Destructuring params gives you typed access to dynamic route segments.

children.title: Using the load event fetch helper safely
children.description: The fetch provided to load is enhanced — it includes cookies on the server and can call internal endpoints with relative URLs.

children.title: invalidate and invalidateAll for re-running loads
children.description: Calling invalidate(url) tells SvelteKit to re-run load functions that depend on that URL. It avoids full page navigations after mutations.

children.title: depends() for manual load dependency tracking
children.description: depends('custom:key') registers a custom dependency in a load function. Calling invalidate('custom:key') re-runs only those loads.

sveltekit-dev.title: Form Actions and Mutations
sveltekit-dev.description: This layer teaches handling form submissions and mutations with form actions. Form actions provide progressive, secure server-side data changes.
sveltekit-dev.challenge: Add create, edit, and delete notes using form actions with Zod validation.

children.title: Defining actions in plus page.server.ts files
children.description: Exporting an actions object from +page.server.ts lets HTML forms POST to the same route. It keeps mutation logic co-located with the page.

children.title: Named actions for multiple forms on one page
children.description: action="?/create" and action="?/delete" route to different handlers in the same actions object. Useful when a page has several mutation concerns.

children.title: Returning validation errors from actions
children.description: Returning fail(400, { errors }) from an action sends error data back to the form without a redirect. The page data receives it via form prop.

children.title: Zod schema validation for form data
children.description: Zod parses and validates form data against a typed schema inside an action. It provides human-readable errors without manual checks.

children.title: Parsing FormData with Object.fromEntries
children.description: FormData must be converted to a plain object before Zod can parse it. Understanding this conversion step prevents runtime type errors.

children.title: Enhance directive for JavaScript-enhanced forms
children.description: use:enhance intercepts form submissions and handles them with fetch instead of a full page reload. Forms still work without JS.

children.title: Custom enhance callback for optimistic updates
children.description: Passing a function to use:enhance gives full control over the submit/result lifecycle. It enables optimistic UI and custom redirect logic.

children.title: Automatic data invalidation after action success
children.description: By default, use:enhance re-runs all load functions after a successful action. This keeps displayed data fresh without manual refreshes.

children.title: Redirect after POST to prevent duplicate submissions
children.description: Throwing redirect(303, "/path") after a successful action follows the POST/Redirect/GET pattern. It prevents form resubmission on refresh.

sveltekit-dev.title: Databases and Persistence
sveltekit-dev.description: This layer covers persisting data with a database and ORM in SvelteKit. Real storage turns the notes demo into a durable application.
sveltekit-dev.challenge: Persist notes in PostgreSQL via Prisma inside your server load and actions.

children.title: Defining models and fields in schema.prisma
children.description: The Prisma schema file describes your database tables as models with typed fields. Running prisma generate creates a type-safe client from it.

children.title: Model relations: one-to-many and many-to-many
children.description: Prisma represents foreign keys as relation fields. Defining both sides of a relation enables nested include queries.

children.title: Creating and applying database migrations
children.description: prisma migrate dev generates SQL migration files from schema changes and applies them. This keeps the database in sync with the schema.

children.title: Seeding the database with initial data
children.description: A seed script populates the database with development data. Prisma runs it via prisma db seed after configuring the script in package.json.

children.title: CRUD operations with PrismaClient
children.description: prisma.model.findMany(), create(), update(), and delete() cover the majority of data operations. All return typed objects matching the schema.

children.title: Filtering and selecting fields in queries
children.description: where, select, and include control what Prisma fetches. Selecting only needed fields reduces payload size and prevents over-fetching.

children.title: DATABASE_URL env variable setup for Prisma
children.description: Prisma reads the connection string from DATABASE_URL in .env. SvelteKit exposes server env vars through $env/static/private.

children.title: Singleton PrismaClient pattern in SvelteKit
children.description: Hot module reloading can create multiple PrismaClient instances. A module-level singleton stored in globalThis prevents connection pool exhaustion.

resources.label: Prisma Docs — Best practices for instantiating PrismaClient

sveltekit-dev.title: Authentication and Hooks
sveltekit-dev.description: This layer adds sessions, login, and request hooks for protected routes. Authentication enables per-user data and secure access control.
sveltekit-dev.challenge: Add login so each user can manage only their own notes using hooks and sessions.

children.title: The handle hook and request interception
children.description: Exporting handle from src/hooks.server.ts intercepts every server request. It is used to attach session data to event.locals before any load runs.

children.title: event.locals for passing data to load functions
children.description: locals is a per-request object populated in handle and readable in all load and action functions. It is the idiomatic place to store the current user.

children.title: Sessions and HTTP cookies for authentication state
children.description: Sessions persist login state between requests. The server stores session data and sends a session ID cookie to the browser.

children.title: Protecting routes by checking locals in load
children.description: Calling redirect(302, "/login") from a load function when locals.user is null locks down authenticated pages without middleware libraries.

children.title: Login and signup form actions with password hashing
children.description: Registration and login are server actions that hash passwords with bcrypt and create sessions. Keeping these server-side protects credentials.

resources.label: Lucia Auth — Username and password tutorial

children.title: Logout action that clears session cookies
children.description: Logout deletes the server-side session and sets the cookie to expire immediately. A server action is the correct place since it runs on the server.

children.title: CSRF protection built into SvelteKit form actions
children.description: SvelteKit form actions automatically validate the Origin header to prevent cross-site request forgery. Third-party fetch-based mutations must add tokens manually.

children.title: Secure and HttpOnly cookie flags explained
children.description: HttpOnly prevents JavaScript from reading session cookies, mitigating XSS. Secure ensures cookies are only sent over HTTPS in production.

sveltekit-dev.title: Advanced UI and Stores
sveltekit-dev.description: This layer covers shared state, animations, and polished interactions. Rich UI patterns make the application feel smooth and professional.
sveltekit-dev.challenge: Add a theme store and animated transitions when notes are added or removed.

children.title: Writable and readable stores for shared state
children.description: Svelte stores are observables that any component can subscribe to. Writable stores allow external updates; readable stores are read-only.

children.title: Derived stores that compute from other stores
children.description: derived() creates a store whose value is automatically computed from one or more source stores. It replaces manual subscription and calculation.

children.title: Context API for component-tree scoped state
children.description: setContext and getContext pass values down a component tree without prop drilling. Context is local to the tree unlike module-level stores.

children.title: ARIA attributes and semantic HTML in components
children.description: Svelte warns about missing accessibility attributes at compile time. Using semantic elements and ARIA ensures screen reader compatibility.

children.title: Built-in transitions like fade and slide
children.description: The svelte/transition module provides declarative enter/leave animations. Applying transition:fade to an {#if} block animates DOM insertions and removals.

children.title: The animate directive for list reordering
children.description: animate:flip animates elements within an {#each} block when keys change position. It makes list reorders feel smooth without manual DOM work.

children.title: Custom actions for reusable DOM behavior
children.description: use:action attaches imperative DOM logic to an element. It is the correct escape hatch for third-party library integration and focus management.

children.title: Reusable component patterns with generics
children.description: Svelte 5 supports generic components via <script generics="T">. This enables type-safe data table and list components that work with any item type.

sveltekit-dev.title: APIs and Advanced SvelteKit
sveltekit-dev.description: This layer covers building API endpoints and advanced rendering options. These features let SvelteKit power APIs and integrate with other clients.
sveltekit-dev.challenge: Add a JSON API endpoint that exposes notes for an external client.

children.title: API routes with plus server.ts files
children.description: +server.ts exports GET, POST, PATCH, DELETE handlers. They return a Response object and act as a REST endpoint alongside page routes.

children.title: json and error helpers for API responses
children.description: SvelteKit's json() shortcut sets Content-Type and serializes the body. The error() helper produces structured error responses with correct status codes.

children.title: SSR, CSR, and prerendering page options
children.description: Setting export const ssr = false disables server rendering per route. prerender = true generates static HTML at build time. Choosing correctly affects SEO and performance.

children.title: SvelteKit adapters for deployment targets
children.description: Adapters transform the build output for a specific platform. adapter-node creates a Node.js server; adapter-static outputs a pure static site.

children.title: Public vs private environment variables in SvelteKit
children.description: $env/static/private exposes server-only vars. $env/static/public (prefixed PUBLIC_) is safe to expose to the browser. Mixing them leaks secrets.

children.title: svelte.config.js and kit configuration options
children.description: svelte.config.js controls the adapter, path aliases, and Vite settings. Understanding it prevents build-time surprises when targeting different hosts.

children.title: $lib path alias for shared utilities
children.description: SvelteKit configures $lib to point to src/lib. Importing from $lib/server prevents circular deps and keeps utility imports short across the codebase.

children.title: Server-only modules with plus server restriction
children.description: Files in $lib/server are restricted from being imported in client code. SvelteKit throws a build error if a browser bundle imports them, preventing data leaks.

sveltekit-dev.title: Testing and Deployment
sveltekit-dev.description: This layer covers testing the app and deploying it to production. Tests and an adapter-based deploy make the project a real, reliable product.
sveltekit-dev.challenge: Add tests and deploy the SvelteKit notes app with a hosted database.

children.title: Vitest setup and test file conventions
children.description: Vitest is Vite-native and shares its config. Test files named *.test.ts run automatically and support the same import aliases as the app.

children.title: Testing pure utility functions and stores
children.description: Svelte stores and helper modules are plain JS and can be tested without a DOM. Vitest runs these tests in Node for speed.

children.title: Mocking modules and environment variables in tests
children.description: vi.mock() replaces module imports with test doubles. Mocking $env modules prevents tests from requiring real secrets.

children.title: Choosing an adapter for your target platform
children.description: adapter-vercel, adapter-netlify, and adapter-node each produce a different output. Matching the adapter to the hosting platform prevents runtime errors.

children.title: Environment variables in production deployments
children.description: Production env vars are set in the hosting dashboard, not in .env files. The PUBLIC_ prefix convention controls which values are embedded in the client bundle.

children.title: Playwright installation and browser setup
children.description: Playwright drives real browsers (Chromium, Firefox, WebKit) from Node.js. It integrates with Vitest or runs standalone via the @playwright/test runner.

children.title: Writing page navigation and form interaction tests
children.description: Playwright tests click buttons, fill forms, and assert URL and DOM state. Testing full user flows catches integration bugs that unit tests miss.

children.title: Using Playwright fixtures and page object model
children.description: Page Object Model encapsulates selectors and actions into reusable classes. Fixtures inject authenticated browser contexts to avoid repeating login steps.

children.title: Preview deployments with adapter-vercel or Netlify
children.description: Hosting platforms create preview URLs for every pull request. They run the production build against staging env vars for safe pre-merge testing.

children.title: Sourcemaps and error monitoring in production
children.description: Uploading sourcemaps to a monitoring tool like Sentry maps minified stack traces back to original SvelteKit code, making production errors debuggable.

nuxt-dev.title: JavaScript and TypeScript Foundations
nuxt-dev.description: This layer grounds you in the language fundamentals Vue and Nuxt build on. Solid JavaScript and types make the rest of the stack approachable.
nuxt-dev.challenge: Build a typed module of helper functions for formatting and filtering data.

children.title: Event Loop and Async Model
children.description: JavaScript is single-threaded but handles concurrency through the event loop, call stack, and microtask/macrotask queues. Misunderstanding this causes bugs where Promises resolve in unexpected order or async code blocks the UI thread, leading to frozen interfaces and race conditions.

children.title: Closures and Scope Chain
children.description: Closures allow inner functions to retain access to outer scope variables even after the outer function has returned. Misunderstanding closures leads to classic bugs like loop variable capture errors, memory leaks from retained DOM references, and unintended shared mutable state in callbacks.

children.title: Structural Typing vs Nominal Typing
children.description: TypeScript uses structural typing — compatibility is determined by shape, not name. This means a class or object literal satisfies an interface as long as it has the required properties. Misunderstanding this causes false confidence when two structurally identical types are semantically different, such as UserId and ProductId both being strings.

children.title: Generic Constraints and Inference
children.description: Generics allow writing reusable, type-safe functions and components without sacrificing specificity. Without understanding constraints (extends) and how TypeScript infers generic types from usage, developers either over-use any defeating the type system, or write overly rigid code that can't be composed.

children.title: Module System: ESM vs CommonJS
children.description: Node.js supports both CommonJS (require/module.exports) and ES Modules (import/export), but they are not fully interoperable. Mixing them without understanding the differences causes runtime errors, breaks tree-shaking in bundlers like Vite, and leads to incorrect default export handling across module boundaries.

children.title: Streams and Buffer Handling
children.description: Node.js streams allow processing data piece-by-piece without loading it all into memory, which is critical for file uploads, HTTP response streaming, and large dataset processing. Ignoring streams and loading entire files into Buffers causes memory exhaustion in production under load.

children.title: Promise Chaining vs async/await
children.description: async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises that makes asynchronous code read synchronously, but errors in async functions must still be caught. Forgetting to await a Promise inside an async function or mishandling rejected Promises leads to unhandled rejection warnings and silent data loss.

children.title: Error Propagation in Async Code
children.description: Unlike synchronous try/catch, errors in async code only propagate correctly when every async call is awaited. Unawaited Promises swallow errors silently, and Promise.all fails fast without cleanup. Mishandling this in Nuxt server routes causes HTTP 500 responses with no useful error information.

nuxt-dev.title: Vue 3 Fundamentals
nuxt-dev.description: This layer covers building reactive components with Vue 3 and the Composition API. Vue is the framework that Nuxt extends into a full stack tool.
nuxt-dev.challenge: Build a reactive product list component with search and a reusable card.

children.title: ref vs reactive: Choosing the Right Primitive
children.description: ref wraps a value in a reactive object accessible via .value, while reactive creates a deeply reactive proxy of an object. Misusing them — such as destructuring a reactive object or replacing a ref's container — breaks reactivity silently, causing the template to stop updating when data changes.

children.title: computed vs watchEffect: Derived State
children.description: computed creates cached derived state that only recomputes when its dependencies change, while watchEffect runs a side effect immediately and re-runs on dependency changes. Using watch/watchEffect for values that should be computed introduces bugs from stale caches and unnecessary re-renders.

children.title: Composables for Logic Reuse
children.description: Composables are functions that encapsulate and reuse stateful logic by leveraging Vue's reactivity APIs. Unlike mixins, composables have explicit data flow and no namespace collisions. Misunderstanding when reactive state should live inside vs outside a composable leads to shared global state bugs across component instances.

children.title: Lifecycle Hooks in Composition API
children.description: Composition API lifecycle hooks (onMounted, onUnmounted, etc.) must be called synchronously inside setup() to be registered correctly. Placing them inside conditionals or async callbacks means they may not run at all, causing DOM operations to fail or event listeners to never be cleaned up.

children.title: Props and Emits Contract
children.description: Props define the public API a component exposes to parents, while emits define the events it can fire upward. Mutating props directly breaks Vue's unidirectional data flow and causes hard-to-trace reactivity bugs. Proper validation using defineProps with TypeScript types or runtime validators prevents entire classes of integration errors.

children.title: Slots and Render Scoping
children.description: Slots allow parent components to inject template content into child components. Scoped slots expose child data back to the parent's slot template, enabling powerful flexible patterns. Misunderstanding slot scope — particularly which component's data is accessible where — leads to template errors and undefined variable bugs.

children.title: HMR and Dev Server Architecture
children.description: Vite's dev server uses native ES modules in the browser and HMR (Hot Module Replacement) to push changes without full page reloads. Understanding HMR boundaries is critical — modules that export mutable state or use side-effectful imports can cause HMR to trigger full reloads, destroying development speed.

children.title: Environment Variables and Modes
children.description: Vite exposes environment variables prefixed with VITE_ to the client bundle via import.meta.env. Variables without this prefix are server-only and will be undefined in the browser. Accidentally exposing secrets by omitting this distinction can leak API keys into the client JavaScript bundle.

resources.label: Vite Docs — Env Variables and Modes

nuxt-dev.title: Nuxt Basics and Routing
nuxt-dev.description: This layer introduces Nuxt structure, file-based routing, and layouts. Nuxt conventions provide the scaffolding for full applications.
nuxt-dev.challenge: Scaffold a shop app with a products page, dynamic product route, and layout.

children.title: Auto-imports and File-based Conventions
children.description: Nuxt automatically imports components, composables, and utilities from their respective directories without explicit import statements. Understanding which directories trigger auto-imports (components/, composables/, utils/) and their scoping rules prevents duplicate imports, naming conflicts, and confusing 'undefined is not a function' errors in templates.

children.title: Layouts and pages/ Convention
children.description: Nuxt uses the pages/ directory with file-system routing and layouts/ for shared page chrome. The NuxtLayout and NuxtPage components are required in app.vue to render them. Skipping these conventions or mixing manual Vue Router configuration with Nuxt's file-based router causes 404 errors and broken navigation.

children.title: Dynamic Routes and Catch-all Segments
children.description: Nuxt generates routes from the pages/ file tree: [id].vue creates a dynamic segment, [...slug].vue creates a catch-all. Misunderstanding route priority — especially when static and dynamic routes coexist — causes the wrong page component to render, and catch-all routes accidentally swallow 404 errors that should propagate.

children.title: Nested Routes with NuxtChild
children.description: Nuxt supports nested routes through directory nesting in pages/ paired with a parent file of the same name containing NuxtPage. Misunderstanding this requires a parent layout file — not just a folder — causes child routes to render as full pages without their parent wrapper, breaking shared UI sections.

children.title: SSR vs SSG vs SPA: Choosing the Right Mode
children.description: Nuxt supports Server-Side Rendering (SSR), Static Site Generation (SSG), and Client-Side Only (SPA) rendering per page via routeRules. Choosing the wrong mode — like SSG for pages with user-specific data — causes stale content to be served to all users from the CDN cache, exposing private data or outdated information.

children.title: Hydration and Client/Server Mismatch
children.description: SSR renders HTML on the server which Vue then hydrates on the client by attaching event listeners to the existing DOM. If client-rendered HTML differs from server-rendered HTML (e.g., due to browser-only APIs or random values), Vue throws hydration mismatch warnings and may re-render the entire component tree, defeating SSR performance gains.

children.title: nuxt.config.ts Structure and Layers
children.description: nuxt.config.ts is the central configuration file controlling modules, runtime config, build options, and Vite/Nitro settings. Nuxt also supports configuration layers for sharing config between apps. Misplacing sensitive secrets in publicRuntimeConfig instead of runtimeConfig.private exposes them to the client-side bundle.

children.title: Plugins and App Initialization
children.description: Nuxt plugins in plugins/ are executed before the Vue app mounts and can extend the app instance, provide global utilities, or register third-party libraries. Plugins prefixed with .server or .client limit execution to one environment. Running browser-only code in a server plugin crashes SSR with 'window is not defined' errors.

nuxt-dev.title: Data Fetching and Rendering
nuxt-dev.description: This layer covers fetching data and Nuxt's rendering modes. Choosing the right rendering and fetch strategy is key to performance and SEO.
nuxt-dev.challenge: Fetch products from an external API with useFetch and add page metadata.

children.title: useFetch vs useAsyncData: When to Use Each
children.description: useFetch is a convenience wrapper around useAsyncData + $fetch that deduplicates requests between server and client. useAsyncData gives more control for complex scenarios like transforming data or using non-HTTP sources. Using $fetch directly inside setup() without these wrappers causes double fetching — once on the server and again on the client — doubling API calls and slowing page load.

children.title: Keys and Cache Deduplication
children.description: useAsyncData uses a key to deduplicate and cache requests across SSR and client navigation. If two calls share the same key, only the first executes and the second receives the cached result. Accidentally using duplicate keys with different parameters causes pages to show stale data from a different request's cache.

children.title: Universal Rendering and Payload Transfer
children.description: In SSR mode, Nuxt serializes useAsyncData results into an inline JSON payload in the HTML. On the client, this payload is read to hydrate state without re-fetching. Understanding this mechanism is critical because mutating the returned data object directly can corrupt the serialized payload, leading to hydration mismatches on navigation.

children.title: Lazy Fetching and Suspense
children.description: The lazy option in useFetch/useAsyncData returns immediately with pending state instead of blocking page render. This enables streaming and skeleton UIs but requires explicit pending and error state handling in the template. Using lazy without handling the pending state causes brief periods of undefined data being rendered, crashing the page.

children.title: defineProps with TypeScript Generics
children.description: In Nuxt/Vue 3 with script setup, props can be typed using TypeScript interfaces passed to defineProps<T>(). This provides full type inference for prop usage in templates. Omitting types or using runtime-only validators loses IDE autocompletion and allows passing wrong prop types that fail silently at runtime.

resources.label: Vue 3 Docs — TypeScript with Composition API

children.title: Auto-generated Types from Nuxt
children.description: Nuxt generates TypeScript types for routes, server API endpoints, and runtime config into .nuxt/types/. These types provide end-to-end type safety from the server route handler to useFetch response. Not running nuxt prepare (or having it in dev watch mode) means these generated types go stale, causing false type errors or missing type safety on API responses.

children.title: server/api/ Route Conventions
children.description: Nuxt server routes in server/api/ are powered by Nitro and support HTTP method-specific file naming like [id].get.ts and [id].post.ts. These routes run exclusively on the server and have access to Node.js APIs. Confusing client composables with server route handlers — such as calling useRuntimeConfig() in a route expecting browser context — causes runtime crashes.

children.title: Event Handling with H3
children.description: Nuxt's server layer uses H3 (the HTTP framework underlying Nitro) to handle requests via event objects. Helpers like readBody, getQuery, and setCookie operate on the H3 event. Not understanding that each request is a stateless H3 event — and not a persistent Express-style req object — leads to incorrect state management in API routes.

nuxt-dev.title: Server Engine with Nitro
nuxt-dev.description: This layer covers building backend API routes with the Nitro server engine. Nitro lets Nuxt apps include their own server logic and endpoints.
nuxt-dev.challenge: Add Nitro API routes that serve and create products for the shop app.

children.title: Nitro's Universal Deployment Model
children.description: Nitro compiles Nuxt's server into a standalone, deployable output that targets multiple runtimes: Node.js, Deno, Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge, and more. This is achieved through a preset system that polyfills or removes Node.js-specific APIs. Using Node.js-only APIs (like fs or process.env) without checking preset compatibility causes deployment failures on edge runtimes.

children.title: Nitro Plugins and Server Middleware
children.description: Nitro supports plugins (server/plugins/) for one-time initialization like connecting to databases, and middleware (server/middleware/) that runs on every request. Unlike Nuxt client plugins, these run in the server context only. Blocking the Nitro plugin initialization with a failed DB connection causes the entire server to fail to start.

children.title: cachedEventHandler and Route Caching
children.description: Nitro's cachedEventHandler wraps a server route with configurable caching (in-memory, Redis, or filesystem) keyed by request parameters. This dramatically reduces database load for read-heavy endpoints. Not invalidating the cache after data mutations causes stale API responses to persist until the cache TTL expires, showing outdated data to users.

children.title: Storage Layer with unstorage
children.description: Nitro includes a built-in storage abstraction powered by unstorage that works with multiple backends (memory, Redis, filesystem, Cloudflare KV) through a unified API. This prevents vendor lock-in at the storage layer. Hardcoding Redis-specific client calls instead of using the storage layer makes switching deployment targets require rewriting server logic.

children.title: Typed Request Bodies and Query Params
children.description: H3's readBody and getQuery return untyped objects by default. Using Zod or TypeScript generics to validate and type these at the entry point of every server route prevents invalid data from propagating into business logic and database queries. Skipping validation means malformed requests can cause unhandled exceptions or SQL injection vectors.

children.title: useRuntimeConfig for Server Secrets
children.description: Nuxt's useRuntimeConfig() composable exposes runtime config on both client and server, but secrets defined under runtimeConfig (without public) are only available server-side. Accessing runtimeConfig.secret on the client returns undefined. Misunderstanding this leads to server API keys being undefined in routes — or worse, accidentally placing them under runtimeConfig.public.

children.title: File System Access in Server Routes
children.description: Nuxt server routes running on Node.js can use fs, path, and other Node.js core modules for file operations. However, these APIs are unavailable on edge runtimes. Structuring code to conditionally use Node.js APIs behind runtime checks, or abstracting via Nitro's storage layer, is required for portable server code.

children.title: Environment Variable Access Patterns
children.description: In Nitro/Node.js, process.env provides access to environment variables at runtime, but in Nuxt the preferred pattern is runtimeConfig backed by .env files. Directly calling process.env bypasses Nuxt's type-safe config layer and fails silently (returning undefined) when variables are missing, rather than throwing a config validation error.

nuxt-dev.title: Databases and Persistence
nuxt-dev.description: This layer covers persisting data through a database in your Nitro server routes. Real storage turns the demo into a durable application.
nuxt-dev.challenge: Persist products in PostgreSQL via Prisma inside your Nitro API routes.

children.title: Drizzle ORM Schema Definition
children.description: Drizzle ORM uses TypeScript to define database schemas as code, enabling full type inference for queries without code generation. The schema becomes the single source of truth for both types and migrations. Defining schemas inconsistently — such as using different column names in schema vs. queries — causes TypeScript errors in queries and runtime SQL failures.

children.title: Drizzle Migrations Workflow
children.description: Drizzle Kit generates SQL migration files from schema changes via drizzle-kit generate, which are then applied with drizzle-kit migrate. Unlike Prisma, there is no automatic migration on app startup. Forgetting to run migrations after deploying schema changes causes runtime SQL errors when the app queries columns that don't exist in the database yet.

children.title: Connection Pooling in Serverless Environments
children.description: Traditional PostgreSQL connections are persistent and expensive. In serverless or edge environments where function instances spin up and down, creating a new DB connection per request exhausts the PostgreSQL max_connections limit quickly. Using a connection pooler like PgBouncer or Neon's serverless driver is essential to prevent connection exhaustion.

children.title: Transactions and ACID Guarantees
children.description: PostgreSQL transactions group multiple operations so they either all succeed or all fail atomically. In Nuxt server routes performing multi-step writes (e.g., create user + create profile), omitting transactions means a partial failure leaves the database in an inconsistent state that is difficult to detect and correct.

children.title: Database Client Singleton Pattern
children.description: In Node.js, importing a module returns the same cached instance across the app. This makes it safe to create a single Drizzle/Postgres client in a utility file and import it across server routes. However, in serverless environments each cold start creates a new instance, requiring connection pooling. Not understanding this distinction leads to connection leaks in serverless deployments.

resources.label: Drizzle ORM Docs — Get Started with PostgreSQL

children.title: Row-Level Security and Multi-tenancy
children.description: PostgreSQL Row-Level Security (RLS) policies enforce data access control at the database layer, preventing one tenant from reading another's data even if application-level checks fail. Using RLS as a defense-in-depth layer — rather than relying solely on WHERE clauses in server routes — provides protection against SQL injection and application logic bugs.

children.title: N+1 Query Problem with Drizzle
children.description: N+1 occurs when fetching a list of records and then issuing a separate query per record to load related data, e.g., fetching 100 posts then 100 separate author queries. Drizzle's relational query API (db.query) with the with option performs JOINs or batched queries to avoid this, and not using it causes severe database performance degradation under load.

children.title: Index Strategy for Common Query Patterns
children.description: PostgreSQL uses indexes to avoid full table scans on large tables. Columns frequently used in WHERE, ORDER BY, or JOIN conditions should be indexed. Not adding indexes on foreign key columns and filter columns causes queries that are fast in development with small datasets to become unacceptably slow in production with millions of rows.

nuxt-dev.title: State and Authentication
nuxt-dev.description: This layer covers shared state with Pinia and adding user authentication. State and auth enable carts, sessions, and per-user data.
topics: Accessing the user on server and client
nuxt-dev.challenge: Add login and a Pinia-backed cart so users manage their own orders.

children.title: Store Definition: Options vs Setup Syntax
children.description: Pinia supports both Options Store (defineStore with state/getters/actions object) and Setup Store (defineStore with a setup function using refs and computed). Setup stores offer full Composition API power including composables, but require manual return statements. Forgetting to return reactive values from a setup store makes them inaccessible to components, causing undefined errors.

children.title: Store Hydration in SSR
children.description: In Nuxt SSR, Pinia stores must be hydrated with server-fetched data to prevent the client from re-fetching on page load. The @pinia/nuxt module handles serializing store state into the Nuxt payload. Not understanding this can lead to double API calls or hydration mismatches when store state differs between server and client.

children.title: nuxt-auth-utils Session Model
children.description: nuxt-auth-utils provides sealed cookie-based sessions using iron-session under the hood, exposing useUserSession() composable on both client and server. Sessions are encrypted and verified server-side on every request. Storing sensitive data client-side in localStorage instead of server-managed sessions exposes it to XSS attacks and token theft.

children.title: Route Protection with Middleware
children.description: Nuxt route middleware (middleware/) runs before navigation on the client and before rendering on the server for universal middleware. Auth guards belong in named middleware applied per-page or globally. Using only client-side guards leaves server-rendered pages accessible to unauthenticated users who bypass JavaScript, leaking protected HTML.

children.title: Typing Store State and Actions
children.description: Pinia infers TypeScript types from the state factory function and action return types automatically. Explicitly typing complex state shapes using interfaces prevents components from passing wrong data types to store actions. Without typed stores, refactoring actions or state shape becomes dangerous as TypeScript can't catch call-site mismatches.

children.title: Store Getters as Computed Properties
children.description: Pinia getters are equivalent to Vuex getters or Vue computed properties — they are cached based on their reactive dependencies and recompute only when dependencies change. Using a regular function on the store object instead of a getter loses caching, causing expensive computations to run on every access instead of only when data changes.

children.title: CSRF Protection in Nuxt
children.description: Cookie-based sessions are vulnerable to Cross-Site Request Forgery unless protected. nuxt-auth-utils's sealed sessions combined with SameSite=Lax cookie attributes mitigate most CSRF attacks. For state-changing operations, adding a server-side CSRF token check or using SameSite=Strict provides full protection. Omitting these allows malicious sites to make authenticated requests on behalf of logged-in users.

children.title: Password Hashing with bcrypt/argon2
children.description: User passwords must never be stored in plaintext or with weak hashing algorithms like MD5/SHA1. bcrypt and argon2 are purpose-built slow hashing algorithms with configurable work factors that resist brute-force and rainbow table attacks. Using fast hash functions for passwords means a database breach immediately exposes all user passwords.

nuxt-dev.title: Modules and Advanced Patterns
nuxt-dev.description: This layer covers the Nuxt module ecosystem and reusable patterns. Modules and composables speed up development and keep code clean.
nuxt-dev.challenge: Add image optimization and a custom composable for currency formatting.

children.title: Building Custom Nuxt Modules
children.description: Nuxt modules extend the framework by hooking into the build lifecycle via defineNuxtModule, addPlugin, addComponent, and addImports helpers. Modules run at build time, not runtime. Writing build-time logic that assumes runtime context (like accessing the database) in a module causes build failures and misplaced side effects.

children.title: Using Community Modules Safely
children.description: Nuxt's module ecosystem (nuxt.com/modules) provides integrations for UI libraries, CMS, auth, analytics, and more. Modules add configuration, plugins, composables, and build hooks automatically. Adding too many modules without understanding their impact inflates bundle size and SSR overhead — each module's plugin runs on every request.

children.title: Render Functions and JSX in Vue 3
children.description: Vue 3 supports render functions using h() and JSX as alternatives to templates for programmatic component creation. Render functions bypass template compilation and access the virtual DOM directly, enabling dynamic component trees that are impossible with static templates. Misusing them without understanding the reactivity implications produces components that never update.

children.title: Teleport for Portal Rendering
children.description: Vue's Teleport component renders its slot content into a different DOM node than its logical position in the component tree, enabling modals, toasts, and tooltips to escape overflow:hidden or z-index stacking contexts. Without Teleport, overlays rendered inside deeply nested components are clipped or hidden behind other elements due to CSS stacking context rules.

children.title: Conditional Types and Template Literals
children.description: TypeScript conditional types (T extends U ? X : Y) enable type-level branching to create precise return type annotations based on input types. Combined with template literal types, they model complex API shapes where the response type depends on the request parameters. Without these, API response types must be manually cast with as, hiding bugs.

children.title: Declaration Merging and Module Augmentation
children.description: TypeScript allows extending existing type declarations through module augmentation, which is how Nuxt plugins add properties to the NuxtApp instance in a type-safe way. Without augmenting the correct interface, accessing plugin-injected properties causes TypeScript errors even though they exist at runtime, or requires unsafe any casts.

children.title: App Hooks vs Build Hooks
children.description: Nuxt provides two hook systems: app hooks that run at runtime (app:created, page:finish, etc.) accessible via the Nuxt instance, and build hooks that run during nuxt build or nuxt dev (build:before, modules:done, etc.) accessible in modules and nuxt.config. Confusing the two and trying to hook into app lifecycle events from a module hook causes the callback to never fire.

children.title: useNuxtApp for Plugin Communication
children.description: useNuxtApp() provides access to the shared Nuxt application instance, including plugins, provide/inject values, hooks, and SSR payload. It is the official way for composables to access app-level context set up by plugins. Using global variables or module-level state instead of useNuxtApp() breaks in SSR where each request has its own app instance.

nuxt-dev.title: Performance and SEO
nuxt-dev.description: This layer focuses on making Nuxt apps fast and discoverable. Performance and SEO are central strengths of server-rendered Nuxt apps.
nuxt-dev.challenge: Audit and optimize the shop for strong Lighthouse and SEO scores.

children.title: Core Web Vitals: LCP, CLS, INP
children.description: Core Web Vitals are Google's standardized performance metrics: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) measures loading speed, Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) measures visual stability, and Interaction to Next Paint (INP) measures responsiveness. These metrics directly affect Google search ranking. Optimizing for traditional metrics like Time to First Byte without addressing LCP still results in poor user experience scores.

children.title: Critical Rendering Path Optimization
children.description: The critical rendering path is the sequence of steps the browser takes from HTML to rendered pixels: HTML parsing, CSSOM construction, render tree, layout, and paint. Render-blocking resources (scripts without defer/async, synchronous CSS) stall this process. Understanding which resources block the path allows targeted optimization to improve First Contentful Paint.

children.title: Lazy Loading Components and Routes
children.description: Nuxt auto-splits pages into separate chunks, but large components imported directly are bundled into the main chunk. Using defineAsyncComponent() or lazy: true in defineComponent delays loading until the component is needed, reducing initial bundle size. Not lazy-loading heavy components like rich text editors causes large JavaScript bundles that delay interactivity.

children.title: Image Optimization with nuxt/image
children.description: The @nuxt/image module provides automatic image optimization: WebP/AVIF conversion, responsive srcset generation, lazy loading, and CDN integration. Serving unoptimized images is often the largest contributor to poor LCP scores and excessive bandwidth usage. Without optimization, a single hero image can add multiple megabytes to page weight.

children.title: useHead and Meta Tag Management
children.description: Nuxt's useHead composable (powered by unhead) manages document head tags reactively — title, meta, link, script — with full SSR support. Meta tags must be set server-side for search engines and social media scrapers to read them, as these crawlers often don't execute JavaScript. Using client-only head updates means OG tags are missing when pages are shared on social media.

children.title: Structured Data and JSON-LD
children.description: JSON-LD structured data embedded in page head tells search engines about content type (Article, Product, BreadcrumbList) enabling rich search result features like stars, prices, and breadcrumbs. Nuxt's useHead or useSchemaOrg (via nuxt-schema-org) makes this type-safe. Missing structured data means missing out on rich snippets that significantly improve click-through rates.

children.title: Bundle Analysis and Tree Shaking
children.description: Vite's production build uses Rollup with tree shaking to eliminate unused code. Libraries that use CommonJS or side-effectful imports cannot be tree-shaken effectively, inflating bundle size. Using rollup-plugin-visualizer or vite-bundle-analyzer reveals which modules dominate bundle size, enabling targeted replacements with lighter alternatives.

children.title: Code Splitting Strategy
children.description: Vite automatically splits vendor code and async imports into separate chunks. Manual chunk splitting via build.rollupOptions.output.manualChunks controls which modules are grouped, reducing duplicate vendor code across page chunks. Without manual chunk configuration, each page bundle may include the same large library, multiplying download size for users navigating between pages.

nuxt-dev.title: Testing and Deployment
nuxt-dev.description: This layer covers testing the app and deploying it to production. Tests and a smooth deploy turn the project into a real, reliable product.
nuxt-dev.challenge: Add tests and deploy the Nuxt shop app with a hosted database.

children.title: Testing Vue Components with @vue/test-utils
children.description: @vue/test-utils provides mount and shallowMount to render Vue components in a test environment. Testing that a component renders correctly, responds to user events, and emits the right values catches regressions without running a browser. Not testing component behavior under different prop combinations leads to production bugs from edge-case prop values.

children.title: Mocking in Vitest: vi.mock and Spies
children.description: Vitest's vi.mock() replaces module imports with controlled stubs, enabling tests to isolate the unit under test from external dependencies like API calls or Pinia stores. Spy functions (vi.spyOn) verify that specific functions were called with expected arguments. Testing without mocking causes tests to depend on external services, making them slow and flaky.

children.title: nuxt/test-utils for Integration Testing
children.description: @nuxt/test-utils provides utilities to spin up a real Nuxt app instance in tests, enabling testing of server routes, middleware, and SSR behavior with actual HTTP requests. Unlike unit tests with mocks, integration tests catch issues that arise from real module interactions, such as incorrect middleware order or broken server API responses.

children.title: Testing Composables with Pinia
children.description: Pinia stores must be initialized with createPinia() before testing composables that use them. @pinia/testing provides createTestingPinia() which creates a fresh store instance with optional initial state and stubbed actions. Using a shared store instance across tests causes test pollution where one test's store mutations affect subsequent tests.

children.title: Playwright Test Architecture
children.description: Playwright runs real browser instances (Chromium, Firefox, WebKit) and automates user interactions through a high-level API. Tests can assert on network requests, localStorage, and visual snapshots. Without E2E tests covering critical user flows like login and checkout, regressions in these paths only surface in production, causing revenue-impacting outages.

children.title: Page Object Model Pattern
children.description: The Page Object Model (POM) encapsulates page-specific selectors and interactions in a class, keeping test files clean and selectors maintainable. When a UI element changes, updating one POM class fixes all tests that use it. Writing selectors inline throughout test files causes brittle tests that require updating dozens of test files when a single element changes.

children.title: Nuxt Deployment Targets and Presets
children.description: Nuxt builds different output formats based on the target deployment platform: Node.js server, static hosting, Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare, and more. Each preset configures Nitro's output appropriately. Deploying a Node.js build to a static CDN causes all dynamic server routes to return 404, and deploying SSR to a static host serves blank pages without JavaScript.

children.title: CI/CD Pipeline for Nuxt Apps
children.description: A proper CI/CD pipeline for Nuxt runs type checking (nuxi typecheck), linting, unit tests, E2E tests, and build verification on every pull request before merging. Skipping the build step in CI means broken builds that fail to compile only surface during deployment, blocking releases and requiring rushed hotfixes under pressure.

astro-dev.title: Web and TypeScript Foundations
astro-dev.description: This layer covers the web fundamentals and types that Astro builds on. A strong base in markup, styling, and TypeScript makes Astro intuitive.
astro-dev.challenge: Build a static styled landing page with semantic, responsive HTML and CSS.

children.title: Semantic HTML Elements
children.description: Semantic elements like <article>, <section>, <nav>, and <main> communicate meaning to browsers, assistive technologies, and search engines — not just visual structure. Using <div> for everything breaks screen reader navigation and hurts SEO; search engines demote pages that don't clearly signal content hierarchy.

children.title: ARIA and Accessibility Tree
children.description: The accessibility tree is a parallel representation of the DOM consumed by screen readers. Misusing ARIA roles or omitting alt attributes causes assistive technology to misinterpret page structure, making content inaccessible to users with disabilities and violating WCAG standards.

children.title: CSS Box Model
children.description: Every element is a box composed of content, padding, border, and margin. Without understanding box-sizing: border-box, adding padding to a fixed-width element causes unexpected overflow and layout breaks — a common source of misalignment bugs in component-based designs.

children.title: Flexbox and Grid
children.description: Flexbox handles one-dimensional layouts (rows or columns) while Grid handles two-dimensional layouts simultaneously. Choosing the wrong model forces brittle workarounds; using Grid for a simple nav bar adds unnecessary complexity, while using Flexbox for a full-page grid creates alignment nightmares.

resources.label: CSS-Tricks — A Complete Guide to Flexbox
CSS-Tricks — A Complete Guide to Grid

children.title: Static Typing and Type Inference
children.description: TypeScript's type system catches category errors at compile time rather than runtime, preventing entire classes of bugs. Relying only on type inference without explicit annotations on function signatures causes types to widen unexpectedly, letting invalid data propagate silently through the codebase.

children.title: Interfaces and Type Aliases
children.description: Interfaces and type aliases both define object shapes, but interfaces support declaration merging while type aliases support unions and intersections. Choosing the wrong one for a given pattern — such as using a type alias when you need augmentation across modules — causes hard-to-trace compilation errors.

children.title: Mobile-First Media Queries
children.description: Mobile-first design starts with the smallest viewport and adds complexity at larger breakpoints, resulting in leaner CSS that doesn't override itself. Writing desktop-first and undoing styles for mobile leads to specificity conflicts and unnecessarily heavy stylesheets on low-power devices.

children.title: CSS Custom Properties
children.description: CSS custom properties (variables) enable dynamic theming and design tokens that cascade through the DOM. Hardcoding color and spacing values instead forces global find-and-replace for any brand change and makes dark mode or multi-theme support nearly impossible without JavaScript.

astro-dev.title: Astro Basics and Components
astro-dev.description: This layer introduces Astro components, the .astro syntax, and the project structure. Understanding Astro's component model is the entry point to the framework.
astro-dev.challenge: Rebuild the landing page as reusable Astro components with props and slots.

children.title: Component Script Fence
children.description: The frontmatter fence (---) in .astro files runs exclusively on the server at build time, meaning any code there never ships to the browser. Placing expensive data fetching or secrets in the wrong location — such as a client-side script tag — exposes them in the browser bundle and bloats JavaScript payloads.

children.title: Props and Component Composition
children.description: Astro components receive typed props defined with TypeScript interfaces in the frontmatter, enabling compile-time validation of component APIs. Omitting type definitions lets invalid prop shapes pass silently and only fail at runtime during rendering, making bugs hard to trace in large component trees.

children.title: HTML-first Output
children.description: Astro components compile to pure HTML with no JavaScript by default, delivering the fastest possible first paint. Adding unnecessary client-side JS through framework components without explicit client directives defeats Astro's performance model and re-introduces the hydration overhead Astro is designed to eliminate.

children.title: Slots and Template Composition
children.description: Astro slots allow parent components to inject HTML content into child component placeholders, enabling flexible layout composition without prop drilling. Ignoring slots forces you to duplicate layout markup across pages or pass raw HTML as strings, breaking encapsulation and making refactoring error-prone.

children.title: Astro Template Expressions
children.description: Astro uses JSX-like expressions in templates but renders them server-side, meaning dynamic values are resolved at build or request time. Treating Astro templates as if they support reactive state like React leads to runtime errors because there is no browser-side reactivity engine unless you explicitly add a framework island.

children.title: Scoped CSS in Astro
children.description: Astro automatically scopes <style> blocks to the component by adding a unique data attribute, preventing style leakage across component boundaries. Using global selectors without the :global() escape hatch inside scoped styles causes unexpected specificity mismatches when styles appear not to apply.

children.title: Strict Mode TypeScript Config
children.description: Astro projects include a tsconfig.json with strict mode enabled, which activates stricter null checks and type narrowing rules. Disabling strict mode to avoid errors masks bugs like null dereferences that will crash pages at runtime when content is missing or API responses are empty.

children.title: Type-safe Component Imports
children.description: Astro resolves component imports with full TypeScript type checking, ensuring component prop interfaces are validated at compile time. Importing components with incorrect paths or missing type exports causes silent any-typing that allows invalid props to reach the template, causing render failures in production.

astro-dev.title: Routing, Layouts, and Pages
astro-dev.description: This layer covers file-based routing, layouts, and dynamic pages in Astro. Routing and layouts are how you assemble a multi-page site.
astro-dev.challenge: Create a multi-page blog with a shared layout and dynamic post routes.

children.title: Pages Directory Structure
children.description: Astro maps files in src/pages/ directly to URL routes, where index.astro becomes the root and nested folders become URL segments. Misunderstanding this convention — such as putting pages in the wrong directory or naming files with incorrect casing on case-sensitive servers — produces 404 errors that only appear in production.

children.title: Dynamic Routes and getStaticPaths
children.description: Dynamic route segments like [slug].astro require a getStaticPaths() function to tell Astro which URL combinations to pre-build. Omitting getStaticPaths in static output mode causes a build error because Astro cannot enumerate routes; returning an incomplete list silently 404s valid URLs at runtime.

children.title: Layout Components and Slot Injection
children.description: Astro layouts are ordinary components that wrap pages using a <slot /> to inject page content, allowing shared headers, footers, and <head> metadata to be defined once. Duplicating layout markup across pages instead means a single structural change (like adding a nav link) requires editing every page file, leading to drift and inconsistency.

children.title: Nested Layouts
children.description: Astro supports nesting layouts so a blog layout can wrap inside a base layout, enabling compositional page structure without repetition. Failing to pass required props between nested layouts — such as the page title or SEO metadata — results in missing <title> tags and broken Open Graph previews in production.

children.title: View Transitions API
children.description: Astro's View Transitions integration wraps the browser's native View Transitions API to animate between pages without a full-page reload. Not importing the <ViewTransitions /> component or using it incorrectly disables the animation fallback, causing jarring hard navigations or JavaScript errors in unsupported browsers.

children.title: Prefetching and Link Optimization
children.description: Astro can prefetch linked pages on hover or viewport intersection, dramatically reducing perceived navigation time. Disabling prefetch on content-heavy sites or applying it globally on every link wastes bandwidth on pages the user never visits, hurting performance on mobile networks.

children.title: Static vs Server Output
children.description: Astro's output: 'static' pre-renders all pages at build time while output: 'server' or 'hybrid' enables on-demand server-side rendering. Using static output for pages that require real-time user data (like personalized dashboards) results in stale content; using server output unnecessarily adds hosting complexity and cost for content that never changes.

children.title: 404 and Error Pages
children.description: A 404.astro file in src/pages/ generates a custom not-found page that Astro and most hosting providers serve automatically. Without it, broken links return the host's default error page, stripping your site's branding and providing no navigation back to valid content, increasing bounce rates.

astro-dev.title: Content Collections and MDX
astro-dev.description: This layer covers managing content with collections, Markdown, and MDX. Content collections give Astro a type-safe content layer for blogs and docs.
astro-dev.challenge: Move blog posts into a typed content collection authored in MDX.

children.title: Collection Schema Definition
children.description: Content collections use Zod schemas defined in src/content/config.ts to validate frontmatter at build time, catching missing fields before the site is deployed. Without a schema, invalid or missing frontmatter passes through silently, causing undefined references in templates that only surface as blank content or crashes in production.

children.title: getCollection and getEntry APIs
children.description: The getCollection() function retrieves all validated entries from a collection while getEntry() fetches a single entry by slug, both providing fully-typed return values. Querying collections without these APIs (such as using fs.readdir) bypasses schema validation and loses TypeScript inference, making content type errors undetectable until runtime.

children.title: MDX Components and Slots
children.description: MDX allows importing and using React/Astro components directly inside Markdown files, enabling rich interactive content within editorial workflows. Forgetting to add @astrojs/mdx to the integrations array in astro.config.mjs causes .mdx files to be served as raw text, and component imports inside them throw build errors.

children.title: Remark and Rehype Plugins
children.description: Remark processes Markdown syntax while Rehype processes the resulting HTML, and both support plugins for syntax highlighting, table of contents generation, and link transformation. Applying plugins in the wrong pipeline (remark vs rehype) causes them to silently fail because they receive the wrong AST format.

children.title: Typed Frontmatter with Zod
children.description: Zod's schema primitives (z.string(), z.date(), z.array()) map directly to YAML frontmatter types, giving you compile-time and build-time guarantees about content structure. Using z.any() or skipping validation entirely means a typo in a date field produces a JavaScript Date object from an invalid string, breaking sort operations silently.

resources.label: Astro Docs — Defining a collection schema

children.title: Reference Fields Between Collections
children.description: Astro's reference() helper creates typed foreign-key relationships between collections, allowing a blog post to reference an author collection entry with full type safety. Without reference(), linking content across collections requires manual slug matching and produces untyped objects that cause TypeScript errors downstream.

children.title: render() and <Content /> Component
children.description: Calling entry.render() on a collection entry returns a <Content /> component that renders the compiled MDX/Markdown body into HTML. Trying to inject the raw body string as innerHTML bypasses Astro's sanitization and MDX component injection, producing broken output and potential XSS vulnerabilities.

children.title: Slug Generation and URL Mapping
children.description: Collection slugs are automatically generated from file names and used to construct page URLs via getStaticPaths(). Custom slug fields in the schema override file-based slugs, but omitting getStaticPaths() entries for custom slugs causes 404s for those routes since Astro never pre-builds them.

resources.label: Astro Docs — Generating pages from content
