astro-dev.title: Islands and Interactivity
astro-dev.description: This layer teaches Astro's islands architecture for adding interactive components. Islands let you ship mostly static HTML with targeted client-side interactivity.
astro-dev.challenge: Add an interactive search island to the blog using a framework component.

children.title: Client Directives
children.description: Astro's client:* directives control when and how a component is hydrated in the browser: client:load hydrates immediately, client:idle waits for the browser to be free, and client:visible waits until the component scrolls into view. Using client:load for every interactive component sends all JS upfront and defeats Astro's progressive hydration model, increasing Time to Interactive.

children.title: Island Isolation and Props Serialization
children.description: Each island is independently hydrated with its own JavaScript bundle, and props passed to islands must be JSON-serializable because they are embedded in the HTML as data attributes. Passing non-serializable values like functions, class instances, or circular references causes hydration to silently fail or throw runtime errors.

resources.label: Astro Docs — Passing props to islands

children.title: Adding the React Integration
children.description: The @astrojs/react integration adds Babel transforms and React runtime to the Astro build pipeline, enabling .tsx and .jsx components. Importing React components without this integration causes build errors; importing them with the wrong file extension causes Astro to treat them as Astro components and fail to resolve JSX syntax.

children.title: Server-Side vs Client-Side React
children.description: React components used in Astro without a client directive are rendered to static HTML on the server with no hydration — no hooks, no events, no state. Using useState or useEffect in a component that lacks a client directive causes a build error or renders a broken static shell, confusing developers who expect React's usual behavior.

children.title: Nano Stores for Shared State
children.description: Nano Stores is the recommended library for sharing reactive state between multiple islands regardless of framework, using tiny observable atoms that update all subscribers. Without a cross-island state solution, each island maintains isolated state, making features like a cart count syncing between a header island and a product page island impossible without full-page reloads.

resources.label: Astro Docs — Sharing state between islands

children.title: Script Tags and Client-Side JS
children.description: Astro's <script> tag in .astro components is bundled and deduped by the build pipeline, meaning the same script imported from multiple components only ships once. Using inline <script> tags with is:inline bypasses bundling and deduplication, potentially shipping duplicate JavaScript and breaking module-based imports.

resources.label: Astro Docs — Scripts and event handling

children.title: Mixing Frameworks in One Project
children.description: Astro uniquely supports React, Vue, Svelte, and other frameworks simultaneously in the same project, each rendered as independent islands. Attempting to pass a React component as a prop to a Vue island fails because the two frameworks use incompatible virtual DOM representations — communication must happen via Nano Stores or the DOM.

children.title: client:only Directive
children.description: The client:only directive skips server-side rendering entirely and renders the component only in the browser, required for components that depend on browser APIs like window or localStorage that don't exist in Node.js. Using client:load on such components causes server-render errors during the build because Node.js has no DOM.

astro-dev.title: Styling and Design Systems
astro-dev.description: This layer covers building a cohesive, responsive design with Tailwind in Astro. Consistent styling makes a content site look polished and trustworthy.
astro-dev.challenge: Apply a Tailwind design system with dark mode across the blog and posts.

children.title: Scoped Styles and :global()
children.description: Astro's scoped styles are confined to the component where they're declared by injecting a unique class attribute, preventing style pollution across components. Without understanding the :global() escape hatch, developers either create overly broad global styles that leak into unrelated components or struggle to style third-party HTML that Astro doesn't own.

children.title: Global Stylesheets
children.description: Global CSS files are imported in layout components or page frontmatter and apply to all HTML output without scoping. Importing a global stylesheet in every component instead of once in a shared layout causes duplicate CSS in the final bundle, increasing page weight and potentially creating specificity conflicts from duplicate declarations.

children.title: Tailwind with Astro Integration
children.description: The @astrojs/tailwind integration configures PostCSS and injects Tailwind's base, components, and utilities layers automatically into Astro's build pipeline. Manually configuring Tailwind without the integration risks missing the purge configuration for .astro files, causing unused utility classes to remain in production CSS and bloating stylesheet size.

children.title: Tailwind Configuration and Design Tokens
children.description: The tailwind.config.mjs file extends the default design system with custom colors, spacing, fonts, and breakpoints that become first-class utilities. Hardcoding values in class names instead of extending the config (e.g., w-[247px]) creates inconsistency in the design system and makes global design changes require find-and-replace across the entire codebase.

children.title: CSS Custom Properties as Design Tokens
children.description: Design tokens defined as CSS custom properties in a :root block create a single source of truth for colors, spacing, and typography that both CSS and JavaScript can read. Duplicating token values across component stylesheets means a brand color change requires updating dozens of files instead of one variable, introducing mismatches and visual inconsistency.

children.title: Dark Mode with CSS and Tailwind
children.description: Dark mode can be implemented via the prefers-color-scheme media query or Tailwind's dark: variant, which requires the darkMode: 'class' config option and toggling a class on <html>. Using inline styles for dark mode values instead of CSS variables or Tailwind utilities breaks the connection between the system color scheme preference and the site's visual state.

children.title: PostCSS and CSS Processing
children.description: Astro runs CSS through PostCSS automatically, enabling autoprefixer (for browser compatibility), nesting, and custom transforms. Skipping autoprefixer means vendor-prefixed properties like -webkit-backdrop-filter are absent from the output, causing visual failures in Safari that are invisible during Chrome-only development.

children.title: CSS Layers and Specificity Control
children.description: CSS @layer allows organizing styles into named layers with explicit priority order, preventing specificity wars between reset styles, component styles, and utility classes. Without layers, adding a Tailwind utility to override a component style requires increasingly specific selectors or !important, eroding maintainability.

resources.label: CSS-Tricks — A Complete Guide to CSS Cascade Layers

astro-dev.title: Server Rendering and Endpoints
astro-dev.description: This layer covers server-side rendering, adapters, and API endpoints. On-demand rendering lets Astro handle dynamic data and form submissions.
astro-dev.challenge: Add a server-rendered contact form backed by an Astro API endpoint.

children.title: SSR Adapters
children.description: Astro requires an adapter to deploy server-rendered output to a specific runtime environment: @astrojs/node for Node.js servers, @astrojs/netlify for Netlify Functions, and @astrojs/vercel for Vercel Edge. Using the wrong adapter or forgetting to add one when switching to output: 'server' causes the build to succeed but the deployed site to crash immediately at the first server-rendered request.

children.title: Request and Response Objects
children.description: In SSR mode, Astro exposes the standard web Request object in page frontmatter, giving access to headers, cookies, and URL parameters at request time. Reading request headers in static output mode throws a runtime error because there is no request at build time; this mismatch is a common source of confusion when migrating from static to hybrid rendering.

children.title: REST Endpoints in src/pages
children.description: Astro treats .ts and .js files in src/pages/ as API endpoints when they export GET, POST, or other HTTP method handlers, returning Response objects. Exporting a default function instead of named HTTP method exports causes Astro to ignore the file as an endpoint, resulting in a 404 for API routes that appear to be correctly placed.

children.title: Middleware in Astro
children.description: Astro middleware runs server-side code before every request, enabling authentication, logging, and request transformation via a src/middleware.ts file exporting an onRequest function. Not calling next() within middleware halts the request chain and returns no response, causing all pages to return empty or hang indefinitely.

children.title: Node.js Adapter and Streaming
children.description: The @astrojs/node adapter enables running Astro as an Express-compatible Node.js server with support for HTTP streaming, enabling faster time-to-first-byte for server-rendered pages. Not enabling streaming when using the Node adapter defaults to buffering entire responses, increasing latency for content-heavy pages and reducing perceived performance.

children.title: Environment Variables and Secrets
children.description: Astro exposes environment variables prefixed with PUBLIC_ to the browser and keeps all others server-only, preventing accidental secret exposure. Using a non-PUBLIC_ variable in a client-side script or island causes it to be undefined in the browser; using a PUBLIC_ prefix on a secret variable exposes it in the HTML source to all users.

children.title: Cookie API in Astro SSR
children.description: Astro's Astro.cookies API provides a typed, ergonomic wrapper for reading and setting HTTP cookies in server-rendered pages and endpoints. Manipulating cookies via raw Set-Cookie headers bypasses Astro's serialization helpers, making it easy to produce malformed cookie strings that browsers silently reject, breaking session persistence.

children.title: Authentication Patterns
children.description: Authentication in Astro SSR typically combines session tokens stored in cookies with middleware that validates the token on every request and injects user data into Astro.locals. Checking auth inside individual page components instead of middleware means protected pages are vulnerable if the component check is accidentally removed during refactoring.

astro-dev.title: Data, CMS, and Integrations
astro-dev.description: This layer covers pulling data from APIs and a headless CMS into Astro. External content sources let non-developers manage the site safely.
astro-dev.challenge: Source blog posts from a headless CMS with optimized cover images.

children.title: Headless CMS with Astro
children.description: Astro fetches content from headless CMSes like Contentful, Sanity, or Storyblok at build time using their REST or GraphQL APIs, enabling full static generation of CMS-driven content. Fetching CMS data client-side instead of at build time exposes API keys in the browser bundle and eliminates the performance benefit of pre-rendered HTML.

children.title: Incremental Static Regeneration
children.description: Astro's hybrid output mode allows individual pages to opt into SSR while keeping others static, enabling on-demand revalidation of CMS content without full rebuilds. Using purely static output with a CMS requires a full redeploy for every content update, making editorial workflows impractical for frequently changing content.

children.title: fetch() in Astro Frontmatter
children.description: The native fetch() API is available in Astro frontmatter for server-side data fetching, running in Node.js during build or on the server during SSR requests. Using fetch() inside a client-side script instead of frontmatter sends requests from the user's browser, leaking API endpoints, increasing latency, and exposing credentials stored in environment variables.

children.title: Parallel Data Fetching with Promise.all
children.description: Multiple independent fetch() calls in Astro frontmatter should be executed in parallel using Promise.all() to avoid waterfall latency, where each request waits for the previous to complete. Sequential awaits for independent API calls multiply request latency, dramatically increasing build times for sites with many content types or API-driven pages.

children.title: Official Integrations Ecosystem
children.description: Astro's integration API allows first and third-party plugins to modify the build pipeline, add file processors, inject scripts, and configure the dev server via astro.config.mjs. Directly modifying Vite config to add behavior instead of using the integration API risks breaking Astro's internal assumptions and causes subtle build failures when Astro's own Vite plugins run in conflicting order.

children.title: Image Optimization with astro:assets
children.description: Astro's built-in component from astro:assets automatically generates optimized WebP/AVIF formats, resizes images, and adds width/height to prevent layout shift. Serving raw unoptimized images with tags bypasses this pipeline, leading to multi-megabyte images being sent to mobile users and failing Core Web Vitals LCP thresholds.

children.title: Typing External API Responses
children.description: Defining TypeScript interfaces for external API response shapes and using them with type assertions or Zod validation ensures fetch() responses are consumed with type safety. Casting API responses to any eliminates the compiler's ability to catch field renames or schema changes in the API, causing runtime property access errors that only appear in production.

children.title: Astro.locals for Request-Scoped Data
children.description: Astro.locals is a typed, request-scoped storage object populated in middleware and accessible in any page or endpoint during the same request lifecycle. Storing per-request data in module-level variables instead of Astro.locals causes data to leak between concurrent requests in SSR, producing one user's session data appearing in another user's response.

astro-dev.title: Performance and SEO
astro-dev.description: This layer focuses on Astro's performance strengths and search optimization. Fast, well-indexed sites are the core promise of the Astro framework.
astro-dev.challenge: Add an RSS feed and sitemap and tune the blog to top Lighthouse scores.

children.title: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)
children.description: LCP measures how long the largest visible content element takes to render, directly impacting perceived load speed and Google search rankings. Serving large unoptimized hero images, delaying them with JavaScript, or failing to add fetchpriority='high' to the LCP image causes poor scores even on sites with fast server response times.

children.title: Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)
children.description: CLS measures unexpected layout movements during page load, penalizing pages where content jumps as images load or ads insert above existing content. Omitting explicit width and height attributes on images and iframes prevents the browser from reserving space, causing content below to shift down and degrading the CLS score.

children.title: Running Lighthouse in CI
children.description: Lighthouse CI integrates automated performance, accessibility, and SEO auditing into deployment pipelines, failing builds when scores drop below defined thresholds. Running Lighthouse only manually during development allows regressions to ship unnoticed; a component that adds 200KB of JavaScript can drop the performance score by 20 points without anyone catching it.

children.title: Performance Budget
children.description: A performance budget sets maximum thresholds for JavaScript bundle size, image weight, and time-to-interactive, enforced through Lighthouse CI or bundler plugins. Without a budget, incremental feature additions each seem minor but compound over time, resulting in a site that gradually degrades from fast to slow without any single change being the obvious culprit.

children.title: Meta Tags and Open Graph
children.description: HTML meta tags for description, Open Graph (og:title, og:image), and Twitter Card provide search engines and social platforms with structured page summaries. Missing or duplicate meta descriptions cause search engines to generate their own snippets from page content, often resulting in irrelevant or truncated previews that reduce click-through rates.

children.title: Sitemap Generation
children.description: The @astrojs/sitemap integration automatically generates a sitemap.xml listing all public URLs, helping search engine crawlers discover and index all pages. Without a sitemap, crawlers rely solely on link discovery, meaning pages that are only accessible via JavaScript navigation or are deep in the site structure may never be indexed.

children.title: Font Loading Strategy
children.description: Loading web fonts with font-display: swap prevents invisible text during font load (FOIT) and instead shows a fallback font immediately, reducing perceived latency. Using the default font-display behavior blocks text rendering until the custom font downloads, causing measurable LCP and FCP regressions on slow connections.

children.title: Critical CSS and Resource Hints
children.description: Critical CSS inlines the styles needed for above-the-fold rendering directly in <head>, eliminating render-blocking stylesheets for the initial viewport. Without critical CSS extraction, the browser must download, parse, and apply all stylesheets before painting any content, causing higher FCP times especially on mobile connections.

astro-dev.title: Testing and Deployment
astro-dev.description: This layer covers testing the site and deploying it to production. Tests and an adapter-based deploy make the project a real, reliable product.
astro-dev.challenge: Add tests and deploy the finished Astro blog with preview builds enabled.

children.title: Playwright Setup and Configuration
children.description: Playwright's playwright.config.ts defines browsers, base URLs, timeouts, and test parallelism, serving as the single source of truth for the test environment. Hardcoding localhost URLs in individual test files rather than using baseURL in config causes all tests to break when the dev server port changes, requiring mass find-and-replace across the test suite.

children.title: Page Object Model Pattern
children.description: The Page Object Model encapsulates page-specific selectors and interactions into reusable classes, decoupling test logic from DOM structure. Writing raw locators directly in every test file means a single CSS class rename requires updating every test that touches the affected element, causing tests to become a maintenance burden instead of a safety net.

children.title: Accessibility Testing in Playwright
children.description: Playwright's getByRole() and getByLabel() locators test by ARIA semantics rather than CSS classes, simultaneously asserting that the accessibility tree is correct. Using CSS selectors like .btn or #submit in tests passes even when the element has no accessible name, hiding accessibility regressions that break screen reader users.

children.title: Visual Regression Testing
children.description: Playwright's screenshot comparison feature captures pixel-level snapshots and fails tests when visual differences exceed a threshold, catching unintended UI changes from CSS or component updates. Without visual regression tests, a CSS refactor that shifts layout by a few pixels ships unnoticed until users report visual bugs.

children.title: Netlify Adapter and Build Config
children.description: The @astrojs/netlify adapter configures Astro to output Netlify Functions for SSR endpoints and Edge Functions for middleware, with the build command and publish directory set in netlify.toml. Misconfiguring the publish directory (e.g., pointing to the project root instead of dist/) causes Netlify to deploy the source code rather than the built output, producing a blank site.

children.title: Continuous Deployment and Preview Deploys
children.description: Netlify automatically builds and deploys preview URLs for every pull request, enabling review of changes in a production-like environment before merging. Skipping preview deploy review means undetected build environment differences — such as missing environment variables or platform-specific behavior — only surface after merging to main, requiring emergency rollbacks.

children.title: GitHub Actions for Astro
children.description: GitHub Actions workflows can install dependencies, run Playwright tests against a built Astro site, and block merges when tests fail, creating a fully automated quality gate. Running tests only locally and relying on manual review allows broken builds to merge when developers are under time pressure, degrading the reliability of the deployment pipeline.

children.title: Environment Variable Management in CI
children.description: Production secrets must be injected into CI build environments via repository or organization secrets rather than committed to source control, with Astro's PUBLIC_ prefix controlling which variables are safe to embed in client HTML. Storing secrets in the repository — even in environment-specific .env files — exposes them in version history and to anyone with repository read access.

laravel-vue.title: PHP Fundamentals
laravel-vue.description: This layer establishes modern PHP skills that Laravel is built on. A solid language base makes the framework far easier to adopt.
laravel-vue.challenge: Write a PHP script that processes a CSV of products into formatted output.

children.title: Type System and Type Juggling
children.description: PHP performs automatic type coercion in loose comparisons, which causes subtle bugs — '0' == false evaluates to true. Understanding strict vs. loose comparison and PHP 8's union types prevents entire categories of authentication and conditional logic bugs that are notoriously hard to debug in production.

children.title: Namespaces and Autoloading
children.description: Namespaces prevent class name collisions across packages and map directly to directory structure when using PSR-4 autoloading via Composer. If you misunderstand this, you'll spend hours chasing 'Class not found' errors and be unable to properly organize large Laravel applications into logical modules.

children.title: Interfaces and Abstract Classes
children.description: Interfaces define contracts that decouple implementation from usage — Laravel's entire service container relies on this pattern. Misunderstanding the difference between interfaces and abstract classes leads to tightly coupled code that is nearly impossible to unit test or swap implementations in later.

children.title: Traits and Code Reuse
children.description: Traits let you compose shared behavior across unrelated class hierarchies — Laravel's own Notifiable and SoftDeletes are traits. Without understanding trait conflict resolution and precedence rules, you'll encounter cryptic method override errors when multiple traits define the same method.

children.title: composer.lock and Reproducible Builds
children.description: The composer.lock file pins exact versions of every dependency and sub-dependency, ensuring that all environments run identical code. Committing this file to version control is critical — without it, a CI server or teammate may resolve a slightly different package version that introduces breaking changes your local machine never saw.

children.title: Semantic Versioning and Version Constraints
children.description: Composer uses semver constraints like ^2.0 (compatible with 2.x) and ~2.1 (compatible with 2.1.x) to resolve packages. Misreading these constraints is how teams accidentally allow major-version bumps that break APIs, or conversely lock themselves into outdated packages with known security vulnerabilities.

children.title: Array Functions and Performance
children.description: PHP's array_map, array_filter, and array_reduce are functional tools that operate on full in-memory arrays, while generators (yield) produce values lazily. Choosing array functions over generators when processing large datasets causes memory exhaustion — a common mistake when handling CSV imports or large query result sets.

children.title: String Handling and Multibyte Safety
children.description: Standard PHP string functions like strlen and substr operate on bytes, not characters — they produce wrong results and data corruption when handling UTF-8 text with multibyte characters. Always use the mbstring extension equivalents (mb_strlen, mb_substr) when your application handles non-ASCII content.

laravel-vue.title: Laravel Basics and Routing
laravel-vue.description: This layer introduces Laravel structure, routing, controllers, and views. Understanding the request lifecycle is the foundation for the backend.
laravel-vue.challenge: Build a Laravel app that lists products through routes, controllers, and Blade.

children.title: Route Model Binding
children.description: Implicit route model binding automatically resolves Eloquent models from route parameters by matching the parameter name to the model's primary key. When you bypass this feature and manually query models in controllers, you lose automatic 404 handling and create redundant code — missing the binding also makes it easy to expose sequential ID enumeration vulnerabilities.

resources.label: Laravel Docs — Customizing the Resolution Logic

children.title: Named Routes and URL Generation
children.description: Named routes decouple URL strings from your application logic, letting you change a URL path in one place without hunting through views and controllers. Hardcoding URL strings instead means a single URL refactor breaks links across dozens of Blade templates and frontend components simultaneously.

children.title: How Middleware Wraps the Request Lifecycle
children.description: Laravel's middleware stack is an onion model — each middleware wraps the next, running code both before and after the inner handler. Misunderstanding this means you place session writes or authentication checks after the response has already been sent, producing bugs where auth state changes don't persist or responses are already committed when you try to modify headers.

children.title: Route Groups and Middleware Inheritance
children.description: Route groups let you apply shared middleware, prefixes, and namespace scoping to sets of routes in one declaration. Without grouping, developers manually repeat middleware assignments on individual routes and inevitably miss one — leaving, for example, an admin route unauthenticated because it was added outside the protected group.

resources.label: Laravel Docs — Assigning Middleware to Routes

children.title: Artisan Make Commands and Code Generation
children.description: Artisan's make:* commands generate correctly namespaced, properly structured boilerplate files that follow Laravel conventions. Writing these files by hand introduces subtle namespace mismatches and missing parent class imports that cause cryptic runtime errors and break framework features like automatic controller discovery.

children.title: Custom Artisan Commands for Automation
children.description: Custom Artisan commands let you encapsulate operational tasks (data seeding, cache warming, report generation) as first-class CLI tools runnable in any environment. Putting this logic in controllers or web routes means it can only run through HTTP requests, making scheduled execution and DevOps automation awkward or impossible.

children.title: Automatic Constructor Injection
children.description: Laravel's service container inspects controller and class constructors via reflection and automatically resolves and injects type-hinted dependencies. If you manually instantiate dependencies with 'new' inside constructors, you bypass the container and make unit testing impossible because you cannot substitute mock implementations.

children.title: Facades vs. Dependency Injection
children.description: Laravel facades are static proxies to container-resolved instances, providing a terse syntax at the cost of hidden coupling and harder testability. Preferring constructor injection over facades makes class dependencies explicit, enables IDE static analysis, and allows true unit tests without booting the full Laravel application.

resources.label: Laravel Docs — Facades vs Dependency Injection

laravel-vue.title: Eloquent and Databases
laravel-vue.description: This layer covers modeling and querying data with the Eloquent ORM. Eloquent and migrations give the app reliable persistent storage.
laravel-vue.challenge: Model products and categories in MySQL with Eloquent relationships and seeders.

children.title: Mass Assignment and the fillable/guarded Properties
children.description: Eloquent's mass assignment protection prevents users from injecting arbitrary database columns through form submissions by requiring you to whitelist fields in $fillable or blacklist them in $guarded. Setting $guarded = [] to 'allow everything' is a critical security mistake that has led to privilege escalation vulnerabilities in real applications.

children.title: Eloquent Relationships and Lazy vs Eager Loading
children.description: Eloquent relationships define how models are joined across tables, but accessing a relationship property inside a loop triggers a new SQL query per iteration — the N+1 problem. Using with() to eager load relationships is essential for performance; the difference between 1 query and 1000 queries for a page listing 100 records is the difference between a fast app and a slow one.

children.title: Migration Versioning and the up/down Contract
children.description: Each migration's down() method must be the exact inverse of up() — dropping the table or column that up() creates. Without a correct down() method, rolling back migrations leaves your database in a partially modified state, and running fresh migrations on staging or CI breaks because the schema is inconsistent.

children.title: Column Modifiers and Index Strategy
children.description: Adding nullable(), unsigned(), default(), and index() modifiers in migrations controls both the database schema and query performance. Missing an index on a foreign key or frequently queried column causes full table scans that are invisible in development with small data sets but catastrophic in production with millions of rows.

children.title: Query Builder vs Eloquent Trade-offs
children.description: Laravel's query builder returns plain arrays/objects and is faster than Eloquent for bulk operations, while Eloquent returns model instances with events, observers, and relationship methods. Using Eloquent for bulk inserts of 10,000 rows fires model events on each row and is dramatically slower than a single query builder insert.

children.title: Preventing SQL Injection with Parameter Binding
children.description: The query builder and Eloquent automatically use PDO parameter binding, which separates SQL structure from user data and prevents SQL injection. Using DB::statement() or DB::select() with string interpolation of user input bypasses this protection completely — a mistake that exposes the entire database to attack.

children.title: Model Factories for Realistic Test Data
children.description: Factories use Faker to generate realistic fake data for each model attribute, enabling consistent, isolated test setups that don't depend on manually created records. Without factories, tests depend on shared database state that can change between runs, causing intermittent test failures that are extremely difficult to diagnose.

children.title: Seeders and Environment-Specific Data
children.description: Database seeders populate the database with initial or reference data and should be split between seeders that always run (roles, permissions) and those only for development (demo users, sample posts). Mixing them causes production deployments to accidentally create test accounts with weak passwords or expose demo data to real users.

laravel-vue.title: Validation, Forms, and Auth
laravel-vue.description: This layer covers request validation, forms, and built-in authentication. Secure forms and login are essential to a usable application.
laravel-vue.challenge: Add product create and edit forms with validation and login-gated access.

children.title: Form Request Classes and Authorization
children.description: Form Requests encapsulate both validation rules and authorization logic for a specific HTTP request, keeping controllers thin and testable. Putting validation inline in controllers couples the validation to the controller method, making it impossible to reuse the same rules in multiple places and dramatically harder to write isolated unit tests.

children.title: Custom Validation Rules
children.description: Laravel allows custom Rule objects and closures for domain-specific validation logic, like checking that a username is unique across a case-insensitive index or that a date falls within a billing cycle. Without custom rules, developers duplicate complex validation logic across multiple controllers or — worse — validate after persisting data and rely on catching database exceptions.

children.title: Laravel Sanctum for SPA Authentication
children.description: Sanctum provides cookie-based session authentication for SPAs and token-based authentication for mobile/API clients from a single package. Confusing Sanctum's SPA mode (cookie + CSRF) with its token mode and mixing strategies causes authentication to fail silently on cross-origin requests or exposes APIs to CSRF attacks.

children.title: Gates and Policies for Authorization
children.description: Gates define simple boolean authorization checks while Policies group authorization logic for a specific model, both powered by the same underlying Gate service. Skipping authorization and relying only on route middleware for access control leaves application logic (like 'can this user edit this specific post?') unguarded at the controller or model level.

children.title: How Laravel's CSRF Token Works
children.description: Laravel generates a CSRF token per session and validates it on every state-changing request (POST, PUT, DELETE), comparing the token in the request body or header against the session value. Disabling CSRF protection on routes that modify data, or failing to send the token from your Vue frontend, opens those routes to cross-site request forgery attacks from any malicious website.

children.title: Excluding Routes from CSRF Verification
children.description: Laravel's VerifyCsrfToken middleware has an $except array for routes that legitimately receive requests without CSRF tokens, such as webhooks from payment processors. Excluding broad patterns like 'api/*' in web.php routes to work around CSRF issues rather than properly configuring your SPA is a common mistake that removes protection from the entire API surface.

resources.label: Laravel Docs — Excluding URIs from CSRF Protection

children.title: Blade Directives and XSS Prevention
children.description: Blade's {{ }} syntax escapes HTML entities by default, preventing cross-site scripting, while {!! !!} outputs raw unescaped HTML. Misusing {!! !!} on user-controlled content is one of the most common XSS vulnerabilities in Laravel applications — it should only ever be used for trusted, pre-sanitized HTML you generate yourself.

children.title: Blade Components and Slots
children.description: Blade components provide a composable UI component system with typed props and named slots, similar to Vue components but rendered server-side. Understanding the distinction helps you decide which UI logic belongs in Blade (server-rendered, SEO-critical) vs. Vue (interactive, client-side), preventing the mistake of duplicating component logic across both systems.

laravel-vue.title: Vue 3 Fundamentals
laravel-vue.description: This layer introduces building reactive interfaces with Vue 3 and the Composition API. Vue provides the dynamic frontend half of the stack.
laravel-vue.challenge: Build a Vue product filter component with reactive search and sorting.

children.title: ref vs reactive — Choosing the Right Primitive
children.description: ref wraps any value in a reactive container accessed via .value, while reactive creates a deep reactive proxy for objects — but reactive loses reactivity when destructured. Destructuring a reactive object into plain variables is a common Vue 3 mistake that silently breaks template updates, leaving the UI stale while the underlying data has changed.

children.title: computed Properties and Side-Effect-Free Getters
children.description: Computed properties are cached based on their reactive dependencies and only re-evaluate when those dependencies change — making them far more efficient than methods for derived state. Writing side effects (API calls, mutations) inside computed properties causes unpredictable bugs because computeds may be re-evaluated at any time by the reactivity system.

resources.label: Vue 3 Docs — Computed Caching vs Methods

children.title: Composables — Encapsulating Stateful Logic
children.description: Composables are functions that use Vue's Composition API to encapsulate and reuse stateful logic — like useFetch, useForm, or useDebounce. Without composables, cross-component logic lives in mixins which have implicit property injection, name conflicts, and no TypeScript inference — problems that composables eliminate by making dependencies explicit.

children.title: Lifecycle Hooks in the Composition API
children.description: Composition API lifecycle hooks (onMounted, onUnmounted, onBeforeUpdate) must be called synchronously inside setup() — calling them inside conditionals or async callbacks causes them to be silently ignored. Missing onUnmounted cleanup for event listeners and timers is the most common source of memory leaks in Vue 3 applications.

children.title: Vite Dev Server and Hot Module Replacement
children.description: Vite's dev server serves files as native ES modules over HTTP, enabling near-instant startup regardless of project size, with HMR that patches only the changed module without a full page reload. Misconfiguring the Vite server proxy for API requests to Laravel causes CORS errors in development that don't appear in production, leading developers to incorrectly modify Laravel's CORS settings.

children.title: Vite + Laravel Integration via laravel-vite-plugin
children.description: The laravel-vite-plugin bridges Vite's asset manifest with Laravel's @vite Blade directive, injecting the correct script and link tags for both dev server and production builds. Without understanding the manifest-based asset loading, developers hardcode script paths that work in one environment but serve stale or missing assets in the other.

children.title: History Mode and Server-Side Fallback
children.description: Vue Router's history mode produces clean URLs without hash fragments, but requires the server to return index.html for all non-file paths so the client-side router can handle navigation. Without configuring Laravel's catch-all route or the web server fallback, direct URL access to any route other than '/' returns a 404 from the server.

resources.label: Vue Router Docs — HTML5 History Mode

children.title: Navigation Guards and Auth Protection
children.description: Router navigation guards (beforeEach, beforeEnter) intercept route transitions and let you check authentication or permissions before rendering, redirecting unauthenticated users to login. Relying solely on hiding navigation links to protect routes rather than implementing guards means any user can navigate directly to a protected URL and reach restricted content.

laravel-vue.title: Building APIs with Laravel
laravel-vue.description: This layer covers exposing Laravel as a JSON API for the Vue frontend. A clean API decouples the backend from the client.
laravel-vue.challenge: Build a JSON API for products with Sanctum token authentication.

children.title: API Resources and Transforming Eloquent Models
children.description: Laravel API Resources transform Eloquent models and collections into JSON-serializable arrays, acting as a presentation layer that decouples your database schema from your API contract. Without resources, adding a new database column automatically exposes it in API responses — leaking sensitive fields like password hashes, internal flags, or PII to frontend clients.

children.title: API Versioning Strategy
children.description: Versioning your API via URL prefixes (/api/v1/) or headers lets you evolve the contract without breaking existing clients. Without versioning, any breaking change to a response structure forces all consumers to update simultaneously — impossible when public mobile apps are involved — and teams resort to adding fields indefinitely rather than cleaning up outdated designs.

children.title: Consistent Error Response Structure
children.description: Laravel's exception handler can be customized to return consistent JSON error envelopes (with status, message, and errors fields) for all API errors. Without this, different error types return different response shapes — Laravel's default returns HTML for server errors and JSON for validation errors — breaking any frontend code that expects a uniform error format.

children.title: HTTP Status Codes and Semantic Responses
children.description: Using the correct HTTP status codes (201 Created, 204 No Content, 422 Unprocessable Entity, 404 Not Found) communicates intent to clients and allows HTTP caching, retry logic, and monitoring tools to behave correctly. Returning 200 OK for everything and encoding success/failure in the JSON body breaks standardized tooling and forces every client to implement custom error detection logic.

children.title: Token Abilities and Scoped Permissions
children.description: Sanctum allows minting tokens with specific ability strings (like 'read:orders', 'write:products') and checking those abilities in middleware or controllers with tokenCan(). Without ability scoping, every token grants full API access — meaning a read-only dashboard integration has the same access as an admin token, violating the principle of least privilege.

resources.label: Laravel Docs — Sanctum API Token Authentication

children.title: Token Expiration and Revocation
children.description: Sanctum tokens are stored in the personal_access_tokens table and can be revoked by deleting the record — making logout and compromise response straightforward. Without implementing token expiration (via expires_at) or a revocation endpoint, stolen tokens remain valid indefinitely and there is no way to force a user out of all sessions.

children.title: Laravel's Built-in Rate Limiter
children.description: Laravel's RateLimiter facade lets you define named rate limit rules (requests per minute per user or IP) applied via the throttle middleware. Without rate limiting on authentication and sensitive API endpoints, your application is vulnerable to credential stuffing attacks and API abuse that can exhaust server resources.

children.title: Custom Rate Limit Keys
children.description: Rate limits can be keyed by authenticated user ID, IP address, or any combination — letting you apply stricter limits to unauthenticated requests while allowing higher throughput for trusted users. Using only IP-based limiting fails for users behind shared IPs (offices, universities) who hit the limit collectively, while rate limiting only by user ID exposes unauthenticated endpoints to abuse.

laravel-vue.title: Connecting Vue to Laravel
laravel-vue.description: This layer wires the Vue frontend to the Laravel API and manages client state. Integration produces a complete single-page application.
laravel-vue.challenge: Build a Vue SPA that lists and manages products through the Laravel API.

children.title: Axios Interceptors for Global Auth and Error Handling
children.description: Axios request and response interceptors let you inject authentication headers on every outgoing request and centrally handle 401/403 responses (redirecting to login) without repeating the logic in every API call. Without interceptors, developers copy-paste auth header injection and error handling into dozens of component methods, guaranteeing that some calls are missing it.

children.title: CSRF Token Injection from Laravel Cookie
children.description: When using Sanctum's SPA cookie authentication, Axios must read the XSRF-TOKEN cookie set by Laravel's /sanctum/csrf-cookie endpoint and send it as the X-XSRF-TOKEN header on all mutating requests. Forgetting to call the csrf-cookie endpoint before the first authenticated request, or misconfiguring withCredentials, results in every POST returning a 419 CSRF token mismatch error.

children.title: Pinia Stores — Actions, Getters, and State
children.description: Pinia stores are the recommended Vue 3 state management solution, with state as reactive data, getters as computed properties, and actions as async methods for API calls. Putting API calls directly in Vue components rather than Pinia actions scatters data fetching logic across the component tree, making it impossible to share or cache the same data across multiple components.

children.title: Pinia vs Vuex — Why Pinia Wins for Vue 3
children.description: Pinia has native TypeScript support, no mutations (simpler API), modular stores without nested modules, and Devtools integration with time-travel debugging. Choosing Vuex for a new Vue 3 project means working against the grain of the ecosystem — Vuex 4 has no plans for major updates and Pinia is now the officially recommended store.

children.title: Inertia's Page Component Model
children.description: Inertia.js eliminates the need for a separate API layer by letting Laravel controllers return Inertia responses that render Vue page components directly, passing server-side data as props. Misunderstanding Inertia's model — treating it like a REST API and making Axios calls from Inertia pages — defeats its purpose and creates redundant complexity.

children.title: Shared Data and Partial Reloads
children.description: Inertia's shared data (via HandleInertiaRequests middleware) injects global props like the authenticated user into every page component without repeating the query in every controller. Partial reloads let you refresh only specific props without a full page reload — misusing full reloads for form submissions throws away scroll position and causes noticeable page flicker.

children.title: Laravel CORS Middleware and the fruitcake/cors Package
children.description: Laravel includes CORS handling via the HandleCors middleware (powered internally by fruitcake/laravel-cors) configured in config/cors.php. Setting allowed_origins to '*' on a credentialed request is rejected by browsers per the CORS spec — a misunderstanding that leads developers to believe CORS can't work with credentials, when the fix is to specify exact origins.

children.title: Preflight Requests and OPTIONS Method Handling
children.description: Browsers send an OPTIONS preflight request before any cross-origin request with custom headers or non-simple methods, expecting CORS headers in the response before sending the real request. If Laravel's CORS middleware is not applied to OPTIONS routes or the web server terminates OPTIONS requests before they reach Laravel, all authenticated cross-origin API calls fail with a cryptic network error.

laravel-vue.title: Queues, Jobs, and Email
laravel-vue.description: This layer covers background processing and sending email in Laravel. Offloading slow work keeps the app fast and responsive.
laravel-vue.challenge: Send an order confirmation email through a queued job when checkout completes.

children.title: Queue Drivers and the Sync Driver Trap
children.description: Laravel supports multiple queue backends (Redis, SQS, database) and a 'sync' driver that executes jobs immediately in the same process — useful only for testing. Deploying with QUEUE_CONNECTION=sync means slow operations (email, image processing) block the HTTP response and users wait, which developers only discover under production load because local development feels the same either way.

children.title: Job Retries, Failures, and the Dead Letter Queue
children.description: Jobs can specify a maximum number of retries and an exponential backoff strategy before being moved to the failed_jobs table. Without configuring retry limits, a job that always fails (due to a bug or downstream service outage) loops indefinitely in the queue, consuming worker capacity and potentially growing the queue to millions of entries.

resources.label: Laravel Docs — Dealing With Failed Jobs
Laravel Docs — Max Job Attempts and Timeout

children.title: Redis for Caching vs Queuing — Separate Databases
children.description: Laravel uses Redis for both the cache and queue systems but defaults both to database 0, meaning flushing the cache also destroys all queued jobs. Always configure separate Redis databases (REDIS_CACHE_DB=1, REDIS_QUEUE_DB=0) to prevent this catastrophic data loss, which is an invisible misconfiguration that only manifests when cache is cleared in production.

children.title: Laravel Horizon for Queue Monitoring
children.description: Horizon provides a real-time dashboard and configuration-driven worker management for Redis queues, letting you set per-queue worker counts, monitor throughput, and get alerts on slow or failing jobs. Running raw artisan queue:work without Horizon means you have no visibility into queue health and must manually restart workers after deployments.

children.title: Mailable Classes and Markdown Templates
children.description: Laravel Mailable classes encapsulate email construction — subject, recipients, headers, and template data — as reusable objects that can be queued, tested, and previewed in the browser. Building emails with raw PHP mail() or putting mail logic directly in controllers makes email code impossible to test without actually sending and breaks the single-responsibility principle.

children.title: Queueing Emails and Mail Drivers
children.description: Implementing ShouldQueue on a Mailable defers email sending to a queue worker, preventing slow SMTP connections from blocking HTTP responses. Sending email synchronously on every request is a common performance bottleneck — users experience 1-3 second delays on any action that triggers an email, and SMTP failures cause HTTP 500 errors.

children.title: Multi-Channel Notifications
children.description: Laravel's notification system sends the same notification via multiple channels (mail, Slack, SMS via Vonage, database) from a single class by implementing different via() channel methods. Building separate Mailable classes, Slack webhook calls, and SMS services for the same event creates a maintenance burden — a change to the notification content must be made in multiple places.

children.title: Database Notifications and Read State
children.description: The database notification channel stores notifications in a notifications table with a read_at timestamp, enabling in-app notification UIs with unread counts. Without the database channel, in-app notifications must be built from scratch with custom tables, losing the unified API and the morphable relationship that lets any model have notifications.

resources.label: Laravel Docs — Marking Notifications as Read

laravel-vue.title: Advanced Patterns and Performance
laravel-vue.description: This layer covers caching, optimization, and clean architecture in larger apps. These practices keep a growing Laravel codebase fast and maintainable.
topics: Eager loading and N plus one
laravel-vue.challenge: Profile and optimize the product API with eager loading and Redis caching.

children.title: Service Classes and Thin Controllers
children.description: Service classes extract complex business logic out of controllers, making them independently testable and reusable across HTTP requests, artisan commands, and queue jobs. Fat controllers — where logic for querying, processing, and responding all lives together — are the single biggest cause of untestable code and duplication in Laravel projects.

children.title: Repository Pattern and Data Access Abstraction
children.description: The repository pattern wraps Eloquent queries behind an interface, allowing you to swap data sources (database, API, cache) without changing business logic. While often over-engineered for small projects, it becomes essential when multiple data sources feed the same domain model or when you need to unit test business logic without hitting the database.

children.title: Cache Tags and Invalidation Strategy
children.description: Laravel's cache supports tags (on Redis and Memcached) that let you invalidate a group of related cache entries with a single call — essential for flushing all cached data related to a specific user or resource. Without tags, developers use time-based expiration and serve stale data, or flush the entire cache on every write which defeats the performance benefits.

children.title: remember() and the Cache-Aside Pattern
children.description: Cache::remember() implements the cache-aside pattern atomically — check the cache, return if hit, otherwise execute the callback and store the result. Manually implementing check-then-set outside of remember() creates a race condition where two simultaneous requests both miss the cache, both hit the database, and both write the same value — the stampede problem.

children.title: v-memo and Preventing Unnecessary Re-renders
children.description: v-memo memoizes a template subtree and skips re-rendering it unless the specified dependency array changes, similar to React.memo — critical for large lists where each row contains computed content. Without memoization, every parent state change causes the entire list to diff and re-render, causing visible lag in lists with more than a few hundred items.

children.title: defineAsyncComponent and Code Splitting
children.description: defineAsyncComponent wraps a dynamic import, causing Vite/webpack to split that component into a separate chunk loaded on demand — reducing initial bundle size. Without async components, every route's component is bundled together and shipped to the user on first load, even if they never visit most routes.

resources.label: Vue Router Docs — Lazy Loading Routes

children.title: Redis Pub/Sub for Real-Time Events
children.description: Redis Pub/Sub combined with Laravel Echo and Laravel Reverb (or Pusher) enables real-time server-to-browser push notifications without polling. Polling APIs from the Vue frontend on a timer is a common but wasteful alternative — it wastes server resources, introduces latency, and doesn't scale, whereas Pub/Sub events are pushed instantly.

children.title: Redis Connection Pooling and Persistent Connections
children.description: Laravel uses phpredis or Predis to connect to Redis, and each queue worker or web request opens a connection that should be reused — connection pooling prevents the overhead of establishing a new TCP connection for every Redis command. Misconfiguring persistent connections or running too many workers without a connection limit causes Redis to hit its max client ceiling and reject new connections.

laravel-vue.title: Testing and Deployment
laravel-vue.description: This layer covers testing the stack and deploying it to production. Tests and a deploy pipeline turn the project into a maintainable product.
topics: Feature and unit tests with Pest
laravel-vue.challenge: Add tests and deploy the Laravel and Vue store to a cloud platform.

children.title: PHPUnit Feature Tests vs Unit Tests
children.description: Feature tests boot the full Laravel application and test complete HTTP request/response cycles, while unit tests target isolated classes without the framework. Writing everything as feature tests is slow and hides architecture problems — if a class can't be unit tested in isolation, it's a sign that it has too many dependencies and should be refactored.

children.title: Database Testing with RefreshDatabase and Transactions
children.description: The RefreshDatabase trait rolls back database changes after each test using transactions (or re-runs migrations), ensuring tests don't pollute each other's state. Without it, tests that create database records leave data behind that causes subsequent tests to find unexpected rows, producing order-dependent test failures that only appear when the full suite is run.

children.title: Mail::fake() and Queue::fake() for Side Effects
children.description: Laravel's fake implementations (Mail::fake(), Queue::fake(), Event::fake()) replace real senders/dispatchers during tests with in-memory collectors you can assert against, without actually sending emails or processing jobs. Without fakes, tests that trigger email sends hit real SMTP servers, making tests slow, environment-dependent, and potentially spammy.

children.title: Mockery and Service Container Substitution
children.description: Mockery creates mock objects with expectation assertions, and Laravel's $this->mock() helper binds the mock into the service container for the duration of the test. Without this, testing a class that depends on a third-party payment gateway requires a live connection to the external service, making tests slow, flaky, and impossible to run in offline environments.

children.title: Zero-Downtime Deploys with Forge and Envoyer
children.description: Laravel Envoyer (integrates with Forge) deploys to a new release directory and atomically symlinks it as current only after all hooks succeed — meaning users see the old version until the new one is fully ready. Without zero-downtime deployment, the window between file upload and artisan migrate exposes users to 500 errors as new code runs against the old schema.

children.title: Environment Variables and Secrets Management
children.description: Production environment variables should be set on the server (via Forge's environment editor) rather than committed in .env files to repositories — .env files in repos expose API keys and database passwords in git history permanently. Laravel's config caching (php artisan config:cache) requires all config values to come from environment variables, not from .env at runtime.

children.title: Running PHPUnit in CI with GitHub Actions
children.description: Integrating PHPUnit into a GitHub Actions workflow ensures that every pull request is tested against a fresh environment with the correct PHP version and a real test database before merging. Without CI, tests are only run when developers remember to run them locally, leading to broken builds on main and regression bugs that are expensive to diagnose.

children.title: PHP Static Analysis with PHPStan or Larastan
children.description: Larastan (PHPStan for Laravel) performs deep static analysis of your codebase — catching type mismatches, undefined properties, and incorrect method calls before they reach runtime. Running at level 5+ catches the majority of common bugs like calling methods on nullable models, and it integrates directly into CI to block merges on type errors.

remix-dev.title: TypeScript and React Foundations
remix-dev.description: This layer grounds you in TypeScript and React, the core of Remix development. These skills underpin every route and component you build.
remix-dev.challenge: Build a small typed React component set with a list, item, and form.

children.title: Type Inference and Annotation
children.description: TypeScript infers types from context but explicit annotations are required at function boundaries and complex data structures. Relying solely on inference at API edges causes silent any-type propagation, which defeats type safety and hides runtime bugs until production.

children.title: Generics and Utility Types
children.description: Generics allow type-safe reuse of logic across different data shapes. Utility types like Partial, Pick, and Omit are essential for typing Remix loader return values and form data without duplicating interface definitions. Ignoring them forces unsafe type casting.

children.title: Component Rendering and the Virtual DOM
children.description: React's reconciliation algorithm compares virtual DOM trees to minimize real DOM mutations. Misunderstanding this leads to unnecessary re-renders, broken key props, and stale closures in event handlers — all common sources of subtle UI bugs in larger applications.

resources.label: React Docs — Preserving and Resetting State

children.title: Hooks: useState, useEffect, useRef
children.description: Hooks encode React's rules about side effects and state co-location. Misusing useEffect for synchronous derived state, or forgetting dependency arrays, creates infinite loops and stale data — problems that Remix's loader pattern is specifically designed to eliminate in most cases.

children.title: Node.js Module System and ESM
children.description: Remix runs on Node.js and increasingly uses ES Modules. Mixing CommonJS require() and ESM import/export in the same project causes hard-to-debug runtime errors and breaks tree-shaking. Understanding module resolution is critical before adding server-side Remix code.

children.title: Async/Await and the Event Loop
children.description: Remix loaders and actions are async functions that run on the server. Blocking the event loop with synchronous I/O or mishandling Promise rejection causes the entire server to stall or crash. Proper async patterns are non-negotiable for reliable data fetching.

children.title: tsconfig.json and Strict Mode
children.description: Remix projects rely on tsconfig paths for route module resolution. Enabling strict mode catches null/undefined bugs at compile time that would otherwise surface as runtime 500 errors in production. Skipping strictNullChecks is the single largest source of TypeScript false confidence.

children.title: Vite and HMR in Remix
children.description: Remix uses Vite as its build tool, which provides fast Hot Module Replacement during development. Misunderstanding how Vite separates server and client bundles leads to importing server-only code into the browser, causing cryptic build errors or security leaks.

remix-dev.title: Remix Basics and Routing
remix-dev.description: This layer introduces Remix structure, nested routing, and the web fundamentals it embraces. Routing is the backbone of every Remix application.
remix-dev.challenge: Scaffold a contacts app with nested routes and a shared sidebar layout.

children.title: Route File Naming Conventions
children.description: Remix uses a file-based routing system where dots in filenames define URL segments and underscores create pathless layout routes. Misreading the naming convention results in routes that never match or layouts that wrap the wrong pages, causing broken navigation at scale.

children.title: Nested Routes and Outlet
children.description: Remix's nested routing maps URL hierarchy to component hierarchy through the Outlet component. Forgetting an Outlet causes child routes to silently fail to render, while incorrect nesting produces layouts that duplicate headers or footers unexpectedly across views.

children.title: Link vs NavLink and Prefetching
children.description: Remix's Link component uses client-side navigation and supports prefetch hints (intent, render, viewport) that preload route data on hover. Using plain anchor tags bypasses this system entirely, causing full page reloads and destroying Remix's performance model.

children.title: useNavigate and Programmatic Navigation
children.description: useNavigate provides imperative navigation for post-action redirects and conditional flows. Using it inside loaders or outside event handlers violates React's rendering contract and causes navigation to fire on every render, creating infinite redirect loops.

children.title: Dynamic Segments and useParams
children.description: Dynamic route segments (e.g. $userId) capture URL path values accessible in both loaders and components. Failing to validate these params before database queries is a security risk, as attackers can inject arbitrary values to probe your data layer.

children.title: useSearchParams for Query Strings
children.description: useSearchParams provides reactive access to URL query parameters and a setter that triggers navigation. Mutating search params via direct window.location manipulation bypasses Remix's state management, causing loaders to not re-run and leaving the UI stale.

children.title: root.tsx and App Shell
children.description: root.tsx is the top-level route that renders the HTML shell including Scripts, LiveReload, and ScrollRestoration components. Omitting the Scripts component breaks all client-side JavaScript hydration, making the app appear to work server-side but fail all interactions.

children.title: remix.config.js and vite.config.ts
children.description: Remix's configuration controls server adapter, future flags, and route directories. Enabling future flags one at a time is the correct migration path; toggling them all at once in an existing project frequently breaks route resolution and loader behavior simultaneously.

remix-dev.title: Loaders and Data Loading
remix-dev.description: This layer covers loading data on the server with loaders. Loaders are how Remix fetches data before rendering each route.
remix-dev.challenge: Load contacts from a data source using loaders with typed loader data.

children.title: The loader Function Contract
children.description: A Remix loader receives a LoaderFunctionArgs object and must return a Response or serializable data. Returning non-serializable values like class instances or circular references causes JSON serialization to fail silently, resulting in undefined data in useLoaderData on the client.

children.title: json() Helper and Response Objects
children.description: Remix's json() utility creates a Response with the correct Content-Type header and serializes data. Returning a raw object without json() works in development but breaks when loaders need to set custom headers or status codes, making error handling inconsistent across routes.

children.title: Parallel Loaders Across Nested Routes
children.description: Remix runs all matched route loaders in parallel, not sequentially. Designing parent routes to fetch shared data (like user session) avoids waterfalls where child routes wait for parent data. Misunderstanding this leads to re-fetching the same data in every child loader unnecessarily.

children.title: Deferred Data with defer() and Await
children.description: The defer() utility allows loaders to return a mix of resolved and streaming data, letting the page shell render immediately while slow data loads. Awaiting all promises before returning blocks the initial HTML response and degrades perceived performance on slow network connections.

children.title: Typing useLoaderData with TypeOf
children.description: Remix infers loader return types automatically when you use typeof loader in useLoaderData<typeof loader>(). Manually duplicating type definitions for loader data creates drift where the type and the actual data diverge silently after refactors, breaking UI rendering assumptions.

children.title: Loader Caching and HTTP Cache Headers
children.description: Remix loaders can set Cache-Control headers on their Response to enable CDN caching at the edge. Ignoring cache headers means every user request hits the origin server even for static data, which collapses under traffic and increases server costs unnecessarily.

children.title: Accessing request.url and URLSearchParams
children.description: Loaders receive the full Request object, giving access to URL, headers, and cookies. Parsing query parameters via regex or string splitting instead of URL/URLSearchParams mishandles edge cases like encoded characters and multi-value params, producing incorrect filter or sort behavior.

children.title: throw redirect() for Authorization Guards
children.description: Throwing a redirect() Response from a loader is the idiomatic Remix pattern for protecting routes. Using imperative navigation hooks inside loader logic or returning null to signal unauthorized access leads to components rendering with empty data instead of bouncing users to login.

remix-dev.title: Actions, Forms, and Mutations
remix-dev.description: This layer covers handling form submissions and mutations with actions. Remix uses standard forms and actions for progressive, resilient data changes.
remix-dev.challenge: Add create, edit, and delete contacts using actions with Zod validation.

children.title: The action Function and FormData
children.description: Remix actions receive the same LoaderFunctionArgs and parse mutations from request.formData(). Treating FormData like a plain object and using bracket notation without get() returns undefined for all fields, silently discarding submitted data and making forms appear broken.

children.title: useActionData for Validation Feedback
children.description: useActionData returns the data returned from the action after submission. Returning validation errors from the action and displaying them via useActionData is the Remix pattern for inline form errors. Using client-only state for this breaks the progressive enhancement model and loses errors on navigation.

children.title: Form vs form and JavaScript-Optional Design
children.description: Remix's Form component enhances native HTML forms with client-side submission while preserving server-side functionality without JavaScript. Relying on onClick handlers for form logic instead of action attributes makes the app completely non-functional when JavaScript fails to load.

children.title: useFetcher for Non-Navigation Mutations
children.description: useFetcher submits data to loaders and actions without triggering navigation, enabling patterns like inline edits and toggle buttons. Using fetch() directly for mutations bypasses Remix's revalidation system, leaving other route data stale until a full page reload.

children.title: useNavigation and Pending States
children.description: useNavigation exposes the current navigation state (idle, submitting, loading) for showing global pending indicators. Implementing custom loading state with useState instead misses navigation-triggered reloads and leaves users without feedback during slow server responses.

children.title: Optimistic Updates with useFetcher
children.description: useFetcher.formData contains the submitted form data before the server responds, enabling optimistic UI that reflects changes immediately. Skipping optimistic updates in high-interaction UIs (toggles, likes, checkboxes) creates laggy experiences that feel broken on normal latency connections.

children.title: HTTP Methods in Remix Actions
children.description: HTML forms only support GET and POST, so Remix actions conventionally use hidden intent fields to distinguish between create, update, and delete operations within a single action function. Routing all mutations through separate endpoints loses Remix's automatic revalidation and complicates server logic.

resources.label: Remix Docs — Multiple Forms in a Route

children.title: Post/Redirect/Get Pattern
children.description: Returning a redirect() from an action after a successful mutation prevents double-submission on browser refresh. Returning data directly from a successful action instead of redirecting means refreshing the page resubmits the form, duplicating database records or triggering idempotency violations.

remix-dev.title: Databases and Persistence
remix-dev.description: This layer covers persisting data through a database and ORM in Remix. Real storage turns the contacts demo into a durable application.
remix-dev.challenge: Persist contacts in PostgreSQL via Prisma inside your loaders and actions.

children.title: Prisma Schema and Data Modeling
children.description: The Prisma schema is the single source of truth for both the database structure and TypeScript types. Modifying the database directly without updating the schema causes type drift where Prisma's generated client no longer matches the actual tables, producing runtime query failures masked by type errors.

children.title: PrismaClient Singleton Pattern
children.description: In development, Vite's HMR creates new module instances on each hot reload, causing too many database connections if PrismaClient is instantiated at module level. The singleton pattern using a global variable prevents connection pool exhaustion during development.

resources.label: Prisma Docs — Best Practices for Instantiation

children.title: findUnique, findMany, and Select
children.description: Prisma's select option restricts which fields are returned from a query, reducing data transfer and preventing accidental exposure of sensitive fields like password hashes. Using findMany without select on large tables returns unbounded result sets that exhaust memory under load.

children.title: Transactions and Atomic Operations
children.description: Prisma's interactive and batch transactions ensure multiple database operations succeed or fail together. Performing related writes without a transaction (e.g., creating a user then their profile) risks partial failure states where the database is left in an inconsistent condition.

children.title: SQLite Limitations and Production Parity
children.description: SQLite lacks full support for ALTER TABLE operations and concurrent writes. Running SQLite in development while using PostgreSQL in production causes migrations that work locally to fail in CI because SQLite silently ignores certain column constraints that Postgres enforces strictly.

children.title: Prisma Migrate and Schema Drift
children.description: prisma migrate dev generates SQL migration files that version the schema changes. Editing the database directly without running migrations causes schema drift that breaks prisma migrate deploy in production, requiring manual reconciliation that risks data loss.

children.title: Repository Pattern with Remix Loaders
children.description: Separating database query logic into dedicated model/repository functions keeps loaders thin and testable. Embedding complex Prisma queries directly inside loader functions makes them impossible to unit test without a live database and couples routing logic to persistence concerns.

children.title: Seeding and Test Databases
children.description: Prisma's seed script (prisma/seed.ts) populates a fresh database with known data for development and testing. Running tests against a shared development database causes flaky tests because data mutated by one test affects others, making failures non-reproducible.

remix-dev.title: Authentication and Sessions
remix-dev.description: This layer adds login, sessions, and protected routes using Remix's session helpers. Authentication enables per-user data and secure access control.
remix-dev.challenge: Add login so each user can manage only their own contacts using sessions.

children.title: createCookieSessionStorage
children.description: Remix's createCookieSessionStorage stores session data in signed, encrypted cookies using a secret. Storing sensitive data in unsigned cookies or using a weak/static secret allows attackers to forge sessions and impersonate any user, representing a critical authentication vulnerability.

children.title: commitSession and destroySession
children.description: Session changes only persist when commitSession() is called and the resulting Set-Cookie header is returned in the Response. Forgetting to commit the session after setting flash messages or updating user data silently discards changes, creating confusion where login appears to succeed but state is lost.

children.title: Hashing Passwords with bcrypt
children.description: Passwords must be hashed with a slow, salted algorithm like bcrypt before storage. Storing plain text passwords or using fast hashing algorithms like MD5 means a single database breach exposes all user credentials immediately, with no time to force password resets.

children.title: Timing-Safe Comparisons
children.description: Standard string equality checks return early on the first mismatched character, leaking timing information that allows attackers to enumerate valid usernames. Using crypto.timingSafeEqual or bcrypt.compare (which is inherently timing-safe) prevents user enumeration attacks on login forms.

children.title: HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite Attributes
children.description: Session cookies must set HttpOnly to prevent JavaScript access, Secure to restrict transmission to HTTPS, and SameSite=Lax or Strict to prevent CSRF attacks. Missing any of these attributes exposes session tokens to XSS exfiltration or cross-site request forgery depending on which is omitted.

children.title: Flash Messages via Session
children.description: Remix sessions support flash values that are automatically deleted after being read once, ideal for one-time success or error messages after redirect. Storing flash data in URL query params instead exposes message content in browser history and server logs, and fails for messages that contain sensitive text.

children.title: remix-auth and Strategy Pattern
children.description: remix-auth provides a strategy-based authentication framework that handles OAuth flows, callback verification, and session persistence in a composable way. Rolling custom OAuth implementations without this abstraction routinely mishandles state parameters and PKCE verification, creating authorization code interception vulnerabilities.

children.title: CSRF Protection in Remix Actions
children.description: Remix's SameSite cookie attribute provides baseline CSRF protection, but custom token validation is still required for APIs that accept cross-origin requests. Relying only on SameSite without understanding its browser compatibility exceptions (especially with subdomain sharing) leaves form actions exploitable.

remix-dev.title: Error Handling and Resilience
remix-dev.description: This layer covers error boundaries, status handling, and resilient UX. Graceful error handling is a core strength of the Remix model.
remix-dev.challenge: Add route-level error boundaries and friendly not-found pages to the app.

children.title: ErrorBoundary in Route Modules
children.description: Each Remix route module can export an ErrorBoundary that catches errors thrown during rendering, loading, or actions for that route subtree only. Without route-level boundaries, any uncaught error destroys the entire page rather than just the failing section, severely impacting UX for unrelated parts of the app.

children.title: useRouteError and isRouteErrorResponse
